In an email dated October 17, 2024 at 19:47:12 UTC, Betsy Catron of WHT Law wrote to Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm regarding the Amy Mills v. Forcht Bank matter. The message, which was CC’d to Sarena Tuttle (two addresses: sarena@whaleylawfirm.com and [EMAIL-531]) and Gregg Thornton ([EMAIL-532]), asked whether there were any new records for the client and what the treatment status was. The subject line read Re: Amy Mills v Forcht Bank, and Betsy’s note to Aaron simply stated: “Hi Aaron, Checking in on whether you have any new records for your client and treatment status. Thanks, Betsy.”

Earlier, on September 16, 2024, Aaron Whaley replied at 11:18 AM, informing Betsy that he was working on it and that Amy Mills had a new MRI on September 11, but he did not yet have those records.

Also on September 16, 2024, at approximately 11:17 AM, Betsy sent another email stating that written discovery had been served on the Plaintiff on August 2, 2024, but they had not yet received any response. She noted that the discovery was past due and asked Aaron to indicate within the next week when he could expect the responses to avoid moving the Court to compel them. Betsy asked for any update on her client’s treatment and, if treatment occurred, to provide the records as soon as possible. The email concluded with her signature block:

Betsy R. Catron, Attorney
Vine Center
333 West Vine Street, Suite 1100
Lexington, KY 40507
Phone: [PHONE-533]
Fax: [PHONE-2449]
www.whtlaw.com

The message also included Aaron Whaley’s signature block, identifying him as affiliated with Whaley Law Firm at 712 Lyndon Ln, Louisville, KY 40222, with the phone number [PHONE-535]. An external email caution line appeared at the bottom of the message.

item_100.note: This note summarizes a sequence of email communications in the case Amy Mills v Forcht Bank, associated with the file Amy Mills Premise [DOB-2442] and the client Amy Mills. The exchanges occurred primarily in December 2024, with earlier context notes dating back to September through November 2024.

On December 12, 2024, Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm wrote to Gregg Thornton of Vine Center, copying Betsy Catron, Sarena Tuttle, and the Amy Mills Filevine project address. Whaley stated that no one was saying Thornton could not obtain records or copies of all of Amy Mills’s MRIs and other scans; those records could be reviewed to determine whether a tear is related to the incident. He added that whether Amy Mills undergoes surgery on January 3 would not affect that analysis. Whaley indicated that January 3 is in three weeks, and that shortly after that date he would be able to provide Thornton with the names of all new providers as well as an executed medical authorization. He also asserted that there is nothing the court will do that would be faster than that timeline. Whaley signed as with Whaley Law Firm, located at 712 Lyndon Ln, Louisville, KY 40222, with the phone number [PHONE-2441].

Later on December 12, 2024, Gregg Thornton replied, stating, “Absolutely not I would never do that. However my client is only responsible for what is causally related and since you said this is a new tear we need to look at it.” Thornton’s signature block followed, showing his firm Vine Center at 333 West Vine Street, Suite 1100, Lexington, KY 40507, with the phone number [PHONE-2440], fax [PHONE-544], and email [EMAIL-545].

Also on December 12, 2024, a separate message from Aaron Whaley appeared at 3:19 PM, prefaced with an external email warning. In that note, Whaley asked Thornton whether he was asserting the right to dictate when the client could receive medical care ordered by her treating physician, given that Amy Mills is in litigation and the surgeon had been instructed to act in accordance with her physician’s orders.

Earlier that same day, at 2:29 PM, Thornton wrote that he could not agree to the proposed arrangement. He explained that because Amy Mills is in litigation, they needed to evaluate the current films and records so that their expert could determine whether anything else was needed before the surgery to be able to opine on whether the surgery is related to the incident. He added that waiting until after the surgery in order to “hide” the litigation from the surgeon is not something they could agree to.

At 12:19 PM, Whaley had written a prior message to Betsy Catron (with Betsy Catron copied on the thread and to other recipients) indicating that he and Amy Mills could agree to provide all information and responses to Betsy Catron’s interrogatories and requests for production. This included disclosing who Amy Mills was treating with after January 3. Whaley noted that January 3 was the date of her upcoming shoulder surgery, and Amy Mills would prefer to wait until after that surgery to reveal the doctor’s identity. He explained that the doctor had stated he would not take a patient if the patient were in litigation, and Amy Mills did not want her surgery canceled.

This December 12 sequence followed earlier communications in the same case. On November 26, 2024, Betsy Catron had written asking for updated records and, specifically, who Amy Mills was currently treating with so those records could be requested. Aaron Whaley replied that he did not have the requested providers for physical therapy or the surgeon’s identity, but he did know that Amy Mills had completed physical therapy and was scheduled for shoulder surgery on January 3.

On November 25, 2024, Betsy Catron had requested updated records and the identity of Amy Mills’s current treating providers so she could request those records. On October 21, 2024, Betsy Catron stated that she did not have the providers but knew there had been a new MRI showing a new tear in Amy Mills’s shoulder labrum, located at the suture from her previous surgery, and that Amy Mills was currently in physical therapy again.

Betsy Catron had previously written on October 17, 2024, checking in for any new records and the status of Amy Mills’s treatment. On September 16, 2024, Betsy Catron sent two messages: at 11:18 AM she asked for an update, noting that Amy Mills had a new MRI on September 11 and that she did not yet have those records; at 11:17 AM she mentioned that written discovery on Amy Mills had been served on August 2, 2024, and, noting some garbled content, asked to be informed within the next week when responses could be expected to avoid court compulsion, and requested any available updates on treatment and records if treatment existed.

In sum, the December 12, 2024 communications built on a longer thread about the management of medical records, the timing and conditions of treatment and surgery (notably a shoulder surgery scheduled for January 3), and the curricular need to identify treating providers—while balancing the plaintiff’s litigation status and the desire to protect the treating physician(s) from appearing to tailor care around litigation. The parties repeatedly discussed whether records could be supplied now and how the identities of treating providers would be disclosed, with the surgeon reportedly not accepting patients who are in litigation and with Amy Mills’s counsel indicating a willingness to provide information after the January 3 surgery and to share the medical authorization and provider list shortly thereafter.

item_101.note: In the case Amy Mills v Forcht Bank (Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2436]; Client: Amy Mills), the following email communications occurred between September and December 2024 among Amy Mills’ counsel and opposing counsel. The participants include Gregg Thornton of WHT Law (Vine Center, Lexington, KY), Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm (Louisville, KY), and Betsy Catron plus Sarena Tuttle on distribution. The notes reference a Filevine project address for Amy Mills Premise [DOB-2435].

On September 16, 2024, Betsy Catron reported that written discovery had been served on the Plaintiff on August 2, 2024, but no responses had yet been received. She asked for a timeline to avoid a court motion to compel and requested any updates on the plaintiff’s treatment and accompanying records. Aaron Whaley replied that he did not have updates yet but was working on obtaining them; he noted that Amy had a new MRI on September 11 and that he did not yet have those records.

A follow-up from Betsy on October 17, 2024, inquired whether Aaron had any new records or information regarding Amy’s treatment status. Aaron responded on October 21, 2024 that he did not have new records to share at that time. He did indicate that Amy’s shoulder MRI showed a new tear of the labrum at the suture from her prior surgery and that she was back in physical therapy.

Toward the end of November 2024, Betsy asked for updated records and to identify Amy’s current treatment providers so she could request those records. On November 26, 2024, Aaron replied that he did not have new information on providers, but he did confirm that Amy had completed physical therapy and was scheduled for shoulder surgery on January 3. Betsy had previously requested information about who was treating Amy for physical therapy and who would perform the surgery.

On December 12, 2024, the parties exchanged further messages. Aaron Whaley told Betsy that they could provide all information and responses to interrogatories and requests for production, including who Amy would be treating with after January 3. He noted that January 3 is the date of Amy’s upcoming shoulder surgery and that Amy would like to wait until after that date to reveal the doctor’s identity, because the doctor had said he would not take a patient who is in litigation, and Amy did not want her surgery canceled.

Gregg Thornton responded later that day, stating, “Aaron we cannot agree to this. First she is in litigation. We need to evaluate the current films and records because our expert needs to tell us if they need anything else before the surgery is performed to be able to give an opinion if it is related to this incident. Waiting until after the surgery because you want to hide the fact that she is in litigation from the surgeon is not something we can agree to.”

In reply, Aaron Whaley asked, “Gregg, are you asserting that you have the right to dictate when my client can receive medical care that has been ordered by her treating physician?” Gregg Thornton later clarified, “Absolutely not I would never do that. However my client is only responsible for what is causally related and since you said this is a new tear we need to look at it.”

item_102.note: Case: Amy Mills Premise [DOB-2433]. Client: Amy Mills. This note summarizes an email thread connected to the case Amy Mills v. Forcht Bank, involving Amy Mills’s medical treatment status, discovery responses, and the scheduling of her shoulder surgery, as discussed by counsel in December 2024 and in prior months.

On September 16, 2024, Betsy R. Catron of WHT Law wrote to Aaron Whaley of the Whaley Law Firm noting that written discovery had been served on the Plaintiff on August 2, 2024 but had not yet been answered. She stated that because the discovery was past due, she wanted to know when she could expect the Plaintiff’s responses, to avoid seeking court intervention to compel. Betsy also requested an update on the Plaintiff’s treatment and asked for records as soon as available. Aaron Whaley replied that he was working on it and that the Plaintiff had a new MRI on September 11, but he did not yet have those records.

On October 17, 2024, Betsy Catron checked in again for an update on whether there were any new records. Aaron’s response on October 21, 2024 stated that he did not have updated records to provide yet but did know that Amy Mills had a new MRI on her shoulder indicating a new tear of the labrum at the suture from her previous surgery, and that she was currently in physical therapy again.

On October 21, 2024 Betsy followed up to inquire about who Amy Mills was currently treating with and what records could be obtained. Aaron replied that he could not provide those details at that moment but reiterated that Amy had had a new MRI on September 11 and that she was in physical therapy again.

On November 25, 2024, Betsy asked for any updated records and specifically requested to know who Amy Mills was currently treating so those providers’ records could be requested. Aaron responded on November 26, 2024 that he did not have updated records to provide at that time, but he did confirm that Amy had completed physical therapy and was scheduled for shoulder surgery on January 3. Betsy had previously asked for the names of Amy’s providers for physical therapy and the surgeon who would perform the surgery.

On November 26, 2024, Betsy had previously asked, “Who are her providers for PT and who is doing the surgery?” Aaron had replied that he did not have updated records to share yet but confirmed the PT status and the January 3 surgery date.

In addition to the above, a December 12, 2024 email thread began with a message from Aaron Whaley to Betsy Catron (and others) stating that he could agree to provide information and responses to interrogatories and requests for production, including who Amy Mills was treating with after January 3. He explained that January 3 was the date of her upcoming shoulder surgery and that Amy would prefer to wait until after the surgery to reveal the doctor’s identity, noting that the doctor had indicated he would not take her as a patient if she remained in litigation and that Amy did not want her surgery canceled.

That same day, December 12, 2024 at approximately 2:29 PM, Gregg Thornton of WHT Law replied: “Aaron we cannot agree to this. First she is in litigation. We need to evaluate the current films and records because our expert needs to tell us if they need anything else before the surgery is performed to be able to give an opinion if it is related to this incident. Waiting until after the surgery because you want to hide the fact that she is in litigation from the surgeon is not something we can agree to.” Thornton’s message was followed by his standard confidentiality and privilege notice with the WHT Law Firm contact information.

The December 12, 2024 exchange thus centers on two core issues: (1) whether Amy Mills can disclose the identity of her treating doctor and provide certain medical information before her January 3 shoulder surgery, given that she is in litigation; and (2) whether the defense team will review current imaging and records and defer any dependent actions until after the surgery to avoid exposing litigation status to the surgeon. The parties reiterate the tension between timely discovery and the implications of litigation status on medical treatment and record sharing, with Amy Mills’s upcoming January 3 shoulder surgery a focal point of the discussion.

item_103.note: Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2430]
Client: Amy Mills

Re: Amy Mills v Forcht Bank

On December 12, 2024, Gregg Thornton, a member of WHT Law, sent an email regarding the Amy Mills v. Forcht Bank matter to Aaron Whaley and Betsy Catron, with Sarena Tuttle and a Filevine project email address copied on the thread. In his message, Thornton stated that the opposing side could not agree to the proposed arrangement because Amy Mills is in litigation. He explained that the defense team needed to evaluate current films and records so their expert could determine whether anything additional was needed before Amy’s upcoming shoulder surgery in order to render an opinion about whether the surgery is related to the incident. Thornton cautioned that waiting until after the surgery to conceal the litigation from the surgeon was unacceptable. He signed as MEM BER at the Vine Center, Lexington, KY, and included standard confidentiality language warning that the email and any attachments are privileged and confidential and should be deleted if received in error.

Earlier that same day, Aaron Whaley replied to Betsy Catron and copied the same recipients. His external-email notice appeared at the top of the message. Whaley reported that he had spoken with Amy and that they could agree to provide Betsy Catron with all information and responses to interrogatories and requests for production, including who Amy is treating with after January 3 (the date of her upcoming shoulder surgery). He stated that Amy would like to wait to reveal the doctor’s identity until after the surgery because the doctor has indicated he will not take her as a patient if she is in litigation, and Amy does not want her surgery canceled.

This email thread sits within a broader discovery and treatment-status narrative dating back to September 2024. On September 16, 2024, Betsy Catron asked Aaron Whaley for an update, noting that written discovery had been served on August 2, 2024, and requesting the plaintiff’s records if there were any treatment updates. Whaley responded later that day that Amy had had a new MRI on September 11, which showed a new tear of her labrum at the suture from her previous surgery, and that she was in physical therapy (PT) again, but Whaley did not yet have the records.

On October 17, 2024, Betsy followed up again, asking if there were any new records for Amy Mills and for an update on her treatment status. Whaley replied on October 21, 2024, saying he did not have updated records and that Amy had received a new MRI showing a labral tear located at the sutures from the prior surgery, and that she was in PT again.

The thread continued with Betsy’s inquiry on November 25, 2024, asking for any updated records and specifically who Amy was currently treating with so those records could be requested. Whaley replied on November 26, 2024, that he did not have updated records; he did know that Amy had completed PT and was scheduled for shoulder surgery on January 3. Betsy followed up again on November 26, 2024, asking for the names of Amy’s PT providers and who would be performing the surgery.

In the same December 12, 2024 exchange, Aaron Whaley stated that he was willing to provide all information and responses to interrogatories and production requests, including the identity of Amy’s current treatment providers after January 3. He reiterated that January 3 marks the date of her upcoming shoulder surgery and that Amy preferred not to disclose the doctor’s identity before that date. He added that the doctor had indicated he would not take a patient who is in litigation, and Amy did not want her surgery canceled.

Taken together, the communications reflect a litigation-centered context for Amy Mills’s medical treatment records related to a shoulder injury and surgery, with contemporaneous concerns about the timing of disclosing treating providers and the potential impact of litigation status on medical care. The messages include multiple external-email warnings and standard confidentiality notices, and they document a sequence of MRI findings (notably a new labral tear at the prior suture site), ongoing physical therapy, a scheduled shoulder surgery on January 3, and the conditional release of treating-provider information after the stated surgery date.

item_105.note: This is a chronological narrative of the email correspondence in the case captioned Amy Mills v. Forcht Bank, involving client Amy Mills. The communications ran from September through November 2024 and were between Betsy R. Catron, an attorney with WHT Law in Lexington, Kentucky, and Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm in Louisville, with Sarena Tuttle and Gregg Thornton copied on several messages.

On September 16, 2024, Aaron Whaley replied to Betsy Catron that he did not have updated records to provide at that moment but noted that Amy Mills had a new MRI on September 11. He stated that he was working on obtaining those records. In Betsy Catron’s reply sent shortly afterward, she reminded Aaron that written discovery had been served on the plaintiff on August 2, 2024 and had not yet produced responses, and she asked for an update on Amy’s treatment and for the records ASAP to avoid court action to compel discovery.

Later on September 16, 2024, Betsy followed up again to request any available updates on Amy’s treatment and to obtain records if treatment existed. Aaron responded that he did not have the records yet but reiterated that Amy had a new MRI on September 11 and was in the process of obtaining them.

On October 17, 2024, Betsy sent another inquiry asking whether there were any new records for the client and what her treatment status was. Aaron’s subsequent message on October 21, 2024, indicated that he did not have updated records but did provide a medical update: Amy had a new MRI on her shoulder showing a new tear of her labrum, located at the suture from her previous surgery, and she was currently in physical therapy again.

Moving forward to late October and November 2024, Betsy continued to seek updated records and current treatment information. On November 25, 2024, Betsy asked whether there were updated records she could obtain and requested confirmation of who Amy Mills was currently treating with so that she could request those records directly. In the following exchange on November 26, 2024, Betsy asked, “Who are her providers for PT and who is doing the surgery?”

Aaron Whaley replied that he did not know the PT providers or the surgeon, but he did know that Amy had completed physical therapy and was scheduled for shoulder surgery on January 3 (the year is not specified in the notes). The thread shows the ongoing attempt to obtain current medical records and verification of treating providers as part of the case management process.

Throughout these exchanges, the email chain included numerous copies to Sarena Tuttle (two addresses) and Gregg Thornton, reflecting the involved legal teams at Whaley Law Firm and WHT Law. Betsy Catron’s messages also carried her attorney signature and contact information, indicating her affiliation with Vine Center in Lexington, Kentucky.

In summary, between September and November 2024, the office of Betsy Catron repeatedly asked for updated medical records and confirmation of Amy Mills’s current treatment status. Aaron Whaley repeatedly reported that new imaging existed—specifically a September 11 MRI showing a labral tear at the prior surgical suture—and that Amy was in physical therapy again, but he did not provide the PT providers or the surgeon involved. By late November, the latest status indicated that Amy had completed physical therapy and was scheduled for shoulder surgery on January 3, with no disclosed information on the treating PT providers or the surgeon.

item_106.note: This file covers the Amy Mills v. Forcht Bank case, with client Amy Mills, and tracks a sequence of internal emails among the attorneys handling the matter.

In late November 2024, Betsy R. Catron, an attorney at WHT Law (Vine Center, 333 West Vine Street, Suite 1100, Lexington, KY 40507), sent an email to Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm (and copied colleagues Sarena Tuttle and Gregg Thornton) asking for any updated records related to Amy Mills and, specifically, who Amy is currently treating with so Betsy could request those treatment records. The message, dated Monday, November 25, 2024 at 16:03:58 UTC, included the external‑email caution note about attachments and links from external senders.

This inquiry followed an earlier thread of communications. On October 21, 2024, Aaron Whaley replied that he did not have updated records in hand but did note that Amy Mills had undergone a new MRI of her shoulder, which revealed a new tear of the labrum at the suture from her prior shoulder surgery, and that she was currently in physical therapy again.

Before that, on September 16, 2024, Betsy had written that written discovery had been served on the Plaintiff on August 2, 2024, but no response had been received. She warned that discovery was past due and asked Aaron to indicate within the next week when he could expect the plaintiff’s responses so as to avoid a court compel order. Betsy also requested any update on Amy’s treatment and, if treatment existed, the corresponding records as soon as possible. The email from Betsy on September 16 included an explicit request for updates on treatment and records and again carried the external‑email caution note.

Aaron’s reply, also dated September 16, 2024, stated that he was working on obtaining the information and that Amy had a new MRI on September 11, but he did not yet have those records in his possession.

A related message from Betsy, sent around the same day, reaffirmed the request for treatment updates and for records ASAP, and emphasized the need to address the discovery timeline to avoid court intervention.

In summary, the correspondence shows a focused effort across September through November 2024 to obtain updated medical records for Amy Mills and to ascertain her current treating provider, in light of a new MRI finding on September 11 and subsequent treatment activity, with multiple reminders about discovery responses and ongoing medical treatment.

item_107.note: Case: Amy Mills v. Forcht Bank (file: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-564]) involves client Amy Mills. The following notes summarize a sequence of counsel communications in late 2024 regarding medical records and treatment status for Amy Mills. The exchanges were between Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm (Louisville, KY) and Betsy R. Catron of Vine Center ( Lexington, KY), with multiple colleagues copied on the emails. The subject line across the thread was Re: Amy Mills v Forcht Bank.

On September 16, 2024, Betsy Catron, attorney at Vine Center, Lexington, KY, emailed Aaron Whaley noting that written discovery had been served on the Plaintiff on August 2, 2024 but no responses had yet been received. She asked for an update on her client’s treatment and requested that records be provided as soon as possible, and she provided her firm’s contact details. In the same exchange, Aaron replied that he did not have updated records at that moment but did report that Amy had a new MRI on September 11 showing a new tear of her labrum at the suture from her previous surgery, and that she was currently in physical therapy again.

Earlier in the thread, Betsy had also communicated that the discovery had been served on August 2, 2024 and emphasized the need for responses to avoid court intervention; she requested updates on treatment and any records as soon as possible, attaching the signature block of her firm (Vine Center, 333 West Vine Street, Suite 1100, Lexington, KY 40507; P [PHONE-565]; F [PHONE-566]; www.whtlaw.com).

On October 17, 2024, Betsy again checked in, asking whether there were any new records for the client and an update on her treatment status.

On October 21, 2024, Aaron replied that he did not have new records but noted that Amy had a new MRI on September 11 showing a labral tear at the suture from her prior surgery and that she was currently in physical therapy again.

On November 25, 2024, Betsy asked for updated records and specifically requested to be told who Amy Mills is currently treating with so Betsy could request those records directly.

The following day, November 26, 2024, Aaron responded: unfortunately, he did not have updated records. He did, however, state that Amy has completed physical therapy and is scheduled for shoulder surgery on January 3.

For reference, the emails included the following participants and contact details: Betsy R. Catron, Attorney, Vine Center, Lexington, KY 40507, P [PHONE-567], F [PHONE-568], www.whtlaw.com; Aaron Whaley, Whaley Law Firm, 712 Lyndon Ln, Louisville, KY 40222, [PHONE-2414]; CC’d recipients included Sarena Tuttle (sarena@whaleylawfirm.com; [EMAIL-570]) and Gregg Thornton ([EMAIL-571]).

item_108.note: Case: Amy Mills v. Forcht Bank, file noted as Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-573], with Client: Amy Mills.

In a December 12, 2024 email chain regarding the Amy Mills v. Forcht Bank matter, Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm asked Gregg Thornton of WHT Law whether Thornton was asserting a right to dictate when Amy Mills can receive medical care ordered by her treating physician. The message opened with the question: “Gregg, are you asserting that you have the right to dictate when my client can receive medical care that has been ordered by her treating physician?”

Thornton replied later that day, stating that his firm could not agree to that position. He explained that Amy Mills is in litigation and that they need to evaluate the current films and records so their expert can determine whether anything else is needed before the surgery to address the incident can be opined as related. He added that waiting until after the surgery to hide the fact that she is in litigation from the surgeon is not something they could agree to.

Also on December 12, 2024, Aaron Whaley sent a separate message to Betsy Catron (with various others copied) indicating a willingness to provide complete responses to interrogatories and production requests. He stated that these would include information about who Amy Mills is treating with at that moment after January 3, which he identified as the date of her upcoming shoulder surgery. He noted that Amy would like to wait until after the surgery to reveal the identity of the treating doctor, explaining that the physician has said he will not take her as a patient if she is in litigation, and Amy does not want her surgery canceled.

The December 12 exchange followed a prior thread beginning in late November 2024. On November 26, Betsy Catron had asked who Amy Mills’ providers for physical therapy were and who would perform the shoulder surgery. Aaron Whaley responded that he could not provide the names of those providers at that time, but did confirm that Amy had completed physical therapy and was scheduled for shoulder surgery on January 3. Earlier in the month, on November 25, Betsy Catron had asked for any updated records and requested the identity of Amy’s current treatment providers so she could request those records. Whaley’s reply on that date did not provide the requested provider names but acknowledged the status of therapy and surgery plans.

This sequence followed prior communications dating back to October 2024. On October 21, 2024, Aaron Whaley indicated that he did not have new records to provide but knew that Amy had completed a new MRI on September 11 showing a new tear of her labrum located at the suture from her previous surgery, and that she was currently in physical therapy again. Betsy Catron had inquired on October 17, 2024 about any new records, and Aaron’s response highlighted the absence of new records at that time, while confirming the September 11 MRI and ongoing physical therapy.

Earlier in September 2024, Betsy Catron had noted in her September 16, 2024 correspondence that the plaintiff’s discovery had been served on August 2, 2024, but the defendants had not yet received responses. Aaron Whaley replied that Amy had a new MRI on September 11, and he did not yet have those records, while Betsy urged prompt provision of updated treatment information and records.

In summary, the communications reflect ongoing litigation in the Amy Mills v. Forcht Bank matter, with the defense insisting on evaluating current medical films and records before surgery to determine relatedness to the incident, and the plaintiff’s counsel seeking to delay disclosing the treating physician’s identity until after the January 3, 2025 shoulder surgery to avoid jeopardizing treatment. The record also documents Amy Mills’ ongoing treatment status: completed physical therapy, a planned shoulder surgery on January 3, 2025, and a September 11 MRI revealing a new labral tear at the suture from her prior surgery. The parties repeatedly discussed production of medical records and identification of treating providers, against the backdrop of litigation considerations and the surgeon’s willingness to treat patients who are in litigation.

item_109.note: Case: Amy Mills v Forcht Bank, Premise file [DOB-2408]. Client: Amy Mills. This narrative reflects the email correspondence regarding discovery and medical records in the Amy Mills v Forcht Bank matter, focusing on communications dated December 12, 2024, and the earlier interim updates between September and November 2024.

On December 12, 2024, Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm sent an email to Gregg Thornton of Vine Center with Betsy Catron, Sarena Tuttle, and the Amy Mills premise email address copied. In his message, Aaron stated that no one was prohibiting the defense from obtaining the records or copies of all of Amy Mills’s MRIs and other scans, and that those records could be reviewed to determine whether the tear is causally related to the incident. He added that whether Amy Mills undergoes shoulder surgery on January 3 would not affect the ability to review those records. He noted that January 3 was three weeks away and that shortly after that date he would provide Thornton with the names of all new providers as well as an executed medical authorization. He closed by saying that there is nothing the court would do that would move faster than that. Aaron’s contact information shared in this message included Whaley Law Firm, 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222, telephone [PHONE-2407], and [EMAIL-577]. The message also referenced that the email was part of the Amy Mills v Forcht Bank case and included the Amy Mills Filevine project email address in the CC line.

Later on December 12, 2024, Gregg Thornton replied (the message timestamp showing 3:37 PM). He asserted that he would never claim the right to restrict records, but emphasized that his client is only responsible for what is causally related. Since Aaron had indicated this was a new tear, Thornton said they needed to look at it. Thornton provided his contact details: Vine Center, 333 West Vine Street, Suite 1100, Lexington, KY 40507; phone [PHONE-578]; fax [PHONE-579]; email [EMAIL-580]; and site www.whtlaw.com. He also included a standard confidentiality disclosure found in professional emails and noted the message originated from an iPhone.

Earlier on the same day, Aaron had sent a preliminary note (timestamp around 3:19 PM) to Betsy Catron with a warning that the message was external email. In that exchange, Aaron asked whether Gregg was claiming the right to dictate when Amy Mills could receive medical care ordered by her treating physician. The thread then includes Gregg’s reply (2:29 PM) stating that they cannot agree to waiting; they need to evaluate the current films and records because their expert needs to determine whether additional records are required before the surgery to assess whether the treatment is related to the incident; Aaron’s attempt to delay disclosure until after surgery to “hide the fact that she is in litigation from the surgeon” was not acceptable to them.

The thread then traces earlier communications in late 2024. In Aaron Whaley’s December 12, 2024 email to Betsy Catron, with a copy to Sarena Tuttle, Gregg Thornton, and the Amy Mills Filevine address, Aaron indicated that they could provide Betsy with all responses to interrogatories and requests for production, including information about who Amy Mills was treating with after January 3. He noted that January 3 was the date of her shoulder surgery and that she wished to wait until after the surgery to disclose the doctor’s identity because the doctor had stated he would not treat a patient who is in litigation, and Amy did not want her surgery canceled.

Previously, Betsy Catron had asked, on November 26, 2024, who Amy Mills’s PT providers were and who would perform the surgery. Aaron responded that he did not have that information at that time but noted that Amy had completed physical therapy and was scheduled for shoulder surgery on January 3. On November 25, 2024, Betsy had requested updated records and to know who Amy Mills was currently treating with so those records could be requested. This line of inquiry sits within ongoing discovery in the case.

The back-and-forth also reveals historical updates from September through October 2024. On September 16, 2024, Betsy Catron wrote asking for an update on Amy Mills’s treatment and records, and Aaron replied that he was working on it and that Amy had a new MRI on September 11, but he did not yet have those records. Betsy followed up on September 16 about the status. On October 17, 2024, Betsy again asked whether there were new records for Amy Mills and about treatment status. Aaron responded on October 21, 2024, stating that he did not have new records but did note that Amy had a new MRI showing a new tear of her labrum, located at the suture from her previous surgery, and that she was currently in physical therapy again. A later message on October 21 referenced that Amy Mills had completed physical therapy and was again in PT. On November 26, 2024, Aaron confirmed that Amy had completed physical therapy and was scheduled for shoulder surgery on January 3, while noting that he did not have updated records at that moment.

In summary, the December 12, 2024 correspondence reflects a dispute over the timing and scope of medical-record disclosures in the Amy Mills v Forcht Bank case. Aaron Whaley indicated that records, including MRIs, could be reviewed to determine causation and that new provider information and an executed medical authorization would be provided after Amy Mills’s scheduled January 3 shoulder surgery. Gregg Thornton disputed any arrangement that would delay evaluation of current films and records and insisted that records must be reviewed to determine whether the tear is related to the incident, arguing against hiding information from the surgeon. The thread also documents a pattern of prior updates from September through November 2024, including MRI findings showing new labral tears near prior surgical sites, ongoing physical therapy, and the evolving intent to disclose physicians’ identities and treatment providers after the January 3 surgery. The CCs and addresses included in the exchanges identify the involved parties and their contact details, including Betsy Catron, Sarena Tuttle, and Amy Mills Premise file email address, along with the law firm and medical-provider contact information.

item_110.note: Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2401]. Client: Amy Mills. Re: Amy Mills v Forcht Bank. This note documents a chain of communications regarding discovery and treatment records in the Amy Mills v Forcht Bank matter.

On December 12, 2024, at 12:19:06 GMT-05:00, Betsy Catron of Vine Center in Lexington, KY received an email from Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm concerning Amy Mills. In that message, Aaron stated that after speaking with Amy, they could provide Betsy with all the information and responses to her interrogatories and requests for production. This included identifying who Amy is treating with after January 3. January 3 was noted as the date of Amy’s upcoming shoulder surgery, and Amy wished to wait to reveal the doctor’s identity until after the surgery. Aaron explained that the doctor had said he would not take Amy as a patient if she were in litigation, and Amy did not want the surgery canceled.

This December 12 email followed a prior thread, beginning with Betsy’s question on November 26, 2024 at 2:11? PM asking who Amy’s providers for physical therapy were and who would perform the surgery. In Aaron Whaley’s reply, sent that same day at 8:48 AM, he explained that he did not know those providers. He did, however, confirm that Amy had completed physical therapy and that she was scheduled for shoulder surgery on January 3.

Earlier in the chain, on November 25, 2024 at 11:06? AM, Betsy had written requesting updated records and asking for the names of Amy’s current treating providers so those records could be requested.

Prior to that, on October 21, 2024 at 11:09 AM, Aaron wrote that he did not know the PT providers but noted that Amy had a new MRI of her shoulder showing a new labral tear at the suture from her previous surgery and that she was currently in physical therapy again.

On October 17, 2024, Betsy had asked Aaron to check for any new records for Amy and to update her on treatment status.

On September 16, 2024, Betsy inquired about treatment and records, and Aaron responded at 11:18 AM that he was working on it and that Amy had a new MRI on September 11, with the records not yet in hand. Earlier that same day, Betsy had noted that written discovery had been served on August 2, 2024, but no response had been received; she cautioned that the discovery was past due and requested within a week an expectation for responses, along with an update on Amy’s treatment and any available records.

Throughout these communications, the email chain included various recipients and CCs, such as Sarena Tuttle and Gregg Thornton, and a Filevine project email address associated with Amy Mills Premise [DOB-2400], as reflected in the CC line of the December 12, 2024 message. The correspondence circulated among counsel concerning the handling of interrogatories, production requests, treatment records, and the forthcoming disclosure of Amy’s treating physician, to be disclosed after her January 3 shoulder surgery, due to litigation considerations.

item_117.note: The record is labeled Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-612], with the client named Amy Mills. It concerns the Knox County case Mills, Amy v. Forcht Bank, docketed as 20-CI-00112, with envelope number 9370967.

On November 27, 2024, at 1:04 PM Eastern, the Kentucky Court of Justice issued a Notice of Electronic Filing for the Knox Circuit Court case Mills, Amy v. Forcht Bank (Case No. 20-CI-00112; Envelope 9370967). The notice originated from [EMAIL-2370] and was electronically mailed to counsel Aaron Gregory Whaley ([EMAIL-2369]), Gregg E. Thornton ([EMAIL-2368]), Betsy R. Catron ([EMAIL-616]), and Joshua Ellis ([EMAIL-617]). The eFiling was prepared by Betsy R. Catron, attorney for the defendant.

The following document(s) were included in this eFiling: a Motion to Compel; Exhibit 1; Exhibit 2; and a Tendered Document described as “Proposed / Tendered Order.” The notice notes that these documents can be viewed at https://kcoj.kycourts.net/eFilingRetrieval/Home/Package?id=FDB93160-0525-448A-B4D6-E333933B19B1.

This message was automatically generated by the Kentucky Court of Justice eFiling system. Do not reply to this email.

Confidentiality note: The Kentucky Court of Justice email states that this message and/or any attachment is intended only for the addressee and may contain information that is privileged, confidential, and/or proprietary work product. If you are not the intended recipient, or an authorized employee, agent, or representative of the intended recipient, you must not read, copy, retain, or disseminate the message or any attachment. Do not forward the message or attachment without the express written consent of the sender. If you have received this message in error, contact the sender immediately and delete all copies of the message and any attachment. Transmission or misdelivery shall not constitute waiver of any applicable legal privilege.

item_118.note: This note concerns the case labeled Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-619], with the client being Amy Mills. The matter is Amy Mills v. Forcht Bank in Knox County Circuit Court, case number 20-CI-00112. On December 2, 2024 at 11:39:29 AM (GMT-05:00), an email from Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm was forwarded to Sarena Tuttle and to the recipient listed as Amy Mills Premise [DOB-620] ([EMAIL-621]), forwarding the prior communications related to the case.

The forwarded message includes a prior email from November 27, 2024 at about 1:08 PM from Elizabeth Romersa, with the subject “Amy Mills vs. Forcht Bank - Knox County Circuit Court 20-CI-00112,” sent to [EMAIL-622] and cc’d to Betsy Catron, Gregg Thornton ([EMAIL-623]), and Elizabeth Romersa. In the body, Romersa writes, “Counsel, Attached please find the recently filed Motion to Compel and exhibits along with the Proposed Order regarding the above-referenced matter. Should you have any questions, please do not hesitate to contact our office directly.” The message ends with “Thanks, Elizabeth Romersa.” Her title is Paralegal to Walter Ward, William Partin, Betsy Catron, James Freel, Whitney Williams, and Jeanette Jayne. The signature block also lists Vine Center, 333 West Vine Street, Suite 1100, Lexington, KY 40507, with phone number [PHONE-624] and fax number [PHONE-625], and the firm website www.whtlaw.com. A confidentiality notice accompanies the email stating that the information is privileged and confidential, and instructions are given if the recipient is not the intended recipient or if it was received in error, including deletion and notification to correct records.

The closing portion shows the sender as Aaron Whaley of the Whaley Law Firm, located at 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222, with contact phone [PHONE-626].

item_119.note: In the matter of Amy Mills v. Forcht Bank, Knox County Circuit Court, case number 20-CI-00112, the file is titled Amy Mills Premise [DOB-2355] and lists Amy Mills as the client. The communication thread begins with a November 27, 2024 email from Elizabeth Romersa, a paralegal at Vine Center in Lexington, KY, to Aaron Whaley at Whaley Law Firm ([EMAIL-629]) with Betsy Catron, Gregg Thornton, and Elizabeth Romersa copied. The subject line was Amy Mills v. Forcht Bank – Knox County Circuit Court 20-CI-00112. In that message, Elizabeth Romersa stated that attached please find the recently filed Motion to Compel and exhibits along with the Proposed Order regarding the above-referenced matter, and she invited any questions to contact their office directly. The message included the standard confidentiality notice: information contained in the email transmission is privileged and confidential, and if you are not the intended recipient you should not read, distribute, or reproduce it; if received in error, delete it and notify the sender. The Vine Center contact details listed were 333 West Vine Street, Suite 1100, Lexington, KY 40507, with P [PHONE-630], F [PHONE-631], and the website www.whtlaw.com. The email was signed as from Elizabeth Romersa, Paralegal to Walter Ward, William Partin, Betsy Catron, James Freel, Whitney Williams, and Jeanette Jayne, affording the firm’s address and standard notice.

In the chain of messages that followed, the forwarded message from Elizabeth Romersa was addressed to [EMAIL-632], with Betsy Catron, Gregg Thornton ([EMAIL-633]), and Elizabeth Romersa copied. Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm, 712 Lyndon Ln, Louisville, KY 40222, was advised that the Motion to Compel and exhibits, along with the Proposed Order, had been filed and attached, and that any questions should be directed to their office. The communications also carried the Whaley Law Firm’s standard contact information and the attorney’s disclosures.

On December 2, 2024, at approximately 11:39 AM, Aaron Whaley replied to Sarena Tuttle’s message, and the body of his reply began with a forwarded message block referencing Elizabeth Romersa’s November 27 email containing the attached Motion to Compel, exhibits, and the Proposed Order. This reply included the same confidential notice and contact details as in the forwarded message, and it reiterated the ongoing handling of the matter.

Shortly thereafter, at around 11:45 AM, Sarena M. Tuttle, a Litigation Paralegal & Workers’ Compensation Case Manager at The Whaley Law Firm, replied with: “Already saved to File :) Has she disclosed her new providers yet?” Sarena’s signature identified her role and contact information: The Whaley Law Firm, 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222; Direct line [PHONE-634]; Office line [PHONE-635] ext 104; Email Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com, and a note that this is a new email address.

At approximately 11:47:21 AM on December 2, 2024 (GMT-05:00), Aaron Whaley responded, “nope. I have reached out to her. Ill let you know.” This line followed the initial content of the thread and confirmed that Aaron had attempted contact with Amy Mills and would provide an update.

Throughout these exchanges, the participants and recipients included the case caption referencing Amy Mills v. Forcht Bank (Knox County Circuit Court 20-CI-00112), the file-note reference Amy Mills Premise [DOB-636], and the involvement of The Whaley Law Firm and Vine Center personnel. The communications included the Motion to Compel and exhibits along with the Proposed Order, the attached confidentiality notices, and the updated contact details for Sarena M. Tuttle (new email address) and the Whaley Law Firm’s main address and phone numbers.

item_120.note: Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-638]. Client: Amy Mills. This matter is Amy Mills v. Forcht Bank in Knox County Circuit Court, docket 20-CI-00112. The note records a recent email thread involving the Whaley Law Firm and related parties.

On December 2, 2024, at 11:45:38 GMT-05:00, Sarena M. Tuttle, Litigation Paralegal and Workers' Compensation Case Manager at The Whaley Law Firm, sent an email regarding the case to Aaron Whaley, with a cc to the file labeled Amy Mills Premise [DOB-2344] ([EMAIL-640]). The email question stated, “Already saved to File :) Has she disclosed her new providers yet?” The message included Sarena’s signature, which lists her title, the firm’s name and address (The Whaley Law Firm, 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222), and contact details: D: [PHONE-2342], O: [PHONE-642] ext 104, E: Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com, with a note to “PLEASE NOTE NEW EMAIL ADDRESS.”

Earlier that day, at 11:39? AM, Aaron Whaley replied with a forwarded message from Elizabeth Romersa. The forwarded email, dated Wednesday, November 27, 2024 at 1:08? PM, was titled “Amy Mills vs. Forcht Bank - Knox County Circuit Court 20-CI-00112” and was sent to [EMAIL-643], with cc’s to Betsy Catron, Gregg Thornton ([EMAIL-644]), and Elizabeth Romersa. The body of that message informed counsel that the attached materials included the recently filed Motion to Compel and exhibits, along with the Proposed Order related to the referenced matter, and invited any questions with direct contact to their office.

Elizabeth Romersa’s email signature identifies her as a paralegal to Walter Ward, William Partin, Betsy Catron, James Freel, Whitney Williams, and Jeanette Jayne. Her signature also lists Vine Center, 333 West Vine Street, Suite 1100, Lexington, KY 40507; P: [PHONE-2338]; F: [PHONE-2337]; and the firm’s website at www.whtlaw.com. The message includes the standard confidentiality and privilege notice, advising that if the recipient is not the intended recipient, they should not read or forward the email or attachments and should delete the message and notify the sender if received in error.

The alternate signature block for the recipient, Aaron Whaley, follows: “Aaron Whaley, Whaley Law Firm, 712 Lyndon Ln, Louisville, KY 40222, [PHONE-647].”

item_121.note: On November 27, 2024, the Knox Circuit Court Clerk processed and accepted a filing in the matter of Mills, Amy v. Forcht Bank, Knox Circuit Court, Case No. 20-CI-00112. The filing, associated with envelope number 9370967, was submitted via eFiling by Betsy R. Catron, attorney for the defendant. The notice confirms the case caption as “MILLS, AMY VS. FORCHT BANK” and notes the envelope number 9370967 and the case number 20-CI-00112.

The electronic notice was sent to the following recipients: Aaron Gregory Whaley ([EMAIL-649]), Gregg E. Thornton ([EMAIL-650]), Betsy R. Catron ([EMAIL-651]), and Joshua Ellis ([EMAIL-652]). The header also shows To: [EMAIL-653], Sarena@whaleylawfirm.com, [EMAIL-654], with no CC recipients listed.

A scheduled event, titled MOTION HOUR, is set for December 16, 2024 at 9:00 AM Eastern Time in the Circuit Courtroom, before Hon. Michael Caperton.

The eFiling includes the following documents: MOTION TO COMPEL; EXHIBIT; EXHIBIT; TENDERED DOCUMENT. The materials can be viewed at the Kentucky Court of Justice eFiling retrieval link: https://kcoj.kycourts.net/eFilingRetrieval/Home/Package?id=FDB93160-0525-448A-B4D6-E333933B19B1.

This notification was automatically generated by the Kentucky Court of Justice eFiling system. Do not reply to this email.

Kentucky Court of Justice Confidentiality Notice
This message and/or attachment is intended only for the addressee and may contain information that is privileged, confidential and/or proprietary work product. If you are not the intended recipient, or an authorized employee, agent or representative of the intended recipient, do not read, copy, retain or disseminate this message or any attachment. Do not forward this message and attachment without the express written consent of the sender. If you have received this message in error, please contact the sender immediately and delete all copies of the message and any attachment. Transmission or misdelivery shall not constitute waiver of any applicable legal privilege.

item_130.note: Case: Amy Mills v. Forcht Bank. The client is Amy Mills, in the matter captioned Amy Mills v. Forcht Bank, with the case file labeled Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2293].

On September 16, 2024, Betsy R. Catron, an attorney at Vine Center in Lexington, KY, wrote regarding the case. She noted that written discovery had been served on the Plaintiff on August 2, 2024, but no response had been received, and because the discovery was past due, she asked Aaron Whaley of the Whaley Law Firm to advise when the responses could be expected so as to avoid petitioning the Court to compel. She also requested an update on Amy Mills’s treatment and asked that any treatment records be provided as soon as possible.

Earlier that same day, at 11:18 a.m., Aaron Whaley replied that he was working on obtaining records and that Amy Mills had a new MRI on September 11. He stated that he did not yet have those records.

Earlier still, at 11:17 a.m., Betsy Catron had written that written discovery had been served on August 2, 2024, but no responses had been received, and she reiterated the need for a timely update to avoid court intervention. She requested information on her client’s treatment and, if treatment had taken place, asked for the records as soon as possible.

On October 17, 2024, Betsy followed up again, asking whether there were any new records for Amy Mills and for an update on her treatment status.

Finally, on October 21, 2024 at 11:08:38 a.m. (Eastern Time), Aaron Whaley sent a further update. He stated that he did not have new records at that moment, but that Amy Mills had recently undergone a new MRI of her shoulder, which showed a new tear of the labrum located at the suture from her previous surgery. He added that she is currently in physical therapy again. The message was sent to Betsy Catron, with CCs to Sarena Tuttle (sarena@whaleylawfirm.com and [EMAIL-691]) and Gregg Thornton ([EMAIL-692]).

Throughout the exchange, the parties’ communications circulated among the Whaley Law Firm and the WHT Law Firm, with the subject line Re: Amy Mills v. Forcht Bank, and the email header identifying Aaron Whaley as the sender and Betsy Catron as the recipient, along with the CC list. The correspondence includes standard firm signatures and disclaimers typical of external email, noting the offices and contact details for Betsy Catron and the Whaley Law Firm.

Teresa looked it up, and in her records it is noted that Amy did as well, and that is why she prescribed maxsalt for the migraines, because they had tried all kinds of things and it didn’t help. She also said that with Amy’s failing spinal fusion above and below 3&4, it could make them worse, but she did not think that was the main cause of them.

item_150.note: In the matter of Amy Mills v. Forcht Bank (Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-775]), the client is Amy Mills. On September 16, 2024, Betsy R. Catron, an attorney with WHT Law at the Vine Center, 333 West Vine Street, Suite 1100, Lexington, KY 40507, sent an email from [EMAIL-2207]. The email was addressed to Aaron Whaley at [EMAIL-2206] and to Sarena Tuttle at sarena@whaleylawfirm.com and [EMAIL-778], with Gregg Thornton cc’d at [EMAIL-779]. The message was sent on Monday, September 16, 2024, at 15:17:13 +0000.

In the body of the email, Catron notes that written discovery was served on the Plaintiff on August 2, 2024, but no response has been received. Because the discovery is past due, she asks to be informed within the next week when she can expect to receive the responses, so as to avoid moving the Court to compel them. She also asks for any update on her client’s treatment; if there is treatment, she requests that the records be provided as soon as possible. The email closes with thanks from Betsy R. Catron, Attorney, followed by her contact information: Vine Center, 333 West Vine Street, Suite 1100, Lexington, KY 40507; phone [PHONE-780]; fax [PHONE-781]; website www.whtlaw.com.

item_151.note: In the case of Amy Mills v. Forcht Bank (case file: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2200]), the client is Amy Mills. On September 16, 2024, at 11:18:22 a.m. Eastern Time, Aaron Whaley of the Whaley Law Firm sent an email to Betsy Catron regarding the matter. In that message, he wrote, “Yes, I am working on it. She had a new MRI on Sept. 11. I don’t yet have those records.” The email was sent from [EMAIL-784] to [EMAIL-785], with CC to Sarena Tuttle at sarena@whaleylawfirm.com and [EMAIL-2197], and to Gregg Thornton at [EMAIL-2196].

Earlier the same day, Betsy Catron replied (email thread subject: Re: Amy Mills v Forcht Bank), noting that written discovery had been served on the Plaintiff on August 2, 2024, but no response had been received. She stated that the discovery is past due and asked that Aaron provide, within the next week, a timeline for when the responses could be expected so as to avoid moving the Court to compel. She also requested an update on her client’s treatment and, if there is treatment, asked for records as soon as possible. Her signature indicates she is an attorney at Vine Center, located at 333 West Vine Street, Suite 1100, Lexington, KY 40507, with a phone number of [PHONE-788], a fax number of [PHONE-789], and the website www.whtlaw.com.

Aaron Whaley’s signature block identifies the Whaley Law Firm as located at 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222, with a contact phone number of [PHONE-790].

item_159.note: Case: Amy Mills Premise [DOB-819]. The client is Amy Mills. This matter concerns expert services in connection with a fall and related medical evaluations.

In August 2024, Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm reached out regarding a case requiring expert work. In an email sent on August 5, 2024 at 11:02:40 a.m. Eastern Time, addressed to Kathleen Johnson of PMR Life Care Plans, along with Amy Mills Premise and others, Aaron explained that he needed a narrative tying injuries to a fall and an independent medical examination (IME), followed by a life care plan. He described the incident as the client slipping and falling on a marble floor at a bank, noting she was in her late 30s at the time. Since the 2019 fall, she has undergone a cervical fusion and a shoulder labral repair, suffers migraine headaches, and continues to experience shoulder and neck pain. Her current treating physicians at Bluegrass Orthopaedics have vacillated between shoulder- versus neck-related causes. Aaron stated that he has records and timelines prepared and could provide records either organized by date or in total, depending on what is easiest for the recipient.

Earlier, on Friday, July 26, 2024 at approximately 1:20 p.m., Aaron Whaley sent an initial inquiry to [EMAIL-820]. He explained that he had obtained the contact information from Seth Gladstein, who spoke highly of their services, and asked for a list of all the services and doctors available, including good medical reviews, narratives, IME reports, life care plans, and related offerings.

On Saturday, July 27, 2024 at about 10:03 a.m. (time approximate in the record), Kathleen Johnson replied to Aaron. She opened with a warm greeting, noting that it had been a while since they last connected. She attached PMR Life Care Plans, LLC’s current team overview and fee schedule. She announced that in September they would be adding six pediatric PM&R life care planners from Gillette Children’s and Vanderbilt University, as well as four additional adult PM&R physicians from Vanderbilt and the University of Kentucky (UK). She offered to discuss the arrangement in more detail by phone or coffee, noting availability on Monday and Tuesday of the following week.

Johnson described PMR Life Care Plans, LLC as a national provider offering forensic consultation, expert medical opinions, and life care planning services, supported by a team of renowned, board-certified adult and pediatric physical medicine and rehabilitation physicians, orthopedic surgeons, and neurologists. She stated that this group comprises academic leaders, researchers, and practicing clinicians whose primary mission is advancing medical knowledge and patient care, with their forensic work grounded in academic rigor. She explained that PMR’s mission-driven approach underpins their damages valuation services, including impartial assessments of future medical care, loss of earnings capacity, and loss of household services for individuals affected by chronic or catastrophic injuries. Johnson emphasized the commitment to impartiality and professionalism, asserting that their evaluations are trusted, credible, and unbiased. She claimed that PMR not only sets the standard for medical expert consultation but also embodies a mission-driven service essential to the advancement and just resolution of complex litigation cases.

Johnson signed as Kathleen Gormley Johnson, Owner/Chief Strategist/Chief Operations of PMR Life Care Plans, LLC, located at 37 River Hill Rd, Louisville, KY 40207. Her contact details were listed as [EMAIL-821], with the website www.pmrlifecareplans.com, business phone [PHONE-822], and mobile [PHONE-823]. The message included a confidentiality disclaimer noting that it may contain privileged information or work product and advising that unintended recipients should notify the sender and delete the message and any attachments.

The thread also contains an earlier message from Aaron Whaley, dated Friday, July 26, 2024, to [EMAIL-824], reiterating his request for information about services and doctors after a referral from Seth Gladstein, and closing with his signature and firm contact details (Whaley Law Firm, 712 Lyndon Ln, Louisville, KY 40222, [PHONE-825]).
item_159.note_summary: Mills | Mills Premise [DOB-826]. | 2024-08-05

item_160.episode_type: imported
item_160.last_activity: 2024-08-05T00:00:00
item_160.note: In late July and early August 2024, the Whaley Law Firm began coordinating with PMR Life Care Plans to obtain expert services for a case involving Amy Mills, labeled Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-828]. The client, Amy Mills, slipped and fell on a marble bank floor in 2019. In the course of the case, Aaron Whaley sought a narrative linking injuries to the fall, an independent medical examination (IME), and likely a life-care plan to support damages valuation.

The outreach began with Aaron Whaley’s email dated July 26, 2024. He explained that he had learned of PMR Life Care Plans through Seth Gladstein and was seeking a full suite of expert services, including medical reviews, narratives, IMEs, and life-care planning. He noted that the client’s fall occurred in 2019, and that since then she has undergone a cervical fusion and a shoulder labral repair, experienced migraine headaches, and continued to have shoulder and neck pain. He described the treating physicians at Bluegrass Orthopaedics as alternating between attributing her symptoms to the shoulder or to the neck, with the latest assessment indicating the problem might originate from the neck. He stated that he had records and timelines available and asked whether PMR preferred records organized by date or all at once.

Kathleen Johnson of PMR Life Care Plans responded on July 27, 2024. She attached PMR’s current team overview and fee schedule and noted upcoming expansions: in September, PMR planned to add six pediatric PM&R life care planners from Gillette Children’s and Vanderbilt University, and four additional adult PM&R physicians from Vanderbilt and UK. She proposed a meet-and-greet via Zoom with Aaron and a few of the doctors to determine the best fit for the project. Johnson asked for Aaron’s project timeline and inquired whether Mills had been treated at UofL or Frazier, and who the treating physicians were that were debating whether the issue was neck or shoulder, as this information could assist the physicians in determining the best approach.

Earlier that same week, on August 5, 2024, Aaron Whaley sent another email to Kathleen Johnson and Amy Mills, reiterating the request. He explained that the case had been quiet for some time and needed to move forward. He described the client as having fallen on a marble bank floor and, at the time, being in her late 30s. Since the 2019 fall, she had undergone a cervical fusion, a shoulder labral repair, and experienced migraine headaches, with ongoing shoulder and neck pain. He reaffirmed that the current treaters at Bluegrass Orthopaedics had vacillated between shoulder and neck as the source of the pain. He stated that he could provide records already sorted by date or in a single bundle, depending on what was easiest for PMR, and that he could send timelines and materials as needed.

In response, also on August 5, 2024, Kathleen Johnson proposed potential physicians for the project. She suggested Dr. Hockenbury, Dr. Carrasquer, Dr. Chen, or Dr. Wagers as possible experts and expressed a desire to arrange a meet-and-greet Zoom with Aaron and a couple of them to determine the best person for the project. She asked for the project timeline and again inquired about Mills’ treating facilities (UofL or Frazier) and the identifying of treating physicians who were debating whether the neck or the shoulder was the source of the problems, noting that this information would assist the physicians in determining the best approach.

Shortly after, Aaron Whaley sent a follow-up email on August 5, 2024, at approximately 11:03 a.m., addressing Kathleen and Amy Mills. He recalled that the case had been quiet for a while and reiterated the needs: a narrative identifying which injuries were related to the fall, an IME, and likely a life plan. He restated the core facts: Amy Mills slipped and fell on a marble bank floor in 2019 when she was in her late 30s; since the fall she had a cervical fusion, a shoulder labral repair, and migraine headaches, with persistent shoulder and neck pain. He again noted that the treating physicians at Bluegrass Orthopaedics had given differing opinions on whether the symptoms were shoulder- or neck-related and indicated he could provide records either by date or in one complete bundle.

In the original thread dating from July 26, 2024, Aaron had reached out to PMR Life Care Plans seeking a comprehensive list of services and available doctors, asking for examples of the work they could provide (reviews, narratives, IMEs, life-care plans, etc.). Kathleen’s July 27 follow-up included a detailed overview of PMR’s team and outlined upcoming expansions and the firm’s mission to provide impartial, academically grounded forensic consultation and life-care planning for litigation.

Together, the exchanges establish a sequence of outreach and coordination intended to secure PMR Life Care Plans’ expert services for Amy Mills’ case. The team would, if engaged, deliver a narrative linking the fall to Mills’ injuries, conduct an IME, and develop a life-care plan, with consideration given to which doctors should be involved and where Mills was treated, and with the aim of moving the case forward as the parties align on the proper medical source of Mills’ current symptoms.

item_161.note: On August 2, 2024, at 7:11 AM Eastern, the Knox Circuit Court filed an electronic notice in the matter Mills, Amy v. Focht Bank, Case No. 20-CI-00112, Envelope 8503106. The eFiling was submitted by Betsy R. Catron, attorney for the Defendant. The Notice of Electronic Filing (NEF) confirms the filing details for the Knox Circuit Court (Court: KNOX (CIRCUIT)) and the case caption Mills, Amy vs. Focht Bank.

The NEF was electronically mailed to the following recipients: Aaron Gregory Whaley ([EMAIL-830]), Gregg E. Thornton ([EMAIL-2152]), Betsy R. Catron ([EMAIL-2151]), and Joshua Ellis ([EMAIL-2150]). The “To” list originally included Whaley, Thornton, Catron, and Ellis, and the envelope number for this filing is 8503106.

The notice indicates that the document included with this eFiling is “NOTICE OF FILING: NOTICE OF SERVICE.” The recipient may view the document at the provided link: https://kcoj.kycourts.net/eFilingRetrieval/Home/Package?id=63270978-4DD6-4AE5-8779-AA94142BA49B. This filing was automatically generated by the Kentucky Court of Justice eFiling system, and the sender requests that recipients not reply to this email.

Confidentiality notice: This message and any attachment are intended only for the addressee and may contain information that is privileged, confidential, and/or proprietary work product. If you are not the intended recipient, or an authorized employee, agent, or representative of the intended recipient, you should not read, copy, retain, or disseminate this message or its attachments. Do not forward this message without express written consent of the sender. If you have received this message in error, please contact the sender immediately and delete all copies of the message and attachments. Transmission or misdelivery does not waive any applicable legal privilege.

The notice states, “Good morning Amy,” and informs her that the attached documents comprise the defense counsel’s third discovery set and fourth set of requests for production of documents.

The message explains that the requests are simply for an updated list of Amy Mills’s medical providers and for confirmation of whether she is still receiving treatment. It invites Amy to reply with her updated list of providers and with a list of any upcoming appointments related to her incident. The email closes with an offer to discuss any questions directly, and is signed by Sarena M. Tuttle, identified as a Litigation Paralegal and Workers’ Compensation Case Manager at The Whaley Law Firm. The firm’s contact details are: 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222; D: [PHONE-841]; O: [PHONE-842] ext 104; E: Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com (PLEASE NOTE NEW EMAIL ADDRESS).

item_166.note: In the case labeled Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2135], the client is Amy Mills. On Friday, August 2, 2024, at 9:04:20 AM, Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm (712 Lyndon Ln, Louisville, KY 40222; [PHONE-2134]) forwarded an email titled “Fwd: Amy Mills vs. Forecht Bank” to Sarena Tuttle and Amy Mills (emails sarena@whaleylawfirm.com and [EMAIL-850]). The forwarding indicates that the original message being shared concerned the matter of Amy Mills vs. Forecht Bank.

The forwarded message themselves contains an earlier email from Elizabeth Romersa, dated Friday, August 2, 2024, at approximately 7:23 AM (noted as 7:23?AM in the record). The subject of that message was “Re: Amy Mills vs. Forecht Bank,” and it was sent to [EMAIL-851], with Gregg Thornton and Betsy Catron CC’d ([EMAIL-852]). In that email, counsel was advised that attached were Defendant’s Notice of Service and Defendant’s Third Interrogatories and Fourth Request for Production of Documents to Plaintiff regarding the above-referenced matter, and that the office could be contacted with any questions or concerns. The message was signed by Elizabeth Romersa, Paralegal to Walter Ward, William Partin, Betsy Catron, James Freel, Whitney Williams, Aaron Griffith, and Jeanette Jayne.

Elizabeth Romersa’s contact information is listed as part of Whitney Williams, Aaron Griffith, and Jeanette Jayne’s team at Vine Center, located at 333 West Vine Street, Suite 1100, Lexington, KY 40507. The telephone number provided is [PHONE-2130], with fax [PHONE-2129], and the firm’s website is www.whtlaw.com.

The email also contains a standard confidentiality notice stating that the information is privileged and confidential, and instructing recipients who are not the intended recipient not to read, distribute, or reproduce the transmission or attachments. It further directs that if the email was received in error, the recipient should delete it and notify the sender by reply email so the records can be corrected. The message ends with a courtesy thank-you.

item_196.note: Case: Amy Mills – Premise-[DOB-2004]. Client: Amy Mills.

On June 11, 2024, an inbound text message was received from Amy Mills (From Number: [PHONE-2003]) to Aaron at [PHONE-2002]. The message begins with “Aaron it’s Amy” and communicates that Amy has taken action regarding MRI-related compliance issues and regulatory approvals.

In the text, Amy states that she has contacted the federal insurance regulating board and the state regulator due to the inaccurate compliance of the issue with the MRI and is requesting approval. She indicates that within the next 7–14 days an investigation will be conducted into her file and into exactly how Aetnas’s practices are aligned or not aligned. She further notes that once they resolve this minor issue, she will provide an update.

Amy also asserts, “They may be the insurance company but they do Not have the final say! Everyone has a governing body and someone over them!” She emphasizes that she is not letting anything go in this case, even down to the most minor detail, and concludes, “It will be fine and we will be back on track soon.”

This message was automatically generated by the Kentucky Court of Justice eFiling system. Do not reply to this email.

Kentucky Court of Justice Confidentiality Notice

This message and/or attachment is intended only for the addressee and may contain information that is privileged, confidential and/or proprietary work product. If you are not the intended recipient, or an authorized employee, agent or representative of the intended recipient, do not read, copy, retain or disseminate this message or any attachment. Do not forward this message and attachment without the express written consent of the sender. If you have received this message in error, please contact the sender immediately and delete all copies of the message and any attachment. Transmission or misdelivery shall not constitute waiver of any applicable legal privilege.

item_200.note: Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-998]. Client: Amy Mills.

On May 24, 2024, an inbound text message was received from Amy Mills (From number [PHONE-999]) to [PHONE-1983]. In the message she explains that the MRI is being blocked by insurance because the approval was sent to the insurer on the 15th and is still pending. She notes that due to the holiday, no one will be in on Monday, so the billing department will contact them again on Tuesday to provide an update. She states that this is a process, and until everything is worked out and revalued, they will have to wait. She is also looking into getting a cervical 3D scan/X-ray done either in Richmond or Nashville. She reports that she is in a lot of pain continuously and is actively working to resolve this physical issue, but she says it is “on the bank for their negligence.” She adds, “I did not do this to my self! They are liable!” She further declares, “I’m not going to lay down on this and ever I will be dotted and T crossed! And I don’t care how long it takes!” She states that they can offer her a substantial amount of money for this case because of future medical expenses, or they can set on it until I’m ready. The inbound message was sent on 2024-05-24 from [PHONE-1982] to [PHONE-1002].
item_200.note_summary: Mills | . Client: Amy Mills. | 2024-05-24

item_204.note: Case: Amy Mills. The client is Amy Mills, and the case is titled Amy Mills v. Forcht Bank. The case file is labeled Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-1968].

On May 23, 2024, at 15:50:36 -0400, Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm forwarded an email to Sarena Tuttle regarding Amy Mills v. Forcht Bank. The forwarded message was part of an email thread concerning the representation in this matter.

In the forwarded message, dated May 23, 2024, at 3:40 PM (noted in the original as 3:40?PM), Dante Clay, a paralegal to Gregg Thornton and to Licha H. Farah, Jr., Brian M. Gudalis, Jillian D. House, and Clay E. Thornton, notified Aaron Whaley that the attached document contained the Notice of Entry of Appearance for Gregg Thornton. The message was sent in reference to the Amy Mills v. Forcht Bank matter, with the subject line “Amy Mills v. Forcht Bank.” It identified the sender as Dante Clay and included the firm details for Vine Center, located at 333 West Vine Street, Suite 1100, Lexington, KY 40507, along with the telephone number [PHONE-1016], the fax number [PHONE-1017], and the website www.whtlaw.com. The email listed the recipient as Aaron G. Whaley at [EMAIL-1018].

The message to Aaron Whaley from Dante Clay included a standard confidentiality notice stating that the information in the email transmission is privileged and confidential, and instructed that if the recipient was not the intended recipient, they should not read, distribute, or reproduce the transmission (including any attachments). If the email had been received in error, the recipient was asked to delete it and notify the sender by reply email so that records could be corrected. The communication concluded with the Whaley Law Firm signature, including the sender’s name, and contact information for Whaley Law Firm: 712 Lyndon Ln, Louisville, KY 40222, [PHONE-1019].
item_204.note_summary: Mills | Mills. | 2024-05-24

item_206.note: The Knox Circuit Court electronically filed a notice in the matter of Mills, Amy v. Focht Bank, Case No. 20-CI-00112, with the file labeled Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-1023] and envelope number 8018865. The notice shows that the eFiler was Gregg E. Thornton, attorney for the defendant, and that the case is captioned MILLS, AMY VS. FORCHT BANK. Catron ([EMAIL-1029]), and Joshua Ellis ([EMAIL-1030]). It also notes that this was automatically generated by the Kentucky Court of Justice eFiling system and that recipients should not reply to the email.

The email contains the Kentucky Court of Justice Confidentiality Notice. It states that the message and any attachments are intended only for the addressee and may contain information that is privileged, confidential, and/or proprietary work product. If you are not the intended recipient or an authorized employee, agent, or representative of the intended recipient, you should not read, copy, retain, or disseminate the message or attachments, and you should not forward them without the sender’s express written consent. If you have received the message in error, you should contact the sender immediately and delete all copies of the message and attachments. Transmission or misdelivery does not constitute a waiver of any applicable legal privilege.
