item_207.note: Case: Amy Mills – Premise [DOB-1951]. Client: Amy Mills.

On May 23, 2024 at 2:02 PM, Sarena Tuttle, a litigation paralegal and Workers’ Compensation Case Manager at The Whaley Law Firm, forwarded a new voice message regarding Amy Mills to Greg Whaley with the note: “Hey AW, Sounds like DC is ready to discuss a settlement on Amy Mills. :)” The forwarding email was dated May 23, 2024 at 14:28:25 -0400 and came from Sarena M. Tuttle, whose contact details are listed as The Whaley Law Firm, 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222. Her direct line is [PHONE-1033], and her office number is [PHONE-1034] ext 104. Her email address is Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com, with a note indicating a new email address.

The forwarded message included the subject line: “Fwd: New Voice Message from Faye Gaither 103 on [DOB-1948] 2:02 PM.” It shows the original sender as RingCentral, and the header indicates the message was sent on Thursday, May 23, 2024, at 2:02 PM, to Sarena Tuttle. The voicemail was logged with a length of 00:20 and was directed to the contact number for Sarena: [PHONE-1036], extension 104 (Sarena Tuttle). The voicemail preview contains the following spoken content: “Hey, Sarena, it is Greg Thorn. I am an attorney in Lexington. I am calling you on your client, Amy Mills. I would left a message for her, and this was a case I wanted to see if we could get settled. My number is 859422 6,000, thanks.” The message also notes that you can listen to this message on your phone or log in to your RingCentral account using your main number, extension number, and password, and that you can manage your voicemails in your RingCentral account.
item_207.note_summary: Mills | Mills – Premise [DOB-1037]. | 2024-05-23

item_212.note: Initial client contact on May 22, 2024, with Amy Mills. The communication was an inbound text message sent from [PHONE-1057] to [PHONE-1058], in relation to the case Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-1059].

In the message, Mills states she has been sick with some kind of flu-like illness in addition to her injuries, and that it has been trying. She notes she is getting a new MRI for her shoulder with a new physician and a follow-up appointment in a week to review the results, and she will let you know more then. She adds, "They are out of their ever loving mind if they think I'm settling for the offer." She further explains that she has since spoken to a judge who is a close family friend and who has confirmed her thoughts as well, concluding, "I'm not settling and they can wait."

item_214.note: In the case Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-1065], the client is Amy Mills. On 2024-05-22, an outbound text message was sent from [PHONE-1066] to [PHONE-1067].

The message read: "Amy, I am just following up. Defense counsel has reached out regarding resolution or setting for trial. He has your records from Bluegrass and since they said you don't need surgery and you didn't follow up as recommended it appears you are done actively treating. Let me know a good time to talk. I can even come down to see you." This note indicates the sender is following up regarding resolution or a trial setting, that defense counsel has the client's Bluegrass records, that those records indicate you do not need surgery and that you did not follow up as recommended, and that it appears you are no longer actively treating. The sender offers to discuss at a convenient time and even to come see you.

item_217.note: Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-1904]. Initial client contact on May 15, 2024 with Amy Mills via an inbound text message from [PHONE-1903] to [PHONE-1081] regarding ongoing medical updates and bereavement.

The message indicates she is being scheduled for another updated MRI of her shoulder with contrast, and that once that MRI is finished she will be scheduled for a follow-up appointment to know more. She says they are doing fairly okay, but it has been a hard long road. She notes there were so many people at the funeral it was unreal; the funeral home couldn't hold them and there were lines down the street of people trying to get in. From city counsel members to judges and politicians to local business owners, and they came from other states even. She adds that it hasn't been easy but we r making it one day at a time. The message was sent on 2024-05-15 via the inbound channel.
item_217.note_summary: Mills | . Initial client contact on May 15,... | 2024-05-15

item_218.note: Case: Amy Mills – Premise file Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-1900]. Client: Amy Mills.

On 2024-05-15, an inbound text message was received from [PHONE-1084] to [PHONE-1085]. The message content is: "I haven't told him nothing about any lawsuit or anything and I'm not going to bc I do not want that to be a factor and I think it has played a huge roll in not getting the treatment I need to heal . Once they know that they don't want to mess with it anymore bc it may cause extra work! I also talked to other physicians about this possibility and their consensus was they same and they try to always refer those cases out if at all possible bc it's a headache So I'm not telling him anything until I get a complete diagnosis". The direction of the message is inbound.

item_231.note: Case: Amy Mills (Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-1846]). Client: Amy Mills. On 2024-04-09, an outbound text message was sent from [PHONE-1138] to [PHONE-1139] in relation to Amy Mills.

The message reads: "Amy you are right on the unknowns and I don't know what was offered to the person pre trial. My point is with a settlement you know what you are getting and you can control it. Jury's are completely unknowns. The $170,000 awarded was before fault was considered. I am just making you aware of the risk. Let me know when you have a final say so on the Dr."

The message content was: "I hope the new Dr. will take you on as a patient. Also, be careful with stories like your Walmart one. For instance there was a jury verdict last week of a man tripping on a pipe that was exposed at a gas station. He had his hip replaced and two shoulder surgeries. $150,000 in medical bills. The jury gave him $170,000. That is real and shows how unpredictable juries are."

item_242.note: In the case file for Amy Mills, titled Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-1803], the client is Amy Mills. The communications center on gathering and updating medical records from Bluegrass Orthopedics, PSC, and on tracking appointments related to her care.

On April 3, 2024, at 11:02:32 a.m. (04:00 offset -0400), Sarena M. Tuttle, the Whaley Law Firm’s litigation paralegal and workers’ compensation case manager, wrote to Betsy R. Catron with the subject FW: Amy Mills. She advised that she was attaching the most recent records received from Bluegrass Orthopedics, PSC and invited Betsy to contact her directly if anything further was needed to discuss the matter in detail. Tuttle noted her new email address and provided her contact information: The Whaley Law Firm, 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222; direct line [PHONE-1181]; office line [PHONE-1182] ext 104; email Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com.

Earlier, on April 2, 2024, at 10:31?PM, Aaron Whaley of The Whaley Law Firm forwarded a message. The forwarded content shows Whaley’s firm details (712 Lyndon Ln, Louisville, KY 40222; [PHONE-1800]) and a note indicating a forwarding of prior correspondence without substantive changes to the case record at that time.

The forwarded message originated from Betsy Catron, dated April 2, 2024 at 4:11?PM, addressed to Aaron Whaley with the subject FW: Amy Mills. Betsy stated that attached were records from Bluegrass Ortho for Amy Mills and asked whether there was anything from 2024 or an update on Amy’s March appointment.

Before this, Betsy had emailed on March 1, 2024 at 12:07 PM, sending a reply to Aaron with a brief “RE: Amy Mills” and simply “Ok, thanks.”

Prior to that, Aaron Whaley had replied on March 1, 2024 at 11:35 AM to Betsy. His message explained that Amy could not make the appointment during a snowstorm, and that she was currently rescheduled for March 11.

On March 1, 2024, at 7:43?AM, Betsy had written to Aaron asking for updates. She noted that there was difficulty obtaining records from Bluegrass Orthopedics and PT Pros and requested any updated records as well as information following the January 6 MRI.

The chain also contains prior correspondence from January 12, 2024. At 12:04 PM, Aaron Whaley wrote to Betsy (Cc’d to Sarena Tuttle and Amy Mills). He stated that the doctor Dr. Huff wanted Amy to see was Dr. McCombs; however, Dr. McCombs is retired. Dr. Huff referred Amy to a spine orthopedic specialist within Bluegrass Orthopedics. He also noted that Amy had an updated MRI on January 6 and that the results were pending.

Earlier that same day, January 12, 2024 at 12:00 PM, Betsy had written: she was trying to obtain all of Ms. Mills’ records from Bluegrass Orthopedics and PT Pros and asked whether Amy Mills had presented to the Nashville clinic she was referred to; if so, Betsy requested details of the provider. Betsy noted that Bluegrass Orthopedics’ record referred to Dr. Paul McCombs in Nashville, but that he is apparently retired, having previously been associated with Howell Allen clinic.

The thread includes the signatures and contact details of the participants. Betsy Catron’s signature lists Vine Center, 333 West Vine Street, Suite 1100, Lexington, KY 40507, with phone and fax numbers (P) [PHONE-1184] and (F) [PHONE-1185], and the website www.whtlaw.com. Aaron Whaley’s signature lists Whaley Law Firm, 712 Lyndon Ln, Louisville, KY 40222, with phone [PHONE-1186] and a standard external email caution. Sarena Tuttle’s signature lists her role at The Whaley Law Firm and provides her contact information, including the note “PLEASE NOTE NEW EMAIL ADDRESS” for Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com. The communications reference the new email address for Sarena and include the firm’s Louisville address and phone numbers.

Together, the exchanges reflect ongoing efforts to obtain 2024 records, confirm the status of Amy Mills’s March appointment, and clarify details regarding the referred Nashville provider and the January 6 MRI results, with the latest update being that the most recent records from Bluegrass Orthopedics were attached to the April 3, 2024 email from Sarena Tuttle.

item_252.note: Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-1227]. The client involved is Amy Mills.

The record begins with a new-case inquiry. On March 27, 2024, Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm (712 Lyndon Ln, Louisville, KY 40222; phone [PHONE-1228]; [EMAIL-1229]) reached out to Kate Smith ([EMAIL-1230]) regarding a potential review by Dr. Orlando. He explained that Dr. Orlando comes highly recommended by a mutual contact, Robert Mattingly, and noted that the case is fairly complicated and he wanted to know what he needed to do to get things rolling. He also indicated that this inquiry related to a case involving Amy Mills.

In response, on March 27, 2024, Kate Smith introduced herself. She explained that she is a nurse and a life care planner who works with Dr. Orlando and helps manage his schedule, records, and related logistics. She stated that she and Dr. Orlando often collaborate on catastrophic cases requiring an IME (independent medical examination) and an LCP (life care plan), though they can work jointly or individually. She attached their standard materials, including an introduction letter, CVs, fee schedules, and W-9s, and noted that she also has her own information on file. She provided her contact details: direct line [PHONE-1231] and email [EMAIL-1232], and offered to discuss the case further by phone. She listed available times for a call: March 28 anytime from 11 a.m. to 4 p.m., March 29 at 9 a.m., April 1 from 10 a.m. to 2 p.m., and April 2 from 10 a.m. to 4 p.m. She also included a notice stating that the message and attachments may contain sensitive information and are for the intended recipient.

Also on March 27, 2024, Aaron Whaley replied to Kate, asking for clarification. He stated that he was inquiring about having one of his cases reviewed by Dr. Orlando, noting that Dr. Orlando comes highly recommended and that Robert Mattingly—his good friend—had recommended Dr. Orlando. He described the case as pretty complicated and asked what he needed to do to get the ball rolling.

On March 27, 2024 at 2:27 p.m., Aaron sent a further message reinforcing his inquiry about Dr. Orlando and thanking Kate for her time.

On March 28, 2024 at 1:03 p.m., Kate replied that it had been great speaking with Aaron and that she had discussed the case with Dr. Orlando; unfortunately, they would not be able to assist with the case.

At 1:06 p.m. on March 28, Aaron asked, “Ok. Why?”

Then at 1:16 p.m., Kate responded that, due to their current busy schedule, they have had to be more selective when accepting cases. She suggested that the case may be more appropriate for a neuro-headache specialist who can discern what are new migraine symptoms, specifically distinguishing those symptoms from a fall versus pre-existing conditions.

Finally, at 1:26 p.m. on March 28, Aaron sent a closing reply: “Ok, thank you. You would not happen to have someone to refer in that area?”

This summarizes the sequence of outreach, responses, and referrals discussed in the correspondence regarding the Amy Mills Premise case.

item_253.note: This note documents a sequence of email communications about a new case for Amy Mills, filed under Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-1749].

On March 27, 2024, Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm in Louisville, Kentucky, reached out to Kate Smith to inquire about having Dr. Orlando review one of his cases. He explained that Dr. Orlando comes highly recommended by his friend Robert Mattingly and described the matter as fairly complicated, asking what steps he needed to take to get the process started. The email was sent to Kate Smith (and included Amy Mills Premise [DOB-1748] in the cc line, along with Marc Orlando and Amy Mills’ filevine-associated contact) and carried Aaron’s signature detailing the Whaley Law Firm’s address and phone number.

On the same day, March 27, 2024 at 2:27 PM, Kate replied. She introduced herself as Kate Smith, a nurse and life care planner who works with Dr. Orlando and helps keep his schedule organized and records uploaded. She stated that they often handle catastrophic cases where an independent medical examination (IME) and a life care plan (LCP) are needed, and she noted that they work both jointly and individually. She attached their usual forms, including an introduction letter, CVs, fee schedules, and W-9s, and mentioned that she also had her own information attached. Kate provided her direct line [PHONE-1747] and her email address [EMAIL-1237]. She also outlined availability for a phone call: March 28 anytime from 11:00 AM to 4:00 PM, March 29 at 9:00 AM, April 1 from 10:00 AM to 2:00 PM, and April 2 from 10:00 AM to 4:00 PM. She emphasized that Dr. Orlando and she frequently work together on catastrophic cases and that they can work jointly or separately. The message included a standard notice indicating that the email and attachments may contain sensitive information intended only for the recipient.

Later the same day, Aaron responded with appreciation but asked for further guidance, writing: “Kate, I am inquiring about getting one of my cases reviewed by Dr. Orlando. He comes highly recommended. Robert Mattingly is one of my good friends and he recommended Dr. Orlando. I have a pretty complicated case and want to know what I need to do to get the ball rolling. Thank you so much for your time.” His signature followed, listing his firm’s name and contact information.

Shortly after, on March 28, 2024 at 1:03 PM, Kate followed up to Aaron with a brief note: it had been great speaking with him earlier that day and that she had discussed the case with Dr. Orlando, but unfortunately, this is a case they would not be able to assist with. Aaron replied about five minutes later, asking, “Ok. Why?”

This chain of emails establishes that the matter originated as a new case for Amy Mills (Amy Mills Premise [DOB-1745]) and that Aaron Whaley sought Dr. Orlando’s review, a request ultimately declined by Kate after consultation with Dr. Orlando. The communications include the initial inquiry, the information about Dr. Orlando’s team and services, the attachments provided, the availability for a call, the standard notice about sensitive information, and the final determination that the case could not be accepted.

item_257.note: On March 15, 2024, in a message titled “Re: Amy Mills,” Sarena M. Tuttle, a Litigation Paralegal and Workers’ Compensation Case Manager at The Whaley Law Firm, reported continuing efforts to obtain records for Amy Mills. She stated that she had sent CRR on Tuesday and had called the Facility and LVM for the Records Clerk, Nicole. She noted that she called the Facility again that day and LVM again for Nicole. The recording she referenced indicated a 24–48 hour callback window and that RR takes 7–10 days. Based on the recording, she expected to have CMRs by the end of next week. She planned to call Nicole again before leaving that day and again on Monday if the records were not received. The message included her signature block with her contact information: Sarena M. Tuttle, Litigation Paralegal and Workers’ Compensation Case Manager at The Whaley Law Firm, 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222; direct line [PHONE-1253]; office line [PHONE-1254] ext 104; email Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com (noting a new email address). The CC line also listed Amy Mills and the email [EMAIL-1255].

Earlier, on March 14, 2024, in an email from Aaron Whaley, he wrote that Amy Mills had seen a doctor on Monday—Dr. Hunt, who is in the same practice as Dr. Huff. Dr. Huff is described as a shoulder doctor, and he had stated that Amy’s problems are from her neck. Dr. Hunt, who had just seen her, said the issue is neck-related, and yet Whaley notes that Dr. Hunt says, “no, it’s your shoulder.” As a result, they need the record from Amy’s most recent visit, and once that record is obtained, they will likely send something to the assistant at Bluegrass Orthopedics to determine which clinician handles the shoulder versus the neck.

In a written note dated March 14, 2024 at 17:33:08 -0400, Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm sent an email from [EMAIL-1265] to Sarena Tuttle (sarena@whaleylawfirm.com) and Amy Mills ([EMAIL-1266]) regarding Amy Mills. The message explains that Amy Mills saw a doctor on Monday—Dr. Hunt, who is in the same practice as Dr. Huff. Dr. Huff is described as a shoulder doctor who had previously said that Amy’s problems originated from her neck, which would make Dr. Hunt a neck doctor. However, Dr. Hunt, who had just seen Amy, now says that the issue is not in the neck but in the shoulder. Because of this discrepancy, the note states that they need the record from the most recent visit, and once that record is obtained, they probably need to send something to the assistant they’ve been dealing with over at Bluegrass Orthopedics to figure out which doctor is addressing the shoulder or neck.

The note concludes with Whaley’s contact information and firm details: Aaron Whaley, Whaley Law Firm, 712 Lyndon Ln, Louisville, KY 40222, [PHONE-1267].

item_266.note: This narrative summarizes the email communications in the Amy Mills case (Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-1291]) regarding MRI and orthopedic follow-up, as documented by The Whaley Law Firm.

On March 12, 2024, Sarena Tuttle, a litigation paralegal and workers’ compensation case manager for The Whaley Law Firm, sent an email about the MRI and ortho follow-up. The subject was Re: MRI and Ortho Follow up. In the body of the message, she noted that a task had been set to order the MRI that day, and she had called Nicole (HIM) to request that the MRI report be faxed. She also stated that she sent a CRR via fax, and that confirmation had been received, with the matter now being that the practice is awaiting records and bills. The email was directed to Aaron Whaley, with Amy Mills CC’d, and included Sarena’s signature block with her title and contact information, along with a note to please note the new email address. The message included The Whaley Law Firm’s address at 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY, and listed direct line and main office numbers.

The record also captures prior correspondence in the chain. On February 14, 2024, at approximately 1:13 PM, Aaron Whaley wrote to Amy Mills, checking in to see whether she had been able to obtain the MRI results and whether she had recently seen the orthopedic specialist or had a scheduled appointment. He closed with thanks.

Later, on March 9, 2024, at approximately 1:43 PM, Aaron Whaley again wrote, stating simply, “Need this MRI report ASAP.”

Also on March 9, 2024, in a forwarded message from Amy Mills dated around 1:25 PM, she replied to Aaron Whaley: “Hello, Yes I did, I had sent an email on this a few times actually. I will be going to a specialist on Monday. I will keep you updated. Thanks, AM.” This indicates that Amy had previously sent multiple emails on the MRI/report issue and that she anticipated a specialist appointment on the following Monday, with a commitment to provide updates.

In summary, the March 12, 2024 note confirms that the team had requested the MRI report be faxed, had arranged for related records (CRR) to be faxed, and were awaiting the associated records and bills, while the February and March exchanges show ongoing client inquiries about MRI results and orthopedic follow-up, and Amy Mills’ confirmation of a pending specialist visit and ongoing updates.
item_266.note_summary: Mills | This narrative summarizes the email... | 2024-03-12
item_266.project_name: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-1292]
item_266.uuid: 767a639c-8e22-48aa-91a0-148bbf23ce2c

item_267.note: Case: Amy Mills – Premise [DOB-1293]. Client: Amy Mills.

Re: Amy Mills. On Friday, March 1, 2024, at 11:35:24 AM, in an email from Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm ([EMAIL-1689]) to Betsy R. Catron of Vine Center ([EMAIL-1688]), with copies to Sarena Tuttle (sarena@whaleylawfirm.com) and Amy Mills ([EMAIL-1296]), Aaron stated that Amy Mills could not attend the previously scheduled appointment due to one of the bad snowstorms and that she is currently scheduled for March 11.

Earlier that day, at 7:43 AM (time shown as 7:43?AM in the record), Betsy Catron replied, explaining that they were having difficulty obtaining records from Bluegrass Orthopaedics (Ortho) and PT Pros, and asking Aaron for any updated records he could provide, as well as any further information following the January 6 MRI.

Looking back to January, 2024, the thread shows that on Friday, January 12, 2024 at 12:04 PM, Aaron Whaley wrote to Betsy Catron (cc’d to Sarena Tuttle and Amy Mills) confirming that the doctor Dr. Huff had wanted Amy to see was Dr. McCombs, but Dr. McCombs is retired. Dr. Huff then referred Amy to a spine orthopedic provider within Bluegrass Orthopaedics. Aaron noted that Amy had an updated MRI on January 6 and that they were waiting on the results.

Also on January 12, 2024, at 12:00 PM (time noted as 12:00?PM in the record), Betsy Catron wrote again, stating that they were still working on obtaining all of Ms. Mills’ records from Bluegrass Ortho and PT Pros. Betsy asked whether Amy had presented to the Nashville clinic referenced by Bluegrass Ortho, and if so, for details of the provider. The Bluegrass Ortho record referred to Dr. Paul McCombs in Nashville, though it appeared that he is now retired; she noted that he had previously been associated with the Howell Allen clinic.

Betsy’s signature block then followed, listing her as Attorney at Vine Center, with the address 333 West Vine Street, Suite 1100, Lexington, KY 40507, and contact information: P [PHONE-1297], F [PHONE-1298], www.whtlaw.com.

The thread also includes Aaron’s closing signature, showing his information as part of the Whaley Law Firm: 712 Lyndon Ln, Louisville, KY 40222, [PHONE-1299], and a standard external email caution notice.

In summary, the communications between January and March 2024 revolved around locating and obtaining Amy Mills’ medical records from Bluegrass Ortho and PT Pros, confirming which physician Amy was referred to (initially Dr. McCombs, who is retired), noting a January 6 MRI with results still pending, and coordinating scheduling after a snowstorm prevented an earlier appointment, with Amy now scheduled for March 11.

item_269.note: Case: Amy Mills (Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-1679]). Client: Amy Mills.

On March 9, 2024, an email thread titled “MRI and Ortho Follow up” was sent from Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm to Sarena Tuttle and Amy Mills. The message, dated Saturday, March 9, 2024 at 13:43:30 -0500, stated simply: “Need this MRI report ASAP.” The email was part of a forwarded chain that began with a note from Amy Mills.

In her reply dated March 9, 2024 at approximately 1:25 PM (noted as 1:25?PM), Amy Mills wrote: “Hello, Yes I did, I had sent an email on this a few times actually. I will be going to a specialist on Monday. I will keep you updated. Thanks, AM.” This was a response to the prior inquiry about whether she had obtained the MRI and whether results were available, and whether she had seen the orthopedist or had a scheduled appointment.

The earlier portion of the thread, dated February 14, 2024 at 1:13 PM, contained Aaron Whaley’s query: “Hi, Amy, I am just checking in to see if you were able to get the MRI, and if so, have you gotten the results? Have you been to see the ortho recently or have a scheduled appointment? Thanks.” The forwarded message includes the signatures associated with the Whaley Law Firm: Aaron Whaley, Whaley Law Firm, 712 Lyndon Ln, Louisville, KY 40222, [PHONE-1305], and the recipient addresses: sarena@whaleylawfirm.com (Sarena Tuttle) and [EMAIL-1306] (Amy Mills), along with Aaron’s email [EMAIL-1307].

item_269.uuid: 0cd7a2dc-194c-422e-8028-4e8a21dccc54
item_270.author_name: Sarena Tuttle
item_270.episode_type: imported
item_270.last_activity: 2024-03-05T00:00:00
item_270.note: Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-1673]. Client: Amy Mills.

On March 1, 2024, Betsy R. Catron, attorney at WHT Law, sent a message about Amy Mills to Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm, with Sarena Tuttle and Amy Mills copied on the email. Betsy reported that the team was having difficulty obtaining records from Bluegrass Ortho and PT Pros and asked Aaron whether he had any updated records he could share. She also requested any additional information following Amy Mills’ MRI performed on January 6. Betsy signed the email as an attorney at Vine Center, Lexington, Kentucky, and noted the firm’s contact details. The thread also included the standard external email caution line.

Earlier in the chain, on January 12, 2024, Aaron Whaley replied to Betsy. He explained that the doctor Dr. Huff wanted Amy Mills to see was Dr. McCombs, but Dr. McCombs is retired. Dr. Huff then referred Amy to a spine orthopedic specialist within Bluegrass Orthopaedics. He also noted that Amy had an updated MRI on January 6 and that the results were still pending.

In Betsy’s January 12, 2024 reply (time approximately noon), she asked for a status update on the case. She inquired whether Amy Mills had presented to the Nashville clinic to which she was referred and, if so, requested details about the provider. Betsy observed that the Bluegrass Orthopaedics record referenced Dr. Paul McCombs in Nashville; however, she noted that Dr. McCombs is now retired. She added that he had previously been associated with the Howell Allen clinic. Betsy’s message indicated that they were continuing to gather all of Ms. Mills’ records from Bluegrass Orthopaedics and PT Pros and sought information regarding the Nashville referral provider.

The March 1, 2024 note concludes with Betsy’s signature and the law firm’s standard contact information. The email chain includes the following signatories and addresses: Betsy Catron, Attorney, WHT Law (Vine Center, 333 West Vine Street, Suite 1100, Lexington, KY 40507; P [PHONE-1311]; F [PHONE-1671]; www.whtlaw.com), and references to Aaron Whaley and the Whaley Law Firm, along with the CC’d recipients Sarena Tuttle and Amy Mills.

item_271.note: Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-1314]. Client: Amy Mills.

In January 2024, the record shows an ongoing correspondence between attorney Betsy Catron of WHT Law and Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm regarding Amy Mills. On January 12, 2024 at 12:00 PM, Betsy Catron wrote to Aaron inquiring about obtaining all of Ms. Mills’ records from Bluegrass Ortho and PT Pros. She asked whether Ms. Mills had presented to the Nashville clinic to which she was referred and requested details about that provider. Betsy noted that the Bluegrass Ortho record referred to Dr. Paul McCombs in Nashville; however, she understood that Dr. McCombs is now retired and that he was previously affiliated with the Howell Allen clinic. Betsy asked for confirmation of provider details and for any additional records.

That same day, January 12, 2024 at 12:04 PM, Aaron Whaley replied to Betsy. He explained that the doctor Dr. Huff had wanted Amy Mills to see was Dr. Paul McCombs, but Dr. McCombs is retired. Dr. Huff had referred Amy to a spine orthopedic surgeon within Bluegrass Orthopaedics. Aaron added that Amy had undergone an updated MRI on January 6 and that they were waiting for the results.

Earlier, on January 12, 2024, Betsy Catron had already sent the initial inquiry regarding records and the Nashville referral. The thread shows Betsy’s concern about sourcing records from Bluegrass Ortho and PT Pros, and her request for information about the January 6 MRI results.

Moving forward to March 2024, the activity continues. On March 1, 2024 at 7:43 AM, Betsy sent another message reiterating that they were having difficulty obtaining records from Bluegrass Ortho and PT Pros. She asked whether Aaron had any updated records he could provide and whether there was any further information following the January 6 MRI.

Later that same day, at 11:35 AM, Aaron responded to Betsy. He stated that Amy Mills could not attend the previously scheduled appointment due to a severe snowstorm. He noted that Amy is currently scheduled for an appointment on March 11. The exchange confirms that the schedule adjustment was weather-related and that plans were set for a later date.

item_286.note: Case: Amy Mills — Premise-[DOB-1371]. Client: Amy Mills. The matter title is MRI and Ortho Follow up.

On February 14, 2024, at 13:13:12 -0500, Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm sent an email (From: [EMAIL-1372]) to Amy Mills at two addresses: [EMAIL-1373] and [EMAIL-1609], with the subject MRI and Ortho Follow up.

In the body of the message, Whaley wrote: “Hi, Amy, I am just checking in to see if you were able to get the MRI, and if so, have you gotten the results? Have you been to see the ortho recently or have a scheduled appointment? Thanks.”

item_287.note: Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-1378]. Client: Amy Mills.

In February 2024, Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm emailed Anna Mercer, the clinical assistant to Dr. Wallace Huff at Bluegrass Orthopaedics, and Amy Mills regarding Amy Mills. He noted that Dr. Huff had referred Mrs. Mills to another doctor at Bluegrass Orthopaedics to evaluate her neck and asked Anna if she knew who that doctor was, and whether that doctor had someone as helpful as Anna whom he could contact.

The email thread from October 2023 shows ongoing coordination about Dr. Huff’s involvement. On October 24, 2023, Anna Mercer indicated she was sending “the note Dr. Huff dictated today.” Earlier in the day, Aaron Whaley had provided a phone number: [PHONE-1604]. Earlier still, Anna asked which phone number she should have Dr. Huff call.

The thread also records several scheduling and billing details. On October 16, 2023, Anna stated that Dr. Huff could do a telephone conference on [DOB-1380] at 3:45 PM or [DOB-1381] at 3:30 PM, and she requested a fax number to send the invoice, noting that payment would need to be received 48 business hours before the conference to avoid cancellation or postponement. Aaron confirmed that 10/24/23 worked for him. Earlier that same day, Anna listed the two available dates for the conference ([DOB-1382] at 3:45 PM and [DOB-1383] at 3:30 PM) and offered to send the invoice once a date was chosen. Aaron responded affirmatively that 10/24/23 would work.

On October 16 and 17, 2023 there were continued exchanges about logistics and payment. Aaron provided a contact number of [PHONE-1384], and Anna explained the conference arrangements and the 30-minute block rate of $1,000 for a telephone conference with Dr. Huff. There are also notes indicating the invoice was faxed and that a check had been issued, with subsequent confirmations that the check had gone out. Anna’s responses repeatedly referenced the schedule and the invoice process, and Aaron’s replies confirmed acceptance of the proposed arrangements.

The email chain includes the usual signatures and contact details for the parties. Anna Mercer is identified as the Clinical Assistant to Dr. Wallace Huff at Bluegrass Orthopaedics, located at 3401 Yorkshire Medical Park, Lexington, KY 40509, with a phone number of [PHONE-1385] Ext. 226 and a corresponding website. The signatures also contain standard confidentiality notices consistent with the Electronic Communications Privacy Act, and the messages reference typical disclaimers about privileged and confidential information and the proper handling of misdirected messages. The Whaley Law Firm signatures repeatedly appear with their Louisville, Kentucky address and phone number as well.
item_287.note_summary: Mills | . Client: Amy Mills. | 2024-02-14
item_287.project_name: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-1597]
item_287.uuid: b264791b-9bf8-4e92-b59a-d66ac62f927f
item_288.author_name: Aaron Whaley
item_288.episode_type: imported
item_288.last_activity: 2024-02-14T00:00:00
item_288.note: Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-1387]. Client: Amy Mills.

On February 14, 2024, a forward of Amy Mills’ file was sent by Aaron Whaley to Amy Mills’ address. The message includes a note from Anna Mercer stating that Dr. Huff referred Amy Mills to Dr. Hunt for evaluation, but the appointment was canceled because Amy Mills canceled her MRI at Lexington Diagnostics. Anna notes that Dr. Hunt’s assistant is Kristen Cox and that her extension is 364.

In a separate thread within the same forward, dated February 14, 2024 at 1:11 PM, Aaron Whaley asked Anna Mercer if she could identify which doctor at Bluegrass Orthopaedics had been referred to for a neck evaluation and whether that doctor had an assistant as helpful as Anna, so he could reach out if needed. Anna had previously sent a note on October 24, 2023, indicating “Here is the note Dr. Huff dictated today,” which suggests Dr. Huff’s referral and related details were being circulated to the parties.

The October 2023 portion of the chain shows additional context about coordinating Dr. Huff’s involvement. Communication on October 24, 2023 included notes of a telephone conference for Dr. Huff and related scheduling details. The thread shows a contact number used in these communications: [PHONE-1388]. Earlier exchanges on October 16, 2023 included the number [PHONE-1389], and discussions about which phone number Dr. Huff should call. There were arrangements for a telephone conference with Dr. Huff, typically conducted on Tuesday afternoons, at a rate of $1,000 per 30 minutes. Dr. Huff offered two possible times for the conference: October 24, 2023 at 3:45 PM or October 31, 2023 at 3:30 PM, with Dr. Huff’s assistant and scheduling details handled through Anna Mercer, Clinical Assistant to Dr. Wallace Huff of Bluegrass Orthopaedics.

The correspondence also covered logistical and billing aspects. Anna asked for a fax number to send an invoice and stated that payment would need to be received 48 business hours before the conference to prevent cancellation or postponement. Several notes reference the invoicing process, including mentions of sending the invoice and a subsequent payment note that the check had gone out. Specific contact and billing details appear in the thread, including a telephone number for inquiries [PHONE-1390]) and a fax line (the exact fax number appears in the exchange as part of the invoicing discussion). The chain includes the standard confidentiality notices typical of these professional communications, with signatures for Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm (712 Lyndon Ln, Louisville, KY 40222, [PHONE-1391]) and for Anna Mercer of Bluegrass Orthopaedics (3401 Yorkshire Medical Park, Lexington, KY 40509, [PHONE-1392] Ext. 226).

In summary, the notes reflect: Dr. Huff referred Amy Mills to Dr. Hunt (Dr. Hunt’s assistant is Kristen Cox, extension 364) for neck evaluation, but the appointment was canceled due to Amy Mills canceling her MRI at Lexington Diagnostics. The broader thread documents prior efforts to identify the referred Bluegrass Orthopaedics physician, coordinate a Dr. Huff teleconference (usually on Tuesdays) at a cost of $1,000 per 30 minutes, and manage the associated invoicing and payment timeline, including specific phone and fax numbers and the parties’ contact details.

item_289.author_name: Aaron Whaley
item_289.episode_type: imported
item_289.last_activity: 2024-02-05T00:00:00
item_289.note: This narrative consolidates the email communications in the Amy Mills case, focusing on the Dr. Huff meeting, referrals, MRI scheduling, and related medical-legal strategy, from October 24, 2023 through February 5, 2024.

On October 24, 2023, Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm reported that he had just met with Dr. Huff and attached the note from that meeting. Dr. Huff referred Amy to Dr. Hunt, another doctor at the same clinic, for a neck evaluation. He instructed Amy to obtain a new cervical MRI first and to follow up with that appointment. Dr. Huff also recommended that Amy pursue pain management for her headaches.

In late November 2023, Amy Mills reported delays affecting the MRI process. She explained that the MRI machine was down and would need to be fixed, with additional rescheduling expected due to the backlog of MRI orders. She also noted that she had been in bed with a debilitating migraine. Aaron Whaley acknowledged the situation in early December, expressing sympathy and asking Amy to let him know when she was rescheduled.

Throughout December 2023, and into January 2024, the thread continued with follow-up about MRI scheduling. On December 29, 2023, Aaron asked whether the MRI had been rescheduled. On January 3, 2024, he emphasized the need for the MRI to be done so he could forward the information to an expert to assess whether Amy might need a revision surgery and to determine whether the neck could be contributing to her headaches. He noted that delaying further could worsen the situation at trial and stressed the goal of obtaining information to present a stronger position to the defense. He also indicated that the longer the delay, the harder it would be to reach favorable trial numbers.

On January 4, 2024, Amy replied that the MRI was scheduled for Saturday, January 6, 2024. She expressed strong desire to have the MRI done and described the ongoing suffering from the injury. She also provided context about other health and financial stresses: a botched root canal, with the filling having deteriorated and the tooth exposed, and the unsuitability of extraction due to her neck injury, implying the tooth would need surgical removal. She commented on the financial aspect of the case, noting that four months of 2019 before the injury were worth more than the annual income estimate, and that tax returns reflected this despite a loss from not working the rest of the year. She mentioned that her husband Jason had already made an offer and, in her view, more than that as a long-standing owner/operator truck driver. She contrasted his claimed Kentucky hazard-pay salary of $84,000 with her perception that his actual work involves approximately one contract per month. She described the medical-legal issues as including medical malpractice for the failure to diagnose and prolonged treatment from the initial ER visit, a traumatic brain injury (TBI), and a possible failed spinal fusion. She also noted that, during back-and-forth negotiations, the defense’s attorney would not come back with a higher offer, which she suspected would concern licensing and insurance boards.

On January 11, 2024, Aaron Whaley followed up to ask whether Amy was able to obtain the MRI on January 6. He reiterated the importance of having the MRI results for expert review and potential revision surgery considerations, as well as to inform the treatment plan for headaches and the overall case numbers for trial.

On January 16, 2024, Aaron again checked in, asking if Amy had been able to get the MRI.

On January 18, 2024, Amy responded that she did indeed obtain the MRI. She stated that the medical report should be available by now. However, due to weather conditions involving snow and ice, she had not yet scheduled the referral appointment. She noted that she had a direct contact she was dealing with regarding that referral as well.

On January 31, 2024, Aaron asked Amy whether her doctor visit had been rescheduled.

Finally, on February 5, 2024, Aaron followed up once more, asking Amy to confirm whether the doctor visit had been rescheduled, indicating that this remains a current issue in the case.

item_291.note: Case: Amy Mills (Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-1398]). Client: Amy Mills.

This narrative summarizes the medical-legal communications between Amy Mills and attorney Aaron Whaley regarding Dr. Huff’s meeting, referrals, and MRI scheduling from October 2023 through January 2024. In late October 2023, after a meeting with Dr. Huff, Whaley informed Mills that Dr. Huff had referred her to Dr. Hunt for a neck evaluation and that she needed a new cervical MRI first. Dr. Huff also recommended that Mills see someone for pain management for her headaches, and Whaley advised her to call and follow up with the referral.

Over the following months, the scheduling of the MRI and related appointments became a recurring topic. On November 10, 2023, Whaley checked in with Mills to see if she had been able to obtain the MRI. By November 28, Mills replied that the MRI machine had been down and there was a backlog; she had also been bedridden for over a week with a terrible debilitating migraine, which contributed to delays in responding. On December 5, 2023, Whaley expressed sympathy for her situation and asked to be informed when she was rescheduled. Mills responded on December 8, 2023, that she would let him know. On December 29, 2023, Whaley followed up again, asking if the MRI had been rescheduled.

In early January 2024, Whaley pressed for progress, noting that the MRI needed to be completed so he could forward the results to an expert to determine whether a revision surgery might be required and to assess whether the neck could be contributing to her headaches. He stressed that the longer they waited, the worse the situation could be for trial purposes, and that obtaining the information was necessary to reach the numbers Mills desired in the case.

On January 4, 2024, Mills replied that she had been called the previous day to schedule the MRI and that it was scheduled for Saturday, January 6, 2024. She emphasized her strong desire to have the MRI done, noting she has been in great pain from the injury and cannot rush the process without harming herself; she added that there hadn’t been a Christmas for her kids because of these issues. Mills also described a dental issue: a botched root canal in which the filling has completely given way, leaving the tooth exposed, and she stated that extraction is not an option due to her neck injury, so the tooth would need to be surgically removed. She criticized the settlement offer being offered, arguing that the 4 months of 2019 before her injury were twice as much (plus) as the annual income estimate; she cited tax returns showing this, but noted that the year was still a loss because she could not work for the remainder of the year. She also alleged medical malpractice, referencing a failure to diagnose and prolonged ER treatment, a traumatic brain injury (TBI), and a possible failed spinal fusion. Regarding the negotiations, she stated that the defense’s lawyer would not go back with a higher offer, which she believed would be disapproved by licensing and insurance boards. She added a side note that if forecasted snow appeared, she might not attend the scheduled appointment due to safety concerns, describing it as PTSD or anxiety and stating she could not risk further harm. Mills concluded by promising to keep Whaley posted.

Subsequent messages show ongoing inquiry about the MRI’s progress. On January 11, 2024, Whaley asked whether the MRI had been completed on January 6. He reiterated the importance of the MRI for moving forward with expert review and trial strategy. On January 16, 2024, he again asked Mills whether she had been able to obtain the MRI. On January 18, 2024, Mills replied that she believed the MRI had occurred and that the MRI report should be available by now, but because of weather (snow and ice), she had not scheduled the referral appointment yet; she also noted that she had a direct contact she had been dealing with regarding the matter. Finally, on January 31, 2024, Whaley followed up with the question: Did your doctor visit get rescheduled?

item_292.last_activity: 2024-01-18T00:00:00
item_292.note: Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-1582]
Client: Amy Mills

This record comprises a chain of email communications between Amy Mills and Aaron Whaley of the Whaley Law Firm concerning medical evaluation after the Dr. Huff meeting, scheduling of an MRI and referral appointments, and related settlement and case issues, spanning from October 24, 2023 through January 18, 2024.

In late October 2023, Aaron Whaley sent a note following a meeting with Dr. Huff. Dr. Huff’s note stated that Amy Mills should be evaluated by Dr. Hunt for a neck assessment, and that she should obtain a new cervical MRI first. He also recommended that Amy see a pain-management specialist for her headaches. Aaron attached Dr. Huff’s note and asked Amy to confirm receipt and to proceed with the recommended steps. Amy later indicated she understood the plan and the references to the referral and MRI.

On November 28, 2023, Amy reported a delay in scheduling the MRI due to issues with the MRI machine being down and a backlog that would push December scheduling. She explained she had been dealing with a debilitating migraine and had not yet been able to respond fully, emphasizing the impact of the delays on moving the case forward. Aaron acknowledged the situation and encouraged Amy to let him know when she was rescheduled.

Throughout December 2023, the exchange continued as scheduling remained uncertain. On December 5, 2023, Aaron asked Amy to inform him when the MRI could be rescheduled. On December 8, 2023, Amy replied that she would let him know. Aaron followed up again on December 29, 2023, asking whether the MRI had been rescheduled.

In early January 2024, the communications focused on whether the MRI had been rescheduled and the strategic importance of obtaining the MRI. On January 3, 2024, Aaron stressed that the MRI needed to be completed so the results could be forwarded to an expert to determine if a revision neck surgery might be necessary and to assess whether neck issues were contributing to Amy’s headaches. He also noted that delaying information would worsen the case if it went to trial and that advancing this information could help secure a better trial outcome.

On January 4, 2024, Amy provided a detailed update reflecting the MRI scheduling status and a number of personal and case-related concerns. She stated the MRI was scheduled for Saturday, January 6, 2024, and expressed strong personal motivation to have the procedure done promptly due to ongoing pain and the desire to resolve the medical issues. She also described additional health challenges: a botched root canal with a filling that had failed, leaving her tooth exposed, with extraction not being an option because of her neck injury and related issues, thus requiring surgical removal. She criticized the insurance settlement offers as insulting, noting that four months of income in 2019 before the injury was at least twice the estimated annual income; tax returns corroborated the higher earnings prior to the injury, while the overall year was a loss due to not working. She contrasted Jason’s claimed salary for hazard-pay roles in Kentucky—citing an online figure of $84,000—with her understanding that he only performed one contract per month. She described medical-legal concerns including alleged medical malfeasance (failure to diagnose and prolonged ER treatment), a traumatic brain injury (TBI), and a possible failed spinal fusion. She also criticized the opposing side’s attorney for not revising a higher offer, suggesting that such conduct could draw the attention of licensing and insurance boards. Finally, she noted a safety-related warning: if forecasted snow materialized, she would not attend the Saturday appointment due to safety concerns, labeling it as possibly PTSD or anxiety and stating she could not risk future harm. She pledged to keep Aaron updated.

On January 11, 2024, Aaron asked Amy whether she was able to obtain the MRI dated for January 6, to enable him to forward the results to an expert and to assess the need for potentially revising the surgery plan and evaluating whether the neck is contributing to headaches. He underscored the urgency given trial timelines.

On January 16, 2024, Aaron again checked in, asking if Amy had been able to obtain the MRI.

On January 18, 2024, Amy replied that she did obtain the MRI, and that the medical report should be in the possession of the involved parties by now. She explained that due to snow and ice, she had not yet scheduled the referral appointment, but she had a direct contact with whom she had been dealing on the matter.

That same day, Aaron responded, “Great! Let me know when it gets scheduled,” indicating continued coordination on the referral and next steps.

In summary, this record documents: (1) Dr. Huff’s note recommending a neck evaluation by Dr. Hunt and a new cervical MRI, plus pain-management for headaches; (2) ongoing MRI scheduling delays and weather-related postponements from late 2023 into January 2024; (3) a detailed January 4, 2024 email from Amy describing health issues (botched root canal with exposed tooth requiring possible surgical removal due to neck injury), the controversial settlement offers and income history, alleged medical malfeasance (ER treatment failures, TBI, possible failed spinal fusion), and safety concerns about travel due to weather; (4) multiple efforts by Aaron Whaley to obtain MRI results and push for a timely medical-and-legal evaluation; and (5) the MRI ultimately taking place on January 6, 2024, with the report to follow, though referral scheduling remained pending due to weather.

item_293.note: Case: Amy Mills – Premises file dated [DOB-1580]. This narrative summarizes the email exchanges and accompanying notes related to Amy Mills, Dr. Huff, Dr. Hunt, MRI scheduling, neck evaluation, pain management, and associated settlement discussions, covering communications from October 2023 through January 2024.

In the brief notes surrounding Dr. Huff’s meeting, attorney Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm communicated with Amy Mills (and copies to other recipients) to provide updates and coordinate next steps. The exchange began with a note dated October 24, 2023, in which Whaley advised that Dr. Huff had referred Amy to Dr. Hunt for a neck evaluation and that she should obtain a new cervical MRI first. He also recommended that Amy see a pain management professional for her headaches and asked her to confirm receipt of the notice and to raise any questions she had.

Amy responded later on October 24, 2023, saying she would print the note and discuss it with her primary physician, and she thanked Whaley for the information. The thread continued with a follow-up from Whaley on November 10, 2023, asking whether Amy had scheduled that appointment.

On November 28, 2023, Amy explained that the MRI machine was down, and there was a backlog for rescheduling; she also described being bedridden for over a week due to a severe migraine. In her message, she noted that a December reschedule had been communicated and that she had continued to experience substantial symptoms.

Whaley responded on December 5, 2023, expressing sympathy for what Amy was going through and inviting her to inform him when she was able to reschedule. Amy replied on December 8, 2023, that she would let him know. A follow-up from Whaley on December 29, 2023 asked whether the MRI had been rescheduled, extending hope for movement on scheduling during the holiday period.

As January 2024 approached, Whaley pressed the matter again. In an exchange dated January 3, 2024, he emphasized the need for the MRI to be completed so he could forward the results to an expert. The goal was to determine whether Amy would require a revision surgery and to assess whether the neck might be contributing to her headaches. He noted that delaying the MRI could hinder obtaining favorable settlement figures by trial, stressing that progress toward trial depended on having this information.

Amy replied on January 4, 2024 with a detailed message concerning the upcoming MRI scheduled for Saturday, January 6, 2024. She stated that she hoped to have the MRI done but was unwilling to rush it, given the ongoing agony from her injury and the impact on her family—specifically mentioning that there had not been a Christmas for her children due to her condition. She also described a botched root canal, where the filling had failed and the tooth was exposed; she explained that extraction was not an option because of her neck injury and related issues, so surgical removal would be necessary. 

Additionally, Amy vented about the financial aspects of the case. She claimed that the four months of 2019 before her injury were worth more than the annual income estimate used for settlement discussions, noting that tax returns showed a loss from not working for the rest of the year. She asserted that Jason, described as an owner/operator truck driver involved in the case for over four years, had offered compensation that she viewed as inadequate. She claimed that Jason’s salary, described as specialized hazard pay for Kentucky, was stated as $84,000, but she contended that in reality he earned that amount by executing one contract per month. Amy then itemized several grounds for medical malpractice: failure to diagnose and prolonged treatment from the ER, traumatic brain injury (TBI), and a possible failed spinal fusion. 

She also criticized the negotiation process, noting that the defense’s lawyer would not revisit a higher offer, which she believed would concern licensing and insurance boards. As a side note about the scheduled Saturday appointment, she warned that if forecasted snow occurred, she would not attend due to safety concerns, describing it as PTSD or anxiety and expressing a need to protect her safety and reduce risk of future harm. She concluded by saying she would keep Whaley posted.

Following this, Whaley sent subsequent inquiries. On January 11, 2024, he asked Amy whether she had been able to obtain the MRI on January 6. Then, on January 16, 2024, he again checked in, asking if she had managed to get the MRI.

Across these exchanges, the thread repeatedly highlighted the central issues: Dr. Huff’s meeting and the referral to Dr. Hunt for neck evaluation, the necessity of a new cervical MRI before any further treatment decisions, the consideration of pain management for headaches, the medical and economic arguments underpinning settlement positions, and the practical barriers to obtaining the MRI, including equipment downtime, scheduling backlogs, health concerns, and safety considerations for winter weather. The communications reflect an ongoing effort to obtain the MRI results and to move the case toward a resolution while addressing Amy Mills’s substantial medical and personal burdens.

item_295.note: Case: Amy Mills – Amy Mills (Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-1572]). Client: Amy Mills.

On January 12, 2024, at 12:04:25 PM Eastern Time, Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm correspondence to Betsy Catron regarding Amy Mills. In his email, he confirms that the physician Dr. Huff wanted Amy to see was Dr. Paul McCombs, who, however, is retired. Dr. Huff then referred Amy to a spine orthopedic specialist within Bluegrass Orthopaedics. Amy Mills had an updated MRI on January 6, and the results were still pending at the time of the note. Aaron’s message was cc’d to Sarena Tuttle and Amy Mills herself, and it included contact details for Aaron Whaley at Whaley Law Firm.

Earlier that same day, around noon, Betsy Catron—an attorney at Vine Center in Lexington, Kentucky— replied to Aaron. She stated that they were still working on obtaining all of Ms. Mills’s records from Bluegrass Orthopaedics and PT Pros. She asked whether Amy Mills had presented to the Nashville clinic to which she had been referred, and if so, she requested details of the provider. Betsy noted that the Bluegrass Orthopaedics record refers to Dr. Paul McCombs in Nashville, but that Dr. McCombs appears to be retired; she added that he was previously associated with the Howell Allen clinic. The message concluded with thanks and Betsy’s standard signature block.

For reference, Aaron Whaley’s email signature identifies his firm as Whaley Law Firm, located at 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222, with the phone number [PHONE-1412]. Betsy Catron’s signature provides her affiliation with Vine Center, 333 West Vine Street, Suite 1100, Lexington, KY 40507, and the office contact numbers [PHONE-1413] (phone) and [PHONE-1414] (fax).

item_296.note: This is a narrative summary of the Amy Mills case (Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-1567]), focusing on the correspondence between Amy Mills and attorney Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm regarding medical imaging, neck evaluation, head- and neck-related symptoms, and settlement dynamics following a personal injury.

On January 11, 2024, Aaron Whaley emailed the recipient(s) about whether Amy had been able to obtain an MRI on January 6. In the preceding exchanges, Amy Mills had written on January 4, 2024 that the MRI had been scheduled for Saturday, January 6, 2024. She expressed strong personal urgency and frustration, noting that she had been dealing with severe agony from the injury and could not rush the process. She stated there had been no Christmas for her kids due to her condition. She also described a botched root canal—specifically that the filling had failed and the tooth was exposed—and that extraction could not be performed because of her neck injury and related issues, necessitating surgical removal. 

Amy also commented on the financial aspects of the case. She described the compensation offer as insulting and noted that the four months of 2019 before she was hurt were twice as much, plus more than the annual income estimate; she asserted the tax returns showed this, but the overall impact was a loss from not working for the rest of the year. She claimed that Jason had already made an offer, and that he earns as an owner/operator truck driver for the four-plus years the case had been ongoing. She asserted that a quick search suggested his specialized hazard-pay salary in Kentucky was $84,000, but, in reality, he performed only one contract per month, which she implied equated to the same figure.

Amy identified several medical- malpractice concerns, including a failure to diagnose and prolonged treatment from the initial ER visit, a traumatic brain injury (TBI), and a possible failed spinal fusion. During their ongoing negotiations, she noted that the defense’s lawyer would not even consider returning with a higher offer, suggesting that such behavior could be frowned upon by licensing and insurance boards.

As a side note regarding the scheduled Saturday appointment, Amy warned that if the forecasted snow materialized, she would not attend the appointment due to safety concerns. She attributed this caution to PTSD or anxiety, emphasizing she could not risk potential future harm, and she pledged to keep Whaley posted.

In a January 3, 2024 message, Aaron asked whether the MRI had been rescheduled, explaining that obtaining the MRI was necessary so he could send the results to an expert to determine whether Amy needed a revision surgery and to assess whether the neck might be causing her headaches. He emphasized that ruling out a neck-related cause for the headaches was important to proceed with treatment for the headaches and to obtain expert opinions. He also noted that having this information was essential to determine the financial numbers sought in the case and that delaying the process could worsen the trial position.

Earlier communications included a December 29, 2023 email in which Aaron asked Amy if the MRI had been rescheduled, and a December 8, 2023 reply in which Amy said she would let him know. On December 5, 2023, Aaron expressed sympathy and requested that Amy inform him when she was rescheduled. In a November 28, 2023 message, Amy explained that the MRI had been scheduled but the machine went down, followed by another reschedule request due to backlog; she noted she had been sidelined by a debilitating migraine and bedridden for over a week, which contributed to the delayed reply. On November 10, 2023, Aaron checked in again to see if Amy had scheduled the appointment. On October 24, 2023, Amy acknowledged receipt of the note and said she would print it and discuss it with her primary physician to plan further steps. Earlier that same day, October 24, 2023 at 4:11 PM, Aaron reported that he had met with Dr. Huff; he attached the note Dr. Huff wrote after the meeting, which indicated a referral to Dr. Hunt for a neck evaluation and to obtain a new cervical MRI first, with a recommendation to pursue pain management for headaches. He instructed Amy to call and follow up about the MRI, and to let him know if she had questions and that she received the information.

The review of the threaded correspondence also includes standard practice details: the Whaley Law Firm’s contact information appears at the end of the exchange, including the firm’s address—712 Lyndon Ln, Louisville, KY 40222—and phone number, [PHONE-1417].

item_297.note: In the case file titled Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-1564], involving client Amy Mills, the notes describe communications surrounding a Dr. Huff meeting and subsequent medical-legal actions. On January 3, 2024, the attorney Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm sent an email to Amy Mills and the Bella Beauty Institute, with Amy copied, asking whether the MRI had been rescheduled. He stated that the MRI needed to be completed so that he could send the results to an expert to determine if revision surgery might be necessary and to assess whether a neck issue could be causing Amy’s headaches. If the neck could be ruled out as the source of the headaches, they could move on to treatment for the headaches, and experts could offer an opinion. He noted that obtaining this information was necessary in order to reach the financial figures sought in the case. He also warned that the longer they waited, the worse things could be if the case went to trial, and he needed the information to push the defense toward trial and improve the outcome.

The email trail shows a back-and-forth regarding the MRI rescheduling. On Friday, December 29, 2023, at 2:44 PM, Aaron wrote: “Amy, hope you had a good Christmas. Has the MRI been rescheduled?” Prior to that, on Friday, December 8, 2023 at 8:03 AM, Amy Mills replied, “Ok, I will let you know.” Earlier, on Tuesday, December 5, 2023 at 2:23 PM, Aaron had written, “I’m sorry to hear that. Let me know when you get rescheduled.” And on Tuesday, November 28, 2023 at 3:45 PM, Amy Mills replied: “We did but then the machine was down for the MRI. It was to be fixed and then I got another call for it to be rescheduled. I was told it would be for sometime in December with the backlog of MRI orders. I have been down and in bed for over a week with a terrible debilitating migraine. This is the reason for the long awaited reply.”

The timeline also shows prior outreach: on Friday, November 10, 2023 at 12:57 PM, Aaron asked Amy if she had made the appointment yet. On Tuesday, October 24, 2023 at 8:42 PM, Amy Mills wrote that she had received the note and planned to print it and make an appointment with her primary physician to discuss next steps; she thanked Aaron. Earlier that same day, at 4:11 PM, Aaron informed Amy that he had just met with Dr. Huff and attached the note from that meeting. Dr. Huff had referred Amy to another clinician at the same clinic, Dr. Hunt, for an evaluation of her neck. He instructed that Amy should obtain a new cervical MRI first and follow up with that appointment, and he also recommended that she see someone for pain management for her headaches. He asked Amy to let him know if she had any questions and to confirm receipt of the message.

In summary, the communications chronicle the plan to obtain a new cervical MRI, to evaluate the neck’s potential role in Amy’s headaches through a referral to Dr. Hunt, and to pursue pain management for the headaches, all in the context of advancing the case with the necessary expert opinions and trial readiness. The MRI’s scheduling, the machine’s downtime, and Amy’s health episodes (including a prolonged debilitating migraine) contributed to delays in obtaining the needed information for the case’s valuation and potential trial strategy.

Client: Amy Mills

Re: Dr. Huff meeting

On December 29, 2023, Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm emailed Amy Mills regarding the Dr. Huff meeting. In the message, he wrote to Amy Mills and included the Bellabeautyinstitute email address in the To field, with a CC to [EMAIL-1422], and dated 14:44:43 -0500. The opening line asked, “Amy, hope you had a good Christmas. Has the MRI been rescheduled?”

This email thread traces back through several prior messages. On December 8, 2023 at approximately 8:03 AM, Amy Mills replied, “Ok, I will let you know.” Earlier, on December 5, 2023 at 2:23 PM, Aaron Whaley replied, “I’m sorry to hear that. Let me know when you get rescheduled.”

Before that, on November 28, 2023 at about 3:45 PM, Amy Mills explained that they had attempted scheduling but the MRI machine was down. She noted it would be fixed and then rescheduled, with an expectation that the appointment would occur sometime in December given the backlog of MRI orders. She also stated that she had been “down and in bed for over a week with a terrible debilitating migraine,” which accounted for the delay in replying.

Earlier still, on November 10, 2023 at 12:57 PM, Aaron Whaley wrote to Amy Mills, seeking to confirm whether she had made the appointment yet.

On October 24, 2023 at 8:42 PM, Amy Mills responded that she had received the message, would print it, and would make an appointment with her primary physician to discuss further steps. She concluded with, “Thank You, Amy Mills.”

Earlier that same day, at 4:11 PM, Aaron Whaley wrote to Amy Mills: he had just met with Dr. Huff and attached the note Dr. Huff made after the meeting. Dr. Huff had referred Amy to Dr. Hunt, another doctor at the clinic, for a neck evaluation. Dr. Huff wanted Amy to obtain a new cervical MRI first, so she would need to call and follow up regarding that MRI. He also recommended that Amy see a specialist for pain management for her headaches. Whaley invited Amy to reach out with any questions and to confirm that she received the message.

The thread then included multiple reiterations of Aaron Whaley’s and Amy Mills’s communications, followed by the standard firm signature blocks for Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm, including his full contact information at 712 Lyndon Ln, Louisville, KY 40222, phone number [PHONE-1423].

item_300.note: Case: Amy Mills – Premise [DOB-1428]. Client: Amy Mills.

In late October 2023, the Whaley Law Firm began coordinating medical steps for Amy Mills following a Dr. Huff meeting. On October 24, 2023 at 4:11 PM, Aaron Whaley wrote to Amy Mills (and to others) after his meeting with Dr. Huff. He said he had met with Dr. Huff and attached the note Dr. Huff wrote after the meeting. Dr. Huff had referred Amy to Dr. Hunt at the same clinic for an evaluation of her neck, and he wanted Amy to obtain a new cervical MRI first, so she would need to call and follow up about that. Dr. Huff also recommended that Amy see someone for pain management to address her headaches. Whaley invited Amy to ask any questions and to confirm that she received the note.

That same day, October 24, 2023, at 8:42 PM, Amy Mills replied that she had received the note, would print it, and would make an appointment with her primary physician to discuss it as well as to take further steps forward. She thanked Whaley for the information.

On November 10, 2023, at 12:57 PM, Whaley sent a follow-up message to Amy Mills, checking in to see whether she had made that appointment yet.

Then, on November 28, 2023, at about 3:45 PM, Amy Mills wrote that they had attempted to proceed but encountered a problem: the MRI machine was down, and after it was repaired she received another call to reschedule. She was told the MRI would be scheduled sometime in December due to the backlog of MRI orders. Amy also stated that she had been down and in bed for over a week with a terrible, debilitating migraine, which explained the delayed reply.

Finally, on December 5, 2023 at 2:22 PM (14:22:54 -0500), Aaron Whaley responded: “I’m sorry to hear that. Let me know when you get rescheduled.” The email exchange demonstrates the plan Dr. Huff laid out—referral to Dr. Hunt for neck evaluation, the need for a new cervical MRI, and consideration of pain management for headaches—along with ongoing efforts to coordinate appointments despite MRI machine downtime and Amy’s migraine-related illness. The communications include the sender and recipient details, with Whaley’s Whaley Law Firm sign-off at 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222, and the firm’s phone number, [PHONE-1429].

item_303.note: This case note concerns client Amy Mills (Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-1543]). The file documents communications about a Dr. Huff meeting and related referrals. On November 10, 2023, at 12:57:25 PM Eastern, Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm sent an email titled “Re: Dr. Huff meeting” to the Bellabeautyinstitute email and to Amy Mills (two recipient addresses: [EMAIL-1441] and [EMAIL-1442]). In that message, he checked in to see whether Amy had scheduled the requested appointment yet.

The preceding exchanges began on October 24, 2023. At 4:11 PM that day, Aaron Whaley wrote that he had just met with Dr. Huff and attached the note from that meeting. He explained that Dr. Huff had referred Amy to another clinic physician, Dr. Hunt, for an evaluation of her neck. Dr. Huff wanted Amy to obtain a new cervical MRI first, and she would need to call and follow up to arrange that MRI. Whaley also recommended that Amy see a pain-management specialist for her headaches. He concluded by inviting any questions and asking that Amy confirm she had received the message.

Later the same day, at 8:42 PM (text in the thread reads “8:42?PM”), Amy Mills responded that she had received the information and would print it and make an appointment with her primary physician to discuss it as well as taking further steps forward. She signed off with thanks: “Thank You, Amy Mills.”

The thread includes the full contact details for Aaron Whaley and his law firm: Whaley Law Firm, 712 Lyndon Ln, Louisville, KY 40222, phone [PHONE-1540]. The messages referenced the Dr. Huff meeting, the referral to Dr. Hunt for neck evaluation, the recommendation for a new cervical MRI, and the suggestion to pursue pain management for headaches.

item_309.note: Case: Amy Mills; Premise date: April 26, 2019.

On April 26, 2019, I met with Dr. Huff, who believes Amy Mills’ problem is in her neck rather than her shoulder. Amy Mills prefers disc replacement over fusion due to her age, specifically a Mobi-C cervical disc replacement. She was referred to Dr. Hunt at Bluegrass Orthopaedics for a neck consult. Additionally, Dr. Huff referred her to pain management for headaches. I emailed Amy Mills the note from the doctor.

item_313.note: Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-1475]. Client: Amy Mills.

On October 24, 2023, at 4:11 p.m. Eastern Time, Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm sent an email to Amy Mills at multiple addresses ([EMAIL-1476], [EMAIL-1477], and [EMAIL-1478]) and to the attorney’s other contacts. He stated that he had just met with Dr. Huff and that he attached the note Dr. Huff prepared after the meeting. Dr. Huff has referred Amy to another doctor at the same clinic, Dr. Hunt, for an evaluation of her neck. Dr. Huff wants Amy to obtain a new cervical MRI first, and he instructed her to call and follow up about that MRI. He also recommended that Amy see a provider for pain management for her headaches. Whaley closed the message by asking Amy to let him know if she has any questions and to confirm that she received the message.

For reference, the message was sent from Aaron Whaley, Whaley Law Firm, 712 Lyndon Ln, Louisville, KY 40222, [PHONE-1479], [EMAIL-1480].

item_314.note: Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-1482]. Client: Amy Mills.

On October 20, 2023, at 16:46:55 Eastern Time, Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm emailed Amy Mills and two additional contacts—[EMAIL-1483] and [EMAIL-1484]—from [EMAIL-1485] regarding migraine information. In the message, he stated that the doctor at Norton Neurology Services is Dr. Alsorogi. He referenced another client he had discussed who had undergone a cervical fusion like Amy and subsequently experienced persistent, intractable migraines. For that client, what ultimately worked best was a medication called Aimovig (140 mg/mL), described as a once-monthly injection. The client was also prescribed Maxalt to take as needed if a migraine started. He provided a link to Aimovig’s website: https://www.aimovig.com/.

The email was signed by Aaron Whaley of the Whaley Law Firm, located at 712 Lyndon Ln, Louisville, KY 40222, with the phone number [PHONE-1497].

item_318.note: In October 2023, in the matter of Amy Mills (Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-1503]), communications were exchanged between Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm and Anna Mercer, the clinical assistant to Dr. Wallace Huff at Bluegrass Orthopaedics, regarding arranging a telephone conference for Amy Mills. The parties discussed the structure and timing of the session, with Bluegrass noting that a telephone conference costs $1,000 per 30 minutes and that these conferences are typically held on Tuesday afternoons. On October 6, 2023, at 9:36 AM, Anna Mercer stated that they could arrange a phone call with Dr. Huff and that the conference would be scheduled on a Tuesday afternoon. Aaron Whaley responded later that day, at 12:45 PM, saying he was free that Tuesday if it worked.

Dr. Huff’s availability was then proposed for two possible dates: on October 6, 2023, at 8:29 AM, Anna Mercer suggested [DOB-1504] at 3:45 PM or [DOB-1505] at 3:30 PM and asked which date would work, promising to send the invoice once chosen. On October 16, 2023, at 10:13 AM, Aaron Whaley replied that [DOB-1506] works for him. At 10:40 AM that same day, Anna confirmed she had scheduled the telephone conference for [DOB-1507] at 3:45 PM and asked for a fax number to send the invoice, noting that payment would need to be received 48 business hours before the conference to avoid cancellation or postponement. At 10:44 AM, Aaron provided a contact number, [PHONE-1508], in response.

The invoice was subsequently faxed. On October 17, 2023, at 11:07 AM, Anna Mercer wrote, “Thank you! I faxed the invoice yesterday.” Later that day, at 12:43:51 PM, Aaron Whaley replied, “I received it, and the check has gone out.” The thread shows that the payment was being handled via a mailed check following the invoicing process.

Throughout the correspondence, standard confidentiality notices were included in each message, reflecting the Electronic Communications Privacy Act (18 U.S.C. sections 2510-2521). The emails also contained the contact information for the parties: Anna Mercer as the Clinical Assistant to Dr. Wallace Huff of Bluegrass Orthopaedics, located at 3401 Yorkshire Medical Park, Lexington, KY 40509, with the office phone number [PHONE-1509], Ext. 226; and Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm, located at 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222, with the phone number [PHONE-1510]. The signatures also listed websites and social media handles for Bluegrass Orthopaedics and related details.

In sum, the parties arranged a [DOB-1511] telephone conference with Dr. Huff at 3:45 PM, agreed on a fee structure of $1,000 per 30 minutes, and completed invoicing and payment steps with the invoice faxed on or around October 16, 2023 and the check issued after the receipt of the faxed invoice, with confidentiality notices appended to every message.
