item_432.note: This note documents the communications surrounding Amy Mills’ personal injury claim and a new Information Request from The Whaley Law Firm, dated early May 2023, with the case reference Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-1969].

On May 4, 2023 at 10:53 a.m. (the email from Sarena Tuttle, the Whaley Law Firm’s Litigation Paralegal and Workers’ Compensation Case Manager, sent to Amy Mills and to the firm’s additional recipients), Sarena opened with a cordial greeting to Amy and noted that nothing was attached to the prior message. She acknowledged that not all conditions are visible on the outside and expressed empathy for what Amy was enduring—physically and mentally, emotionally, financially, and spiritually—describing the incident as a life-changing event with lasting impact. Sarena stated they were not trying to create additional difficulty; if there were another way to obtain the information they would pursue it. She explained that while they understand tasks may present challenges and that they would grant as much time as the court allows, they needed to obtain information to resolve the matter. Specifically, the Defense was requesting Amy’s employment history, and the team could not move forward without it, though they understood it might take a couple of days for Amy to gather the information. Sarena proposed breaking down the request: begin with a simple list of places where Amy provided counseling or social work services, and then, later, gather more detailed information the Defense was seeking. She reiterated their willingness to help and support Amy through the process. The message closed with Sarena’s contact details and a note that this was her new email address.

Earlier, Amy Mills had sent a reply on May 4, 2023 at 9:07 a.m. describing the daily challenges she faces, which she said are often overlooked. She explained that the attachment she had prepared took hours to complete due to concentration problems, difficulty with sitting and standing, issues with typing, and blurred or separating words when reading. She shared that her child needed a school excuse and a permission slip signed, and she could not even hold a pen because of numbness, sharp electric “zapps,” and muscle spasms—extending to the extent of needing someone else to write and sign for her. She described these struggles as an ordeal that affected not only herself but also her family, noting that the fatigue and pain were a direct result of the accident. Amy stated that at first glance people might not notice anything, but if someone was with her every second, they would understand. She expressed frustration with inconsistent reports from doctors who reviewed the same scans without her accompanying notes, leading to conflicting diagnoses and questioning how to obtain the correct treatment. She reflected on four years of ongoing issues and their impact on every dimension of her life, wishing the accident had never happened. She thanked Sarena for taking the time to view what she had attached as a way to provide insight into her daily reality. Amy indicated she would send the completed document and would have someone assist her in putting it together as soon as possible. She closed with thanks and her name, Amy Mills.

The thread then moves to earlier correspondence on May 3, 2023. At 3:11 p.m., Sarena replied to Amy, thanking her and apologizing for any frustration and confusion. She signed as before, including her role and contact information.

At 2:35 p.m. on May 3, Amy Mills responded, agreeing to list the requested items and to send something for Sarena to review. She said she would await Sarena’s guidance on whether to change, add, or modify the information, and expressed appreciation to proceed from that point.

At 1:06 p.m. on May 3, Sarena had written that Amy should provide a list of all jobs that fell under the counseling/social worker umbrella, whether internship or paid employment. She indicated that she had reviewed the file and that Aaron Whaley had reviewed emails to Cecilia, and she again requested a simple list of all such positions.

At 12:44 p.m. on May 3, Amy Mills wrote in frustration that she had sent everything multiple times and that emails had been forwarded to Whaley Law Firm staff as her case had seen multiple case managers, but documentation had not been forwarded. She questioned whether she was understanding correctly. She stated that it was a very difficult task after the time that had passed and the circumstances related to the case. Amy discussed what would be accepted versus rejected and suggested this contributed to confusion about how her information—cosmetologist/beautician, social worker, certified counselor/therapist, training and education, or a combination of these—was being categorized. She mentioned sending screenshots as proof of time/date stamps for the information even before a deposit took place and noted that she was attempting to locate saved emails with documents to re-forward. She asserted she had no control over how her information was stored, transferred, or otherwise handled within the law firm, yet reiterated her transparency and willingness to provide any information requested. She recalled having discussed discovery and disclosure with Aaron and emphasized that she sent everything because she did not know what might be important or pertinent to her case, and she questioned how Aaron could be fully aware of what he needed to request if she did not provide all materials. She cited a confusing point from the prior email: the statement that “we do not need educational material,” but the sentence cuts off with “the inte,” indicating that the message ended mid-thought.

Note: The May 3, 2023, 12:44 p.m. email ends abruptly with the words “the inte,” and the remainder of that thought is not included in the record. The record, as provided, preserves all other content and timing from the thread.

item_433.note: Initial client contact in this matter occurred on May 4, 2023, when Amy Mills sent an email to Sarena M. Tuttle regarding her personal injury claim (case reference: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-1971]). In the message, sent from Amy Mills’s [EMAIL-1972], she described a daily reality she feels is often overlooked: the attachment she included had taken her hours to complete because of concentration difficulties, trouble with sit/stand rotation, difficulty typing, and the ability to see and read without the words becoming blurry and separating on the page. She explained that after finishing the attachment, her child needed help with a school excuse for being out sick and a permission slip to sign, and she could not do it. Because she is left-handed and could not hold a pen due to complete numbness, sharp electric zaps, and muscle spasms, she had to enlist someone else to write and sign her name. She stressed that this one task took hours and that the frustration stems from these unspoken struggles that should not be happening, all tied to the accident four years prior. She emphasized that at a casual glance she might appear fine, but unless someone is with her every moment, others cannot understand, and she often tries to hide it because she is tired of being asked if she is okay. She also expressed frustration with medical reports: multiple doctors reviewing the same scans and tests without her input, producing different and sometimes conflicting diagnoses, making it difficult to obtain the correct treatment. She characterized the past four years as a continuous cycle of cause and effect, stating it is “beyond frustrating” and “INSANE” in her reality as she works through these issues daily. She thanked Sarena for viewing the note and described it as a form of clarification and insight into something that should not be difficult to perform, yet is for her due to the condition from the accident. She indicated that she would send the completed document as soon as possible, with help from someone who would assist her in putting it together. The message concluded with her thanks and her name, Amy Mills.

In the same thread, the overarching email correspondence shows that Sarena Tuttle of The Whaley Law Firm acknowledged the message the next day, May 3, 2023, commenting, “Awesome, thank you so much. Again, I apologize for the frustration and confusion.” Sarena’s signature identified her as a Litigation Paralegal and Workers’ Compensation Case Manager, with the firm’s address at 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222, and listed direct and office lines, along with her new email address, Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com.

Earlier on May 3, 2023, there was a sequence of back-and-forth communications. At approximately 11:29 AM, Amy Mills indicated that she planned to list items and send them for review, asking for feedback on any needed changes and noting that the case had changed case managers multiple times within the firm, but documentation had not been forwarded. She referenced that the documentation was initially sent to the firm and suggested there had been confusion over where information was stored within the firm, and she mentioned forwardings to the firm’s current team as the case evolved. She also described confusion arising from the mix of roles—cosmetologist/beautician, social worker, and certified counselor/therapist (training and education), and that these lists might be relevant to her case.

Sarena responded at 11:51 AM, expressing some confusion and clarifying that Celia—the person with whom Amy had previously corresponded—was no longer with the firm, and that she would only accept the requested documents as needed. She asked for a straightforward list of any and all counseling/social-work-related work Amy had engaged in, whether as an internship or paid employment, and she reiterated contact information for the firm.

At 12:44 PM, Amy wrote again to express frustration, noting that she had already sent everything multiple times and that emails had been forwarded within the Whaley firm due to changes in case personnel. She explained that there appeared to be a mix-up between cosmetologist/beautician and social worker or certified counselor/therapist roles, as well as training and education records. She provided context about having shared screenshots as proof of time/date stamps before a deposit, and said she would search saved emails to re-forward the information. Amy also mentioned that she did not control how her information was stored or transferred within the firm, but she had remained transparent and forthcoming about all materials. She referenced discussions with Aaron about discovery and disclosure and explained that she sent everything because she did not know what would be important or pertinent to the case, and she believed that not sharing could prevent others from knowing what to request. She gave an example of a point of confusion: the firm had stated that educational material was not needed, yet internship materials appeared to be exactly that. She reiterated that gathering the information would take time and that she would continue to work on it, adding that the circumstances during the intervening period had been challenging.

Later on May 3, 2023, Sarena replied at 1:06 PM with a clearer directive: she again apologized for the frustration and stated that what was needed at that moment was a list of Amy’s counseling/social-work-related jobs, including internships and paid positions, with dates and details on any compensation. She reminded Amy that she and Aaron had reviewed the file and emails to Celia, and again stressed that the requested documentation was what was needed, not educational material. She included the firm’s contact details in her signature.

Amy’s response at 2:35 PM confirmed her intention to compile the list and send it for review, indicating she would allow Sarena to review the draft and request changes if necessary, and she expressed appreciation for proceeding from there.

Finally, at 3:11 PM on May 3, Sarena wrote a concluding note to Amy, acknowledging that while Amy had said she would attach information, the only items attached were two screenshots of Amy’s emails with Celia. Sarena reiterated that Celia was no longer with the firm and that she was not privy to those conversations. She emphasized that the firm would only accept requested documents as needed and questioned where such information was stored in Amy’s file. She reminded Amy that in her deposition she had described training and education in substance abuse, behavioral and/or mental health counseling, which was the basis for the “new skills” reference. Sarena clarified that Amy’s counseling skills, not her cosmetology background, were relevant to the case. She urged Amy to provide the requested information promptly, noting that it might take a day or two to gather everything, and she asked Amy to start with a resume or list of counseling-related work, including the employers, volunteers, internships, dates, and compensation details, explicitly stating that educational material was not required. The message concluded with Sarena’s signature and updated contact information.

In summary, the case involves Amy Mills seeking information and clarification related to her personal injury claim arising from the 2019 incident, a request for documentation about her counseling- and social-work–related employment, internships, and training, and an ongoing exchange with The Whaley Law Firm to resolve inconsistencies in file handling, roles, and the specific materials required for discovery. Amy’s May 4 email adds a personal account of the daily functional impairments she experiences due to the accident, the impact on her ability to perform ordinary tasks, and her intent to provide a more complete document with assistance as soon as possible.

item_434.note: Case: Amy Mills – Premise [DOB-1974]. This narrative records the communications concerning Amy Mills’ personal injury claim and a new information request issued in May 2023 by The Whaley Law Firm, specifically by Sarena M. Tuttle, Litigation Paralegal and Workers’ Compensation Case Manager, to Amy Mills. The message thread begins with an email dated May 3, 2023 at 11:50 a.m. from Sarena Tuttle to Amy Mills (and to the broader distribution list including Celia’s former contacts). In that note, Sarena explains she is confused because Amy had said she would attach information, but the only attachments were two screenshots of emails with Celia. Sarena notes that Celia is no longer with the firm and she is not privy to any conversations Amy had with Celia, so she will only accept documents as they are needed and cannot find where the information was saved in Amy’s file. Sarena points out that in Amy’s deposition, Amy stated she had training and education in substance abuse, behavioral, and/or mental health counseling, which is what the “new skills” reference means — that the new skills are counselor work rather than beautician/cosmetologist work. Sarena asks Amy to send the requested information as soon as possible, acknowledging it may take a day or two to gather everything. She instructs Amy to begin with a list or resume of any counseling-related work she has done, including dates and the method and amount of compensation. She clarifies that educational materials are not needed; what is required is information about who Amy has worked for, volunteered for, or completed internships with. The message ends with Sarena’s contact details and a note to “PLEASE NOTE NEW EMAIL ADDRESS.”

The email thread continues with a reply from Amy Mills dated May 3, 2023 at 11:29 a.m. in which she expresses remaining confusion about what “new skills” refers to. She explains difficulties in assembling documents because items have been requested multiple times and some materials have been lost or misplaced during changes in who is handling the file. Amy states she found an email she intends to attach that contains daily notes of her work at the Trillium Center, an inpatient and outpatient facility for adolescents and adults, including details about her supervisor, the supervisor’s CV/resume, copies of licensure, scheduled sessions, and activities, among other information related to the requests and prior merit discussions. She notes that whenever she finds documents that may or may not be needed, she has forwarded them to the law office because of memory issues related to a traumatic brain injury/nerve damage from injuries in this case. She emphasizes that she is reviewing saved and sent emails to gather the required information and acknowledges that this material corresponds to one month of what had been required for two years of her master’s curriculum. She indicates she will forward this exact email separately as an attachment and closes with thanks and her name, Amy Mills.

Earlier on the same day, at 10:20 a.m., Sarena Tuttle had written to Amy with a more directive request: there is no defined time frame, and Amy should provide any and all documents related to any and all employment (including internships and volunteering) under the category of substance abuse, behavioral, or mental health counseling. If documentation does not exist, Amy should provide a list of all places where she provided services, with dates and any compensation details. Sarena provides her standard contact information and reiterates the lack of a time frame.

Earlier still, at 9:17 a.m. on May 3, 2023, Amy Mills replied that she did not understand what “new skills” meant. She said she would assemble what she could as soon as possible, though items are in storage and gathering them is hindered by physical limitations. She noted that the course’s required internship curriculum to obtain the diploma was among the items requested and asked about the time frame (e.g., 5, 10, 15 years) for the documents.

On May 1, 2023 at 9:30 a.m., Sarena responded that there is no time frame and reiterated that the request is for employment verification information. She asked Amy to identify places where she has applied, worked, or volunteered using the new skills, and to provide details even if documentation is not available.

The thread also includes prior correspondence from April 28 and April 29, 2023. On April 28, Sarena informed Amy that the office had received a request from the Defense Attorney for Forcht Bank and asked Amy to review the document and provide information related to her applications for employment or participation in a practicum or internship program as a counselor or Counselor Associate, inviting any questions. On April 29, 2023 at 1:30 p.m., Amy stated that the transcripts already show the course work and grades, and a copy of the diploma, and asked whether that documentation is insufficient.

Amy had previously written on April 29, 2023 at 1:30 p.m. that the transcripts from college prove she completed the coursework with grades and that a diploma was received as a result of completing these requirements, and she questioned whether this material was substantial enough.

The correspondence overall centers on obtaining documentation of Amy Mills’ counseling-related employment or practice experiences, including internships and volunteer work, tied to her related skills claimed in the deposition, in support of her personal injury claim. The communications reflect the firm’s need to obtain employment verification or equivalent documentation—past, present, and, if applicable, within the past several years—about where Amy worked, volunteered, or interned in substance abuse, behavioral, or mental health counseling, the absence or presence of compensation, and dates of service. The dialogue also reveals Amy’s difficulties with document retrieval and storage, her use of prior emails as sources of information due to memory concerns arising from a traumatic brain injury, and her ongoing effort to forward relevant materials to the law office.

Signature blocks in the messages identify Sarena M. Tuttle as the sender of the Whaley Law Firm communications, with the firm’s address at 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222; phone numbers D: [PHONE-1975] and O: [PHONE-1976] ext 104; and email address Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com. The correspondence also notes a “Please note new email address” reference in Sarena’s signature. The email chain includes references to Celia (a former firm member) and indicates that Celia’s conversations are not accessible to Sarena. The May 3, 2023 date line is shown in the header of the initial note (11:50 a.m.) as well as subsequent replies within the same day, and earlier messages span from April 28 to April 29, 2023.

item_435.note: Case: Amy Mills – Premise Incident Case Note dated [DOB-1979]
Client: Amy Mills

On May 3, 2023, Amy Mills, the client in a personal injury claim, initiated contact with Sarena M. Tuttle of The Whaley Law Firm regarding a new information request. In her first note, sent in the afternoon, Amy said she would compile a list of items, send them for review, and then ask whether anything needed to be changed or added before proceeding.

Earlier that same day, at about 1:06 p.m., Sarena responded with a focused request. She asked Amy to provide a comprehensive list of all jobs that fall under the counseling or social worker umbrella, including internships and paid positions. Sarena explained that both she and Aaron had reviewed the file and related emails, and she asked that Amy simply send a complete list of all counseling/social work roles she had held, regardless of whether they were volunteer, internship, or paid work. Sarena provided her contact details for The Whaley Law Firm and noted Amy’s new email address.

Earlier still, at roughly 12:44 p.m., Amy replied with visible frustration. She explained that she had already sent the requested information multiple times and that emails had been forwarded to the firm’s current case manager (the case had been reassigned several times within the firm). Amy expressed confusion over what would be accepted as responsive documentation and asserted that there had been mixups between descriptors such as cosmetologist/beautician, social worker, and certified counselor/therapist, including training and education. She claimed to have sent information before the deposit and to have screenshots documenting time and dates as proof. She also noted she was trying to locate other saved emails with documents to re-forward, and she described her lack of control over how information was stored or transferred within the firm. Amy emphasized her transparency and her practice of sending everything to ensure that the team could identify what might be important for discovery or disclosure, including concerns about what material might matter for the case.

Earlier in the day, at about 11:29 a.m., Amy had written to say she would send what she could, noting the ongoing confusion about what constitutes “new skills” and asking to gather documentation from storage. She mentioned an email containing daily notes from work at the Trillium Center, an inpatient and outpatient facility for adolescents and adults, including her supervisor, her supervisor’s CV/Resume, copies of licensure, scheduled sessions, and activities relevant to the request. She described having forwarded any potentially relevant documents to the law office in case they were needed later, citing her traumatic brain injury (TBI) and nerve damage as factors affecting her memory and recall. She indicated she would forward the exact email separately with attachments and thanked the recipient.

At about 11:51 a.m., Sarena replied with some confusion of her own. She noted that the only attachments present were two screenshots of Amy’s emails with a person named Celia. Sarena stated that Celia was no longer with the firm and that she was not privy to the contents or context of conversations with Celia. She explained that she would only accept documents as they were needed and reiterated that the firm might not have those materials saved in Amy’s file. Sarena referenced Amy’s deposition, stating that Amy had testified to receiving training and education in substance abuse, behavioral and/or mental health counseling, and clarified that “new skills” referred to these counseling-related capabilities rather than cosmetology. She urged Amy to send the requested information promptly and suggested starting with a list or resume of any counseling-related work, including dates and compensation, noting that educational material was not required, only information about employers, volunteering, and internships.

Earlier still, at around 10:20 a.m., Amy had written to say there was no defined time frame for the requested information. She asked for any and all documents related to employment—internships, volunteering, etc.—in the area of substance abuse, behavioral or mental health counseling. If documentation was lacking, she asked for a list of every place she had provided services, with dates and any compensation, if applicable.

Finally, at about 9:17 a.m., Amy had sent a morning note indicating that she did not fully understand what “new skills” referred to and that she would assemble what she could as soon as possible, noting that her materials were in storage.

In sum, the day’s exchanges tracing May 3, 2023, show Amy Mills attempting to respond to a formal information request concerning her counseling-related work history, education, internships, and related qualifications, while contending with repeated case-management changes, document-control issues within the firm, and ongoing uncertainties about exactly which materials would be deemed responsive or necessary for discovery and disclosure in her personal injury claim. The Whaley Law Firm’s responses emphasized the need for a clear list of counseling-related employment (including internships and volunteer work) with dates and any compensation, and they clarified that educational materials were not required. The communications also noted administrative changes within the firm (including the departure of a person named Celia) and attempted to align Amy’s documentation with the firm’s information-gathering process. Contact details referenced include The Whaley Law Firm’s address at 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222, and the firm’s phone and email channels for Sarena M. Tuttle, the Litigation Paralegal and Workers’ Compensation Case Manager.

item_436.note: Case: Amy Mills, Premise - [DOB-1981]. Client: Amy Mills. Re: Your Personal Injury Claim – New Request for Information.

On May 3, 2023, The Whaley Law Firm sent a new information request to Amy Mills regarding her personal injury claim. The message came from Sarena M. Tuttle, a litigation paralegal and workers’ compensation case manager at The Whaley Law Firm, located at 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222. Her direct lines were D: [PHONE-1982] and O: [PHONE-1983] ext 104, and her email was Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com, noted as a new address. The email also copied Aaron Whaley and a case-specific email alias for Amy Mills Premise [DOB-1984], and stated the recipient addresses included [EMAIL-1985] and Amy Mills.

The content began with an acknowledgment of thanks and an apology for prior frustration and confusion. The firm signaled that the request was a continuation of their efforts to obtain information pertinent to Amy Mills’ personal injury claim.

Earlier in the day, Amy Mills replied that she would compile a list of items and then allow Sarena to review them, indicating she would determine if anything needed modification or addition before proceeding.

Prior to that, at 10:20 a.m., Sarena had written that there was no fixed time frame and explicitly requested documentation relating to any and all employment under the counseling, behavioral health, or mental health counseling umbrella—whether internships or paid positions. She advised Amy to provide documentation for any and all places where she provided services, with dates and compensation details if any, and to supply a list if documentation was not available.

At 11:29 a.m., Amy asked for clarification about the term “new skills,” expressing uncertainty about what that phrase referred to in the context of the request.

Shortly afterward, at 11:51 a.m., Sarena responded with some confusion of her own, noting that Amy had said she would attach information, but only two screenshots of emails with someone named Celia had been received. Sarena explained that Celia was no longer with the firm and that the team would only accept documents as needed; she referenced Amy’s deposition, which indicated training and education in substance abuse, behavioral, and/or mental health counseling, clarifying that “new skills” referred to Amy’s counseling work rather than cosmetology. Sarena urged Amy to send the requested information promptly, starting with a resume or list of counseling-related work, including dates and compensation, and stated that they did not need educational material but did need information on employers, volunteer work, and internships.

At 12:44 p.m., Amy again expressed frustration, stating she had sent everything multiple times and that her documentation had been forwarded to a different part of the firm due to multiple changes in case managers, but the documentation had not followed along. She described ongoing difficulty with the case filing and what seemed to be a mix-up among roles (cosmetologist/beautician, social worker, and certified counselor/therapist) and training/education. Amy asserted she was attempting to locate saved emails with documents to re-forward and noted she had limited control over how her information was stored or transferred within the firm. She emphasized that she had been transparent and forthcoming and referenced discussions about discovery and disclosure, explaining that she was sending everything because she did not know what would be deemed important or pertinent to the case. Amy suggested the “new skills” issue might relate to the practical and clinical applications required in her educational programs, and she indicated gathering time would be needed to compile relevant material, including internship material, which could be time-consuming.

At 1:06 p.m., Sarena reaffirmed the request, explaining that there was no stated time frame and reiterating the need for any and all documents related to employment in counseling/behavioral or mental health counseling, including internships and positions. She advised that if documentation was unavailable, Amy should still provide a list of places where she provided services, with dates and compensation details if applicable.

Around 2:35 p.m., Amy responded that she would compile a list and send something for review, and that Sarena could indicate whether any items needed to be changed or added, with the understanding that they could proceed from there.

Finally, at 3:11 p.m., Sarena closed the thread with a courteous note: “Awesome, thank you so much. Again, I apologize for the frustration and confusion.” The message included Sarena’s full signature, reiterating her role and contact information, and noting the new email address.

Throughout the thread, Amy’s inbox referenced a note about daily logs from her time at the Trillium Center (an inpatient and outpatient facility for adolescents and adults), including a supervisor, supervisor’s CV/resume, licensure copies, scheduled sessions, and related activities—items she described as pertaining to past discussions of merit. Amy attributed her difficulty in gathering and forwarding documents to the TBI/nerve damage resulting from injuries in her case and to the firm’s changing personnel, which she felt had led to lost or misfiled documentation. She also highlighted a broader issue that the information she sent before had been directed to the wrong place or person within the firm’s file system, leading to several misunderstandings about what material was being requested and what was considered “new skills.”

This exchange illustrates an ongoing process to collect employment-related documentation tied to Amy Mills’ counseling and mental health-related work for purposes related to her personal injury claim, including internships and volunteer work, with an emphasis on concrete details (employers, supervisors, dates, and compensation) rather than solely on educational materials. The firm remained focused on obtaining a comprehensive list and supporting documentation, while Amy continued to navigate internal firm changes and the effects of a traumatic brain injury on her ability to recall and assemble the requested records.

item_437.note: Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-1988]. Client: Amy Mills. Re: Your Personal Injury Claim - New Request for Information. From: Amy Mills to Sarena Tuttle. Date: May 3, 2023, 12:44:32 PM Eastern Time.

Chronology of the email exchange:

On April 29, 2023 at 1:30 PM, Amy Mills emailed to address the new information request related to her personal injury claim. She stated that the law firm already has her transcripts showing coursework and the diploma, and she referenced the materials tied to the request, noting that her transcripts and course work are on file with the college. She indicated that the requested items include documentation from a two-year master’s curriculum and the related diploma, and she suggested that the documentation had already been provided but that it was now being requested again.

On May 1, 2023 at 9:30 AM, Sarena M. Tuttle, a Litigation Paralegal and Workers’ Compensation Case Manager at The Whaley Law Firm, replied that there is no “time frame” for the request and that the firm is seeking employment verification information related to the “new skills” referenced in Amy’s deposition. She explained that the firm needs information about places where Amy has applied, worked, or volunteered using those new skills, rather than educational materials, and she asked Amy to provide a list or resume of any counseling-related work with dates and compensation details, if any. She reiterated the firm’s contact details and noted that Celia, who previously handled the matter, is no longer with the firm and that Sarena was not privy to any prior conversations with Celia.

On May 3, 2023 at 9:17 AM, Amy Mills replied that she was still unclear about what “new skills” referred to. She said she would gather what she could, acknowledging that items are in storage and that her physical capabilities create a need for assistance. She indicated that the requested items include the course-required internship curriculum necessary to obtain her diplomas and asked what time frame was requested for these documents.

On May 3, 2023 at 10:20 AM, Sarena responded, clarifying again that there is no fixed time frame and restating the scope of the request: Amy should provide any and all documents related to employment (including internships, volunteering) under the areas of substance abuse, behavioral health, or mental health counseling. If documentation is not available, Amy should provide a list of places where she provided services, with dates and any compensation.

On May 3, 2023 at 11:29 AM, Amy Mills replied with further details. She thanked Sarena for the reply and admitted continued confusion about the meaning of “new skills.” Amy explained that while she searches through accessible emails to gather documents, she found an email containing daily notes from her work at the Trillium Center (an inpatient and outpatient facility for adolescents and adults), including her supervisor, her supervisor’s CV/resume, copies of licensure, scheduled sessions and activities, and other information relevant to prior discussions of merit. She stated that she has forwarded any documents she could locate because she cannot always recall everything, especially given a traumatic brain injury and nerve damage resulting from the injuries in her case. Amy noted that this effort reflects only one month of what the master’s program required over two years and that she would forward this exact email as an attachment separately. She emphasized her intention to continue gathering and forwarding pertinent documents as requested.

On May 3, 2023 at 11:51 AM, Sarena responded with some confusion and clarification: Amy had stated she was attaching information, but the only attachments were two screenshots of emails with Celia. Sarena reminded that Celia is no longer with the firm and she is not privy to those conversations. She reiterated that she would only accept requested documents as needed and did not see any of this information saved to Amy’s file. Sarena then referenced Amy’s deposition, noting that Amy had testified to receiving training, education, etc., in substance abuse, behavioral and/or mental health counseling, which is what “new skills” referred to. Sarena clarified that Amy’s counseling work, not beautician/cosmetologist work, is the focus. She urged Amy to send the requested information as soon as possible, starting with a list or resume of any counseling-related work she had performed, including dates and the method or amount of compensation. Sarena again stated that educational materials were not required, and she repeated that they needed to know who Amy had worked for, volunteered for, and with whom she completed internships. She closed with the law firm’s contact information and noted the new email address for Sarena.

Finally, on May 3, 2023 at 12:44 PM, Amy Mills sent a closing communication continuing her frustration and detailing several procedural concerns. She stated that she had sent everything multiple times and that the emails she sent were forwarded to the firm’s case manager at (@wlf) because the case had changed case managers several times, yet the documentation had not been forwarded. She asked for confirmation of whether her understanding was correct. Amy described the situation as extremely difficult given the amount of time that has passed and the circumstances directly related to the case. She argued that emails requesting documentation should be filed in her file for present or future use and suggested that the confusion around the mix of roles—cosmetologist/beautician, social worker, and certified counselor/therapist—along with training and education, likely contributed to the misfiling. Amy referenced the email screen shots as proof that she had sent the information repeatedly, noting time/date stamps already captured in those screen shots even before the deposit occurred. She indicated she was attempting to locate any saved documents to re-forward and reiterated that she has little control over how her information is stored or transferred within the law firm. She stated continued transparency about all information. Amy noted that she had previously discussed discovery/disclosure with Aaron, explaining that she sends everything because she does not know what might be important or requested; failing to do so would leave him unaware of what to request. She provided an example: a perceived contradiction in a prior email claiming “we do not need educational material,” while internship material appeared to be exactly that. She speculated on possible reasons for the confusion—what practical and clinical applications were required in her education to obtain degrees and certifications—admitting it would take time to gather the necessary items. She concluded by hoping her clarification helps.

These exchanges collectively document Amy Mills’s attempt to provide information for her personal injury claim, the firm’s attempts to constrain the scope of the requested information to counseling-related employment (as opposed to general educational materials), and Amy’s ongoing effort to reconcile different interpretations of what constitutes “new skills” and what documents are required, along with ongoing concerns about record-keeping, case-management changes, and the accessibility of her materials.

item_438.note: This narrative summarizes a May 3, 2023 email exchange in the personal injury matter for Amy Mills, client of the case labeled Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-1990].

The thread began with an email from Sarena M. Tuttle, a litigation paralegal and workers’ compensation case manager at The Whaley Law Firm, dated Wednesday, May 3, 2023 at 1:06 PM EDT. The message was sent from sarena@whaleylawfirm.com to Amy Mills at [EMAIL-1991], with copies to [EMAIL-1992] (Aaron Whaley) and to Amy Mills Premise file address [EMAIL-1993], as well as to Amy Mills Premise [DOB-1994]. In the body of the email, Sarena apologized for the frustration and stated that all she needed at that time was a list of Amy’s jobs that fall under the counseling/social worker umbrella, whether internships or paid employment. She noted that she had reviewed the file and Aaron had reviewed the emails to Celia, and she requested simply a list of any and all jobs in that umbrella.

Later that day, at 12:44 PM (times indicated as approximate in the thread), Amy Mills replied with considerable frustration. She said she had sent everything she had multiple times and that emails she had sent were forwarded to the firm’s current case manager, but she questioned whether the documentation had been forwarded at all due to multiple changes in case managers. She asked for clarification on whether the information would be accepted and filed in her file for present or possible future use. Amy suggested that the confusion likely originated from the mixing of roles—cosmetologist/beautician, social worker, and certified counselor/therapist—along with training and education, or all of the above. She asserted that the email screenshots she had sent served as proof of time/date stamps, even before any deposit, and that she was attempting to locate saved emails to re-forward the information. She explained she had no control over how her information was stored or transferred within the law firm but emphasized her transparency and willingness to provide any and all information. She also referenced prior discussions with Aaron about discovery/disclosure and stated that she sent everything because she did not know what might be important or pertinent to the case, arguing that without sending everything, Aaron could not be fully aware of what to request. Amy gave an example of a confusing point in the prior correspondence: a statement that “we do not need educational material,” which, in her view, contradicted the internship material she believed to be relevant. She speculated about what practical and clinical applications were required in her educational studies to obtain degrees and certifications on file and acknowledged that gathering the requested information would take time. She closed by hoping the message clarified things.

A follow-up reply from Sarena was sent on the same day at 11:51 AM. She expressed that she was a bit confused and noted that Amy had said she was attaching information, but the only attachments were two screenshots of emails with Celia. Sarena explained that Celia was no longer with the firm and that she was not privy to the contents or context of any conversations Amy had with Celia. She stated she would only accept requested documents as they were needed and did not see where the information had been saved in Amy’s file. Sarena reminded Amy that in Amy’s deposition she had stated receiving training, education, etc., in substance abuse, behavioral and/or mental health counseling, and that “new skills” referred to Amy’s skills as a counselor rather than as a beautician/cosmetologist. She requested that Amy send the requested information as soon as possible and suggested starting with a list/resume of any counseling-related work, including dates and the method or amount of compensation. Sarena emphasized that educational material was not needed and reiterated the need to know who Amy had worked for, volunteered for, or completed internships with. The message carried Sarena’s signature with contact details for The Whaley Law Firm.

At 11:29 AM on May 3, Amy Mills replied again. She indicated she was not sure what “new skills” meant and described the process of combing through emails to save electronic documentation, noting that documentation was being requested multiple times and had been lost or misplaced during staff transitions. She mentioned a specific email containing daily notes of work at the Trillium Center, an inpatient and outpatient facility for adolescents and adults, which included her supervisor, the supervisor’s CV/resume, copies of licensure, scheduled sessions and activities, and other information relevant to the requests and discussions of merit. Amy stated that whenever she found documents that might be needed—even if they might not be—they had been forwarded to the law office due to her difficulty recalling details caused by a traumatic brain injury and nerve damage related to injuries in the case. She explained that the materials she found represented one month of a two-year master’s curriculum and again stated her intention to forward the exact email in attachments. She closed by thanking the recipient.

Earlier in the day, at 10:20 AM, Sarena replied again to Amy, reiterating that there was no defined time frame and instructing Amy to provide any and all documents related to employment (internships, volunteering, etc.) under the domains of substance abuse, behavioral or mental health counseling. She also advised that even if Amy did not have documentation, she should provide a list of all places where services were provided, with dates and any compensation, if applicable, and again reminded Amy to use the format of a list rather than educational material.

At 9:17 AM, Amy Mills had replied to the chain asking for clarification about what “new skills” referred to. She stated she would gather the necessary information as soon as possible, noting that the requested materials were in storage and that she would need assistance due to physical limitations. She explained that the requested items included the course-required curriculum of internships necessary to obtain diplomas and asked about the time frame for the documents (for example, whether 5, 10, or 15 years' worth of materials were being sought). The thread ends with a truncated line from a May 1, 2023 message, reading “On Mon, May 1, 2023 at 9:,” indicating an earlier correspondence before the May 3 exchanges.

Throughout this exchange, the communications repeatedly reference the underlying case management context at The Whaley Law Firm, the evolving list of case managers (including changes in who handles Amy’s file), and the ongoing effort to obtain a precise, comprehensive timeline of Amy Mills’s related counseling and related work experience—whether paid, volunteer, or internship—under the broad umbrella of counseling and mental health services, while navigating questions about what constitutes “educational material” versus practical work experience. The thread also highlights Amy’s perception of administrative fragmentation (switching case managers and incomplete forwarding of documents) and her efforts to supply documentation tied to her training, licensure, and practical work at relevant facilities, including the Trillium Center, in the face of health-related limitations.

item_439.note: Case: Amy Mills – Premise [DOB-1996]. Client: Amy Mills. This narrative summarizes the email thread regarding a new request for information tied to Amy Mills’s personal injury claim, with communications between Amy Mills and Sarena Tuttle of The Whaley Law Firm dated April 28, 2023 through May 3, 2023.

On April 28, 2023 at 10:41 AM, Sarena M. Tuttle, a Litigation Paralegal and Workers’ Compensation Case Manager at The Whaley Law Firm, wrote to Amy Mills. She noted that the defense attorney for Forcht Bank had attached a new request and asked Amy to review the document and provide information related to her applications for employment or participation in a practicum or internship program as a counselor or Counselor Associate. She also requested that, if documentation was unavailable, Amy provide a list of places where she provided services, with dates and any compensation, if applicable. Sarena’s signature included the firm’s address at 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222, with direct and office phone numbers and her new email address.

The following day, April 29, 2023 at 1:30 PM, Amy Mills responded. She stated that the documents include transcripts showing course work and grades, and mentioned having a copy of something accessible via a link that appears to reference completing the requirements (the text includes a garbled portion: “copy of thttps://www.google.com/maps/search/712pleating these requirements”). She asked whether this material was substantial enough to satisfy the request.

On May 1, 2023 at 9:30 AM, Sarena replied again, explaining that the request is for employment verification information—specifically, places where Amy has applied, worked, or volunteered using the new skills. She instructed Amy to provide information about employment or practicum/internship involvement in counseling, including any places where such work occurred, and to include dates and any compensation.

On May 3, 2023 at 9:17 AM, Amy sent another message. She indicated she did not understand what “new skills” referred to and that she would assemble what she could as soon as possible, noting that items are in storage and she would need assistance due to physical limitations. She added that the requested items include the course-required internship curriculum in order to obtain diplomas and asked what time frame was required for the documents (e.g., 5, 10, or 15 years).

Later on May 3, 2023 at 10:20 AM, Sarena replied that there was no specified time frame. She reiterated the need for any and all documents related to employment, internships, or volunteer work under the realms of substance abuse, behavioral, or mental health counseling. She requested that, even in the absence of documentation, Amy provide a list of all places where she provided services, with dates and any compensation.

That same day, May 3, 2023 at 11:28 AM, Amy responded with thanks and further context. She stated she was still unclear about what “new skills” referred to. She described going through emails to gather documentation and noted a separate issue: documentation requested multiple times had been lost or misplaced during transfers. She reported finding an email documenting daily notes of work at the Trillium Center, described as an inpatient and outpatient facility for adolescents and adults. She noted the presence of her supervisor, her supervisor’s CV/Resume, copies of licensure, scheduled sessions and activities, and other information related to this request and past discussions of merit. Amy explained that whenever she encountered documents that may be relevant, she forwarded them to the law office, due to memory difficulties stemming from a traumatic brain injury and nerve damage, depending on the doctor’s assessment. She emphasized that she is attempting to assemble saved and sent emails to gather the requested information and pointed out that one month of the work documented in this material also related to two years of her master’s curriculum, tying back to her initial email indicating the content had already been sent previously. She said she would forward this exact email separately as an attachment and closed with thanks. Her message included the line “(See attachment).”

In sum, the thread shows a back-and-forth in which the firm requested comprehensive employment-related documentation tied to counseling and related services, while Amy sought to clarify the scope and time frame, explained challenges with access to stored documents and memory, and attempted to gather and forward relevant materials, including materials from her experience at the Trillium Center and items related to her master’s curriculum. The emails include multiple signatures, with Sarena M. Tuttle’s contact details at The Whaley Law Firm (712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222; D: [PHONE-1997]; O: [PHONE-1998] ext 104; E: Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com, noted as the new email address).

item_440.note: Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2001]. Client: Amy Mills.

Between April 28 and May 3, 2023, a series of emails concerning a new request for information in Amy Mills’s personal injury claim were exchanged. The correspondence was initiated by Sarena M. Tuttle of The Whaley Law Firm, acting as a litigation paralegal and workers’ compensation case manager, and circulated to Amy Mills and several recipients related to the case.

On April 28, 2023, at 10:41 AM, Sarena Tuttle sent an email to Amy Mills and others noting that the defense attorney for Forcht Bank had attached a request. She asked Amy to review that document and provide any information related to Amy’s applications for employment or participation in a practicum or internship program as a counselor or Counselor Associate. If Amy could not provide documentation, Sarena asked for a list of any places where Amy had provided services, with dates and any compensation received.

The following day, April 29, 2023, at approximately 1:30 PM, Amy Mills replied. She stated that the defense has her transcripts showing the coursework and grades completed at college, along with a copy of the diploma received upon completing those requirements. She asked whether this documentation might be substantial enough to satisfy the request.

On May 1, 2023, at 9:30 AM, Sarena Tuttle responded to Amy’s message. She clarified that the request was for employment verification information—specifically, places where Amy had applied, worked, or volunteered using the newly acquired skills. She emphasized that the request was not limited to classes, grades, or diplomas.

Amy Mills sent another reply on May 3, 2023, at about 9:17 AM. She expressed uncertainty about the meaning of the term “new skills.” She said she would assemble what she could, but noted that items were in storage and that her physical capabilities would make gathering materials difficult. She reiterated that the requested items included the course-required curriculum for internship per the course requirements needed to obtain the diploma, and she asked about the time frame for the requested documents (whether it covered 5, 10, or 15 years).

Later on May 3, 2023, at 10:20 AM, Sarena Tuttle sent a final clarification. She stated that there is no time frame. She asked Amy to provide any and all documents related to any and all employment (including internships and volunteer work) under the fields of substance abuse, behavioral health, or mental health counseling. She advised that, even if documentation is not available, Amy should provide a list of every place where she provided services, with dates and the amount of any compensation, if applicable. The email included Sarena’s contact information and noted a new email address for correspondence.

Signature details for Sarena M. Tuttle appeared at the end of the May 3, 2023 message, identifying her roles as Litigation Paralegal and Workers’ Compensation Case Manager with The Whaley Law Firm, along with the firm’s address (712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222), direct line [PHONE-2002]), office line [PHONE-2003] ext 104), and the email address Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com, accompanied by a note to “PLEASE NOTE NEW EMAIL ADDRESS.”

item_441.note: Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2005]
Client: Amy Mills

Re: Your Personal Injury Claim - New Request for Information

The record begins with an email thread concerning Amy Mills’s personal injury claim and a new request for information. On April 28, 2023, Sarena M. Tuttle, a Litigation Paralegal and Workers’ Compensation Case Manager at The Whaley Law Firm, located at 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222, with contact numbers D: [PHONE-2006] and O: [PHONE-2007] ext 104 and email Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com (noting the new address), sent Amy Mills a message following receipt of a request from the Defense Attorney for Forcht Bank. The message asked Amy to review the attached document and provide information related to her applications for employment or participation in a practicum or internship program as a counselor or Counselor Associate, and invited her to contact them with any questions.

On April 29, 2023, Amy Mills replied. She stated that the request concerns her transcripts, which show coursework and grades, and a copy of the diploma received as a result of completing these requirements. She asked whether this documentation was substantial enough for the defense’s needs.

The following day, April 30? or April 29? The timeline shows April 29 as the day of Amy’s message; on May 1, 2023, Sarena responded again. In her May 1 message, Sarena explained that Amy’s response referenced classes, grades, and diplomas, but the defense’s request is for employment verification information—specifically, places where Amy has applied, worked, or volunteered using the new skills referenced in the request.

Finally, on May 3, 2023, Amy Mills sent a reply clarifying her understanding and next steps. She wrote that she is unsure what “new skills” refers to. She indicated she would gather what she can for the request as soon as possible, but noted that her belongings are in storage and she would need help due to physical limitations. She acknowledged that the course-required internship curriculum, as part of the requirements to obtain the diplomas, is among the requested items. She also asked for clarification on the time frame for the requested documents (e.g., 5, 10, or 15 years) and signed her name, “Amy Mills.”

item_442.note: Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2009] centers on client Amy Mills. On April 29, 2023 at 1:29:48 PM, Amy Mills sent an email from [EMAIL-2010] to Sarena Tuttle at sarena@whaleylawfirm.com with the subject Re: Your Personal Injury Claim - New Request for Information. In her message, Amy stated that “they have my transcripts that state the course work of this request with grade from the college proving I have done this course work with a copy of the diploma received as a result of completing these requirements!” and asked, “Is this not substantial enough?” She signed the message, “Thank you, Amy Mills.”

Earlier, on Friday, April 28, 2023 at 10:41 AM, Sarena Tuttle replied to Amy. The message read: “Good morning Amy, Yesterday our office received the attached request from the Defense Attorney for Forcht Bank. Please review the document and provide us with any information related to your application(s) for employment or participation in a practicum or internship program as a counselor or Counselor Associate. If you have any questions, please do not hesitate to contact us to discuss them in further detail.” Sarena M. Tuttle is identified as a Litigation Paralegal and Workers’ Compensation Case Manager at The Whaley Law Firm, located at 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222. Her direct line is [PHONE-2011], the office line is [PHONE-2012] ext 104, and her email is Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com, noted as the “PLEASE NOTE NEW EMAIL ADDRESS.”

This note details a sequence of email correspondence concerning a new request for information related to Amy Mills’s personal injury claim. The subject line of the first message is “Re: Your Personal Injury Claim - New Request for Information.” On May 1, 2023, at 09:30:23 AM Eastern Time, Sarena Tuttle, a litigation paralegal and workers’ compensation case manager at The Whaley Law Firm, sent an email from sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com to [EMAIL-2015] and Amy Mills, with cc to [EMAIL-2016], Aaron Whaley, [EMAIL-2017], and Amy Mills Premise [DOB-2018]. The body of the message begins by addressing Amy: “Good morning Amy, Your response speaks of classes, grades & diplomas. They are asking for employment verification information. Places where you have applied, worked, or volunteered using these new skills.” The message is signed by Sarena M. Tuttle, Litigation Paralegal & Workers’ Compensation Case Manager at The Whaley Law Firm, with the firm’s address at 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222, and contact numbers D: [PHONE-2019] and O: [PHONE-2020] ext 104. Her email is Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com, and the email warns to “PLEASE NOTE NEW EMAIL ADDRESS.”

Earlier in this thread, on Saturday, April 29, 2023, at 1:30?PM, Amy Mills wrote in reply: “In regards to this They have my transcripts that state the course work of this request with grade from the college proving I have done this course work with a copy of the diploma received as a result of completing these requirements! Is this not substantial enough? Thank you, Amy Mills.”

Prior to that, on Friday, April 28, 2023, at 10:41 AM, Sarena Tuttle had written: “Good morning Amy, Yesterday our office received the attached request from the Defense Attorney for Forcht Bank. Please review the document and provide us with any information related to your application(s) for employment or participation in a practicum or internship program as a counselor or Counselor Associate. If you have any questions, please do not hesitate to contact us to discuss them in further detail.” The message is again signed by Sarena M. Tuttle, with The Whaley Law Firm’s contact information and the note “(PLEASE NOTE NEW EMAIL ADDRESS).”

On April 27, 2023, at 4:06 PM Eastern, the Knox Circuit Court in Kentucky electronically filed a notice in the matter Mills, Amy v. Forcht Bank, Case No. 20-CI-00112, with Envelope Number 5927892. The header shows this as an NEF (Not Electronic Filed) for Knox 20-CI-00112, Mills, Amy v. Forcht Bank, Envelope #5927892. The notice originated from [EMAIL-2024]. The eFiler listed was Betsy R. Catron, attorney for the defendant. The case caption is Mills, Amy versus Forcht Bank, and the case number is 20-CI-00112. The envelope number is 5927892. The notice was electronically mailed to Whaley, Aaron Gregory at [EMAIL-2025]; Catron, Betsy R at [EMAIL-2026]; and Ellis, Joshua at [EMAIL-2027].

The notice indicates that the following document(s) were included in this eFiling: NOTICE - OTHER: NOTICE OF SERVICE. The document(s) can be viewed at the link: https://kcoj.kycourts.net/eFilingRetrieval/Home/Package?id=4CC2F622-AEC0-4DA2-A189-ABC87794E3C3. This notice was automatically generated by the Kentucky Court of Justice eFiling system, and recipients were instructed not to reply to the email.

Kentucky Court of Justice Confidentiality Notice states that this message and/or attachment is intended only for the addressee and may contain information that is privileged, confidential and/or proprietary work product. If you are not the intended recipient, or an authorized employee, agent or representative of the intended recipient, you must not read, copy, retain or disseminate this message or any attachment. Do not forward this message and attachment without the express written consent of the sender. If you have received this message in error, please contact the sender immediately and delete all copies of the message and any attachment. Transmission or misdelivery shall not constitute waiver of any applicable legal privilege.

item_448.note: Case: Amy Mills Premise-[DOB-2041]. Client: Amy Mills. A forwarded email titled “Fwd: Amy Mills V. Forcht Bank” concerns the case and was circulated on April 27, 2023. The forward originates from Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm and was sent to Sarena Tuttle and Amy Mills Premise [DOB-2042] (the recipients listed in the To line include Sarena Tuttle and Amy Mills Premise [DOB-2043]).

Within that forward, there is a message from Misty Harmon dated April 27, 2023 at 4:09 PM (the record notes “4:09?PM” with a question mark after the time). In that message, Misty states: “Please see the attached Notice of Service, 2nd Set of Interrogatories and 3rd Set of Request for Production to Pl, Amy Mills (word version). Should you have any questions, please do not hesitate to contact the office.” Misty’s signature identifies her as a paralegal to Jillian D. House, Betsy R. Catron, Catie Coldiron Joseph, Kaylee F. Collins, Ryan J. McElroy, and Tori S. Wells. The message is sent from Vine Center, 333 West Vine Street, Suite 1100, Lexington, Kentucky 40507, with P: [PHONE-2044] and F: [PHONE-2045], and the firm’s website listed as www.whtlaw.com. A confidentiality disclaimer accompanies the email, stating that information contained in the transmission is privileged and confidential and outlining steps to take if the recipient is not the intended recipient.

The email chain also includes the contact details of the sender, Aaron Whaley, from Whaley Law Firm, located at 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, Kentucky 40222, with telephone number [PHONE-2046]. The forward and attached communications concern the case “Amy Mills V. Forcht Bank” and reference the Notice of Service, the second set of interrogatories, and the third set of request for production to Plaintiff Amy Mills, provided in Word format.

item_454.note: Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2061]. Client: Amy Mills.

In an update dated February 28, 2023 at 2:20 PM, Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm sent a note to Amy Mills, Sarena Tuttle, and the Amy Mills Premise file. He stated that certified records have been requested from all of Amy’s providers. He asked Sarena to confirm that a request has been placed for the physical therapy (PT) that Amy is currently attending. He also indicated that once all the certified records are collected, he intends to send them to an expert on brain injuries to obtain their opinion.

Earlier that same day, at 2:16 PM, Amy Mills replied. She wrote: “Hey Aaron I went to my specialist appointment on Thursday and you are listed on my release of information documents. I’m continuing physical therapy and that’s about all I know at this point besides the Orthopedic specialist in Lexington stating that I had a lot of scar tissue Damage. Thanks Amy Mills” The message was signed with Amy Mills’ name and included her email signature, which quotes: “The heart of a fool is in his mouth, but the mouth of a wise man is in his heart.” — Benjamin Franklin.

The thread concluded with Aaron Whaley’s closing, identifying him as with the Whaley Law Firm located at 712 Lyndon Ln, Louisville, KY 40222, and listing the firm’s phone number as [PHONE-2062]. The correspondence reflects the case history and ongoing plan to obtain certified medical records for review, with the intention of consulting a brain-injury expert once those records are gathered.

She is willing to attempt mediation and would request a new trial schedule if mediation is unsuccessful. The matter is designated with the tags #DC, #Lit, and #MTSA.

item_459.note: On February 13, 2023, at 12:32 PM Eastern, the Knox Circuit Court processed and accepted a filing in the case Mills, Amy v. Forcht Bank, Case No. 20-CI-00112, Envelope Number 5591173. The NCP (for eFiler) is listed as KNOX 20-CI-00112, Mills, Amy vs. Forcht Bank, Envelope # 5591173. The eFiler was Aaron Gregory Whaley, attorney for the plaintiff. The court handling the matter was Knox Circuit Court. The case caption is Mills, Amy vs. Forcht Bank, Case Number 20-CI-00112, Envelope Number 5591173.

The electronic notice was sent to the following recipients: Aaron Gregory Whaley at [EMAIL-2071], Betsy R. Catron at [EMAIL-2072], and Joshua Ellis at [EMAIL-2073]. The notice also confirms that a Motion Hour was scheduled for February 13, 2023, at 9:00 AM Eastern Time, before Hon. Michael Caperton, in an unspecified room. The eFiling included three documents: a Disclosure Statement and two Exhibits.

Additional details are available at the Kentucky Court of Justice eFiling system links: https://kcoj.kycourts.net/efiling/Dashboard/Receipt?caseid=5591173 and https://kcoj.kycourts.net/eFilingRetrieval/Home/Package?id=712FAF87-AED4-44BF-9736-FCEB365C6D64.

Re: High Priority Mills Expert Disclosures - Due [DOB-2088]. On February 13, 2023, Sarena M. Tuttle of The Whaley Law Firm (712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222; D: [PHONE-2089]; O: [PHONE-2090] ext 104; E: Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com; PLEASE NOTE NEW EMAIL ADDRESS) sent an email to Aaron Whaley regarding the Mills expert disclosures that were marked high priority and due February 11, 2023. The message, which was CC’d to the file labeled “Amy Mills Premise [DOB-2091]” at [EMAIL-2092], stated: “Hey AW, Please give this a good run over. I have attached the Commonwealth IME & both addendums, the FCE report & the Voc/Eco Report & Supplement Letter. Please let me know if I missed anything.” The email included the attachments described and requested Aaron’s review to ensure nothing was missed.

Earlier correspondence included an email from Aaron Whaley sent on Friday, February 10, 2023, at 3:42 PM, in which he instructed: “Under B for Linda Jones list all documents we sent to Linda. For both experts include their reports. Also list a generic all medical providers one and specifically say Dr. Cyna Khalily.” This directive was part of the ongoing Mills expert disclosures discussion.

Before that, on Friday, February 3, 2023, at 12:06 PM, Sarena Tuttle wrote: “Hey AW, Please find attached the rough draft of Mills Expert Disclosures. Nazar section complete as phy section already templated - Voc/Eco generic rough outline provided but needs your silver language added.” The message was signed with Sarena M. Tuttle’s contact information and included the firm’s standard signature block, which lists The Whaley Law Firm, 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222, with D: [PHONE-2093], O: [PHONE-2094] ext 104, E: Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com, and the note: (PLEASE NOTE NEW EMAIL ADDRESS). The email was followed by the closing lines of Aaron Whaley’s signature block, which showed the Whaley Law Firm address at 712 Lyndon Ln, Louisville, KY 40222 and the phone number [PHONE-2095].

item_464.note: Case: Amy Mills, Premise case file dated [DOB-2097], involving client Amy Mills.

This series of emails concerns the High Priority Mills Expert Disclosures due February 11, 2023. The thread begins with an exchange between Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm and Sarena Tuttle of The Whaley Law Firm. On February 13, 2023, Aaron replied to Sarena with a brief confirmation: “Looks good.” This reply followed Sarena’s message sent earlier that morning, in which she asked Aaron to review a package of materials she had attached and to let her know if anything was missed. In Sarena’s note, she stated that she had attached the Commonwealth IME and both addendums, the FCE report, and the Voc/Eco Report and Supplement Letter, and she requested a thorough review.

The history of the drafting process began on February 3, 2023, when Sarena sent what she described as the rough draft of Mills Expert Disclosures. She noted that the Nazar section was complete and the phy section had already been templated, while the Voc/Eco section had a generic rough outline provided but still needed Aaron’s “silver language” added.

Prior to that, on February 10, 2023, Aaron sent a directive regarding how disclosures should address Linda Jones. His instruction was to list all documents sent to Linda Jones under the B section, ensure that both experts’ reports were included, provide a generic entry for “all medical providers,” and specifically name Dr. Cyna Khalily.

The emails include standard correspondence formatting and signatures from both attorneys. Sarena’s signature lists The Whaley Law Firm, 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222, with direct line and office numbers, and a note indicating a new email address. Aaron’s correspondence is shown with his contact details at The Whaley Law Firm. The initial message announcing the high-priority disclosures was sent in the subject line: Re: High Priority Mills Expert Disclosures - Due [DOB-2098], and the message chain included a cc to Amy Mills Premise via a Louisvillle accident law firm filevine app address.

In February 2023, the Mills matter produced a short email thread about high-priority expert disclosures. On February 13, 2023, Sarena M. Tuttle of The Whaley Law Firm sent a message to Aaron Whaley with the subject Re: High Priority Mills Expert Disclosures - Due [DOB-2111]. She asked him to give the matter a thorough review and noted that she had attached the Commonwealth IME and both addendums, the FCE report, the Voc/Eco Report, and a Supplement Letter, requesting that he let her know if anything was missed. Her message included her contact details: The Whaley Law Firm, 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222; direct line [PHONE-2112]; office line [PHONE-2113] ext 104; email Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com, with a note to use the new email address.

Earlier in the sequence, on February 10, 2023 at 3:42 PM, Aaron Whaley replied with instructions: Under B for Linda Jones, list all documents we sent to Linda; for both experts, include their reports; also list a generic “all medical providers” document and specifically name Dr. Cyna Khalily.

Prior to that, on February 3, 2023 at 12:06 PM, Sarena Tuttle wrote: “Hey AW, Please find attached the rough draft of Mills Expert Disclosures. Nazar section complete as phy section already templated - Voc/Eco generic rough outline provided but needs your silver language added.” Her message was signed with her Whaley Law Firm details (712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222; D: [PHONE-2114]; O: [PHONE-2115] ext 104; E: Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com) and included the parenthetical note “PLEASE NOTE NEW EMAIL ADDRESS.” The email thread then circulated Aaron Whaley’s signature block, which read: “Aaron Whaley, Whaley Law Firm, 712 Lyndon Ln, Louisville, KY 40222; [PHONE-2116].”

item_466.uuid: b060199a-b93f-4461-8ee0-f442206f75ba
item_467.author_name: Aaron Whaley
item_467.episode_type: imported
item_467.last_activity: 2023-02-10T00:00:00
item_467.note: Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2118]. Client: Amy Mills.

In February 2023, an appointment-update thread related to Amy Mills Premise was communications between Amy Mills and attorney Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm. On February 10, 2023, Whaley forwarded the “Fwd: Appt update” message to Amy Mills Premise, attaching prior correspondence that began earlier in January 2023.

In Amy Mills’s message dated January 13, 2023 at 4:10 PM, she told Aaron Whaley that she had indicated in paperwork that he would have access to her medical records. She explained that she had gathered the paperwork for the appointment and reviewed the records from her March appointment. The blood work from that appointment showed a high white blood cell count, which usually signals an infection. She asked her doctor, JP, about this, and he recalled that it was a bad sinus infection and that she tends to get them every spring. She added that she does not remember a great deal, and she questioned the sequence of events surrounding a fall. She asserted that it seems impossible to have been knocked out cold—she described a knot in the left side of her head that remains sensitive, along with bruising she associates with blunt force trauma—that she could have bladder incontinence, vomiting, loss of space and time for days without memory, waking up unable to move, and wearing clothes that were wet from water on the floor, all without blunt head trauma. She noted that the foyer where the incident occurred is a small space with a bench. She said she spoke with JP about the ER visit and that the only thing in question was whether she hit her head on the floor or on the bench, and if she hit her head on the bench, whether she also hit her neck on the bench. She emphasized that she is not sure about these details “1,000 percent” because, as she said, it knocked her out or rendered her unconscious.

Earlier, on January 13, 2023 at 3:49 PM, Aaron Whaley replied, asking what orthopedist Amy had seen. He also stated that he had sent her records to a medical reviewer to determine the best case from a medical standpoint to argue that her migraines are the result of the fall, and he expected to have that review back within about two weeks.

In another thread from January 13, 2023 at 3:41 PM, Amy Mills recounted that she had gone to an orthopedic appointment the previous day and was in significant pain, to the point of tears. The orthopedic doctor was scheduling six weeks of physical therapy and warned that if there were no improvement, another surgery might be required. She described ongoing severe pain and noted that the doctor said scar tissue had built up as a result of prolonged treatment. She attributed the scar tissue to the severity of head and neck injuries and to the initial ER visit, during which the report characterized the injuries as a muscle “strain,” even though the ER tests indicated injuries.

On January 10, 2023 at 11:17 AM, Amy Mills wrote again, describing that she had been down for about 15 days with migraines, to the point of being unable to do anything, with JP’s help almost constantly. She expressed frustration that she felt treated as though she were not a married person despite being a single mom. She stated that her medical records document what is labeled as “suicide migraines” linked to the traumatic brain injury (TBI), and that with the head injury the migraines are even worse. She had discussed with someone off the record about disability cases; that person had approved and denied disability cases and believed she would actually qualify as disabled, though the doctor and economist assessments had not been fully considered with respect to her head injury. She discussed concerns about a nurse’s report from a visit that suggested relying on a nurse instead of a physician, and noted that the nurse’s report came from the ER visit where she was told she was fine, which conflicted with her own report. She described vomiting, an inability to have a conversation, and significant memory gaps (“lost a lot of space and time”). She criticized what she called the malpractice of prolonged treatment, claiming the physician’s report said she was fine while the scans and radiology did indicate injuries that should have informed a diagnosis. She stated that her primary physician had repeatedly said that delaying treatment had caused prolonged treatment and complications, leaving her with lifelong issues. She asserted that this should never have happened and that she had video evidence of rain during the incident, no rugs, no signs, making the situation clearly negligent—“100 percent.” She mentioned that a woman named Bobby from a band had testified that many people had fallen there and that those involved knew there was an issue and that this could have been avoided, calling it criminal.

On January 9, 2023 at 3:42 PM, Aaron Whaley replied that he would add Amy’s information to her file and asked how the demand was progressing. He invited her to share any further thoughts about the demand.

Earlier on January 9, 2023 at 3:14 PM, Amy Mills wrote that she had an orthopedic appointment scheduled for Thursday in Lexington.

The exchange closed with signatures that repeated the familiar phrases: “The heart of a fool is in his mouth, but the mouth of a wise man is in his heart.” — Benjamin Franklin. The thread also included standard contact information for Aaron Whaley and the Whaley Law Firm, located at 712 Lyndon Ln, Louisville, KY 40222, with the phone number [PHONE-2119]. The closing lines also reprinted Amy Mills’s name and the Franklin quote in a loop.

Tuttle, The Whaley Law Firm, 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222; D: [PHONE-2125]; O: [PHONE-2126] ext 104; E: Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com (PLEASE NOTE NEW EMAIL ADDRESS).

Tuttle of The Whaley Law Firm sent an email titled Re: Def IROG answers to Aaron Whaley. In the body, she wrote: “Here you go. They are in the Pleadings Folder.
