Forcht Bank, case number 20-CI-00112, and is labeled in the file as Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2205] for client Amy Mills. A Notice of Electronic Filing (NEF) was issued for the eFiler in this matter: Knox 20-CI-00112, Mills, Amy v. Forcht Bank, Envelope 5512786.

The Notice of Electronic Filing was filed on January 24, 2023 at 2:33 PM Eastern. The eFiler listed is Aaron Gregory Whaley, Attorney for the Plaintiff. The email communicating the filing was sent from [EMAIL-2206] to [EMAIL-2207], Betsy R. Catron at [EMAIL-2208], and Joshua Ellis at [EMAIL-2209]. No CC addresses are listed. The case details to which this notice pertains are: Court — Knox (Circuit); Case Caption — Mills, Amy v. Forcht Bank; Case Number — 20-CI-00112; Envelope Number — 5512786.

The notice indicates that the following individuals were electronically mailed the notice: Aaron Whaley ([EMAIL-2210]), Betsy R. Catron (bet [EMAIL-2211]), and Joshua Ellis ([EMAIL-2212]). The eFiling included five documents, all described as “NOTICE - OTHER: NOTICE OF SERVICE - CMR/CMB,” corresponding to the following entities: Norton Orthopedic Institute (NORTON ORTHO INST), Grace Health (GRACE HEALTH), BHC South Imaging (BHC SOUTH IMAGING), BHC Orthopaedics (BHC ORTHO), and Baptist Health Corbin (BAPTIST HEALTH CORBIN).

Additional details provided with the filing include three links: a dashboard receipt page at https://kcoj.kycourts.net/efiling/Dashboard/Receipt?caseid=5512786; a document retrieval page at https://kcoj.kycourts.net/eFilingRetrieval/Home/Package?id=A6CD3AEE-85AF-426D-8639-481EDA5BC1F2; and a filing page for subsequent case matters at https://kcoj.kycourts.net/efiling/Filing/?parentId=5512786. The notice notes that this is an automatic generation from the Kentucky Court of Justice eFiling system and requests that recipients not reply to the email.

item_504.episode_type: imported
item_504.last_activity: 2023-01-13T00:00:00
item_504.note: Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2258]. Client: Amy Mills.

On January 13, 2023, Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm emailed Amy Mills with a Re: Appt update. He asked, “What Ortho did you see? I have sent your records to a medical reviewer to see what the best case from a medical standpoint we can make to say your migraines are the result of the fall. I should have that review back in the next two weeks.”

Amy Mills replied later that day, describing the orthopedic appointment she had attended the previous day. She said she went to the orthopedic specialist and had been in severe pain all day, to the point of tears. The doctor reportedly scheduled six weeks of physical therapy, and if there was no change, another surgery would be required. She described terrible, constant pain that was not getting better and noted that the doctor said she had scar tissue buildup from prolonged treatment. She attributed the scar tissue to two factors: the severity of injuries to her head and neck, and her initial ER visit, where the report labeled the injury as a muscle “strain” even though the ER tests performed that day showed injuries.

Earlier messages from Amy Mills provide additional context. On January 10, 2023 at 11:17 AM, she wrote at length about her condition. She had experienced approximately 15 days of migraines in the previous month and a week, during which she was largely unable to do anything. She mentioned that JP had to help her almost all the time. She described disputes about marital status affecting support, noting difficulties related to being a single mom as well. She referred to migraines she labeled as “suicide migraines” that stemmed from a traumatic brain injury (TBI); with the head injury, the migraines were especially disruptive, preventing her from working or engaging in other activities, as her head injury exerted control over every aspect of her life. She stated she had discussed her situation with someone off the record who evaluates disability cases and who had expressed that she would actually qualify for disability. She claimed that the assessments by a doctor and an economist had not been taken into account in her favor. She criticized the nurse report discussed in their talks, arguing that the nurse was not a doctor and suggesting they could rely on a nurse’s findings if necessary. She asserted that the nurse from that ER visit was the same ER nurse who had reported she was fine, while her own report described different symptoms. She described vomiting and an inability to have a conversation, noting that she had lost a lot of time—days—that she did not remember. She described medical malpractice for prolonging treatment because the ER report had said she was fine, while the scans and radiology reports on which a diagnosis should have been based showed injuries. Her primary physician allegedly stated multiple times that the ER visit had caused prolonged treatment and complications, and she warned that she was “stuck with this for the rest of [her] life.” She contended that the event should never have happened, and she claimed to have video showing it had occurred again during rain, with no rugs or signs. She stated that this was clearly negligent, “100 percent.” She added that “Bobby the lady from the band” stated that she, she and multiple people had fallen there; they knew it was an issue and that it could have been completely avoided. She concluded with the assertion that this was criminal.

Also on January 9, 2023, at 3:42 PM, Aaron Whaley replied to Amy, saying, “Ok, thanks for letting me know. I will add that to your file. Let me know how it goes. Have you put any more thought into the demand?”

Earlier that same day, at 3:14 PM, Amy Mills wrote that she had an orthopedic appointment scheduled for Thursday in Lexington. She included the line, “The heart of a fool is in his mouth, but the mouth of a wise man is in his heart.” attributed to Benjamin Franklin, followed by contact details for Whaley Law Firm.

The correspondence concludes with the signatures of Aaron Whaley, Whaley Law Firm, 712 Lyndon Ln, Louisville, KY 40222, and Amy Mills, including the Franklin quote.

item_504.project_name: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2259]
item_504.uuid: 73acf25b-217d-4f56-b3d9-b309fbfce31d
item_505.author_name: Aaron Whaley
item_505.episode_type: imported
item_505.last_activity: 2023-01-13T00:00:00
item_505.note: Case: Amy Mills — Premise, [DOB-2260]. Client: Amy Mills.

In January 2023, communications regarding Amy Mills continued between her and her attorney, Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm. On January 13, 2023, Whaley forwarded an appointment-update email to Amy Mills Premise. The forwarded message included Amy’s reply, in which she reported that she had attended an orthopedic appointment the day before. The orthopedic specialist is scheduling six weeks of physical therapy, and if there is no improvement after that, another surgery may be required. Amy stated that she is in “terrible constant pain” and that she is in tears. She noted a scar tissue buildup, which she attributed to prolonged treatment, and she explained that the scar tissue results from both the severity of injuries to her head and neck and from the initial ER visit, where the ER report listed a muscle “strain,” but the tests performed that day showed injuries. She added a brief update on her current situation.

Earlier in the thread, on January 10, 2023, Amy wrote to Aaron describing her condition in more detail. She said she had been down for about 15 days in the last month and a week due to migraines, requiring JP to help her almost all the time. She commented on issues tied to her marital status and being a single mother. She stated that her medical records document migraines described as “suicide migraines” resulting from a traumatic brain injury (TBI); with the head injury, the migraines are worse. She mentioned discussing her situation off the record with someone who handles disability cases and who indicated she would qualify for disability, asserting that the doctor and economist’ assessments were not given full consideration. She noted concerns about a nurse’s report from a visit, pointing out that the nurse is not a doctor, and that if necessary they could pursue a nurse’s report rather than a physician’s. She referenced that the nurse from the ER visit had told her she was fine, while her own report described she was vomiting and unable to have a conversation, resulting in a loss of days she could not account for. She contended that there had been malpractice in prolonging treatment because the ER report said she was fine, yet scans and radiology results showed injuries that should have informed the diagnosis. Her primary physician has stated multiple times that the ER visit contributed to prolonged treatment and complications, and Amy stated that she is “stuck with this for the rest of my life” and that it “should have never happened.” She claimed to have video showing a situation where it was raining and something was done again, noting there were no rugs or signs. She described the situation as clearly negligent “100 percent,” and mentioned “Bobby,” described as a lady from a band, who had told her that she and multiple others had fallen there and that those involved knew it was an issue. Amy stated that the incident could have been avoided and concluded with the assertion that it was criminal.

On January 9, 2023, Aaron Whaley replied: “Ok, thanks for letting me know. I will add that to your file. Let me know how it goes. Have you put any more thought into the demand?” Earlier that same day, at 3:14 PM, Amy Mills replied that she had an orthopedic appointment scheduled for Thursday in Lexington and signed off with a quote from Benjamin Franklin: “The heart of a fool is in his mouth, but the mouth of a wise man is in his heart.”

The thread closes with Amy Mills’ contact details and her sign-off, which repeats the Franklin quote in her signature. The overall record documents ongoing medical concerns following head and neck injuries, ongoing pain and migraines, disability considerations, and disputes over medical treatment, ER reporting, and potential negligence.

item_518.note: Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2310]. Client: Amy Mills.

On December 9, 2022, at 06:15:23 Eastern Time, Baptist Health EFT Server automatically sent an invitation to a shared folder to sarena@whaleylawfirm.com. The folder is named THE WHALEY LAW FIRM 125337076. If this is the recipient’s first time accessing the site at https://baptistftp.bhsi.com, a password must be set up, and the email address to which this message was sent should be used as the username. The invitation to view this shared folder will expire if not verified within 30 days of receipt. The recipient was instructed to use the View Folder link below to verify access and view the shared files. The message from the sender read: “A Mills 125337076, EXPIRES IN 5 DAYS.”

This is an automated message. Any support issues or questions should be responded to via email, and responses may take up to 5 business days. It is recommended that questions regarding the information shared be directed to the Baptist Health department that was originally contacted. An FAQ link is provided for further information on accessing files from Baptist Health EFT: Baptist Health EFT FAQs.

The transmission may contain information that is privileged, confidential, and/or exempt from disclosure under applicable law. If you are not the intended recipient, you should delete this message; any disclosure, copying, distribution, or use of the information contained herein (including any reliance thereon) is strictly PROHIBITED.

Baptist Health will never send emails that require you to send account or personal information to us via public email or pop-up windows.

This email, facsimile, or letter and any files or attachments transmitted with it contain information that is confidential and proprietary. It is intended only for the use of the individual(s) and entity(ies) to whom it is addressed. If you are the intended recipient, further disclosures are prohibited without proper authorization. If you are not the intended recipient, any disclosure, copying, printing, or use of this information is strictly prohibited and possibly a violation of federal or state law. If you have received this information in error, please notify Baptist Health immediately at [PHONE-2311] or via email to the sender.

The body of the email states that invoice 0000088 for $550.00 is past due and was due on July 25, 2022. It asks the recipient to check on the status of this payment and requests a response.

The email directs the recipient to visit the link below to view the invoice: https://my.freshbooks.com/#/link/eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9.eyJzeXN0ZW1pZCI6MjU2NDMyNCwidXNlcmlkIjo3MDY2NzcsInR5cGUiOiJpbnZvaWNlIiwib2JqZWN0aWQiOjI4MDMyNDUsImV4cCI6MTY5ODkzMTI2NiwibGV2ZWwiOjB9.tyLx9KoSTEl_r9sTcY7aCSE5kYZq19QFixKejboSsyg?companyName=Jeremy&invoiceNumber=0000088&ownerEmail=accounting%40commonwealthime.com&type=primary

The message footer reads “=====================” and then notes that it is powered by FreshBooks, inviting the recipient to keep all invoices organized and accessible online with the prompt “Get Started” and provides this signup link: https://my.freshbooks.com/#/signup.

item_532.note: Case: Amy Mills v. Forcht Bank (Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2373]). Client: Amy Mills.

In the matter of Amy Mills v. Forcht Bank, communications between counsel progressed through late summer and fall of 2022. On August 22, 2022, Betsy Catron of WHT Law emailed Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm with several requests and updates. She reported that they had requested records and bills from Ms. Mills’s Norton providers, noting that Ms. Mills was understood to have undergone an MRI at the end of May; however, none of those Norton providers had records of recent treatment. They were still waiting on Ms. Mills’s records and bills from Baptist Health Corbin and would provide copies once received. Betsy asked for guidance on any other recent medical treatment Mrs. Mills might have had. She also inquired about a previously advised Functional Capacity Evaluation (FCE) that was to occur in late July and asked when a demand might be provided. Betsy further noted that they had requested tax returns for 2016, 2018, and 2020 several times, explaining that the absence of these documents significantly limited their ability to evaluate Ms. Mills’s potential earning impairment claim. She also asked for the last day Bella Beauty was in business and requested any information about additional recent medical treatment. Betsy concluded by inviting Aaron to contact her if there were questions or if she wished to discuss the matters further. The message was sent from Betsy to Aaron, and it did not include the lengthy signature block she later added in other messages.

Later that same day, August 22, 2022 at 12:30 PM, Aaron Whaley responded, stating that he would forward any records they might be missing. He noted that Amy Mills had completed the FCE and attached a copy of it to his reply. He also said he was in the process of conducting an economic analysis.

A little over a month later, on September 21, 2022, Betsy followed up again. In a message dated 9:19 AM, she asked when a demand could be expected, explaining their preference for the demand to be issued at least two months before mediation. She proposed that if a demand could be provided by October 7, they could schedule a December mediation, and she asked for Aaron’s thoughts on this timeline.

Aaron replied later that same day, September 21, 2022, at 10:04 AM, indicating that he was “only waiting on economic report” and that he was hopeful about the timeline (“Fingers crossed”).

On October 11, 2022, Betsy sent another follow-up, asking if Aaron knew when they could expect to receive a demand, expressing thanks in advance. That same day, Aaron responded at 11:55:59 AM, stating that he was waiting on the final report from the economist and that once that was complete, he would be able to prepare the demand, adding that it should be soon.

Throughout these exchanges, the correspondence circulated among Betsy Catron (Attorney at WHT Law), Aaron Whaley (Whaley Law Firm), and copies were distributed to the Amy Mills file ([EMAIL-2374]) and Sarena Tuttle. The sequence reflects ongoing coordination around the timing of the demand and the status of medical and economic analyses in the Amy Mills v. Forcht Bank case.

item_536.note: This note summarizes an internal case communication about Amy Mills v. Forcht Bank. The file is labeled Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2386], and the client is Amy Mills. The correspondence involves Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm in Louisville and Betsy R. Catron of WHT Law in Lexington, with Saraena Tuttle and a file-link email for Amy Mills cc’d, and the subject line references the Amy Mills matter against Forcht Bank.

The August 22, 2022 exchange lays out a detailed set of discovery and damages-related tasks. Betsy Catron wrote to Aaron Whaley in the morning, explaining that they had requested records and bills from Mills’s Norton providers because she reportedly underwent an MRI at the end of May. She reported that none of those Norton providers had any records for recent treatment and that they were awaiting records from Baptist Health Corbin, promising to provide copies upon receipt. Betsy also asked Aaron to advise if there was any other recent medical treatment of Mills. She inquired about a late July Functional Capacity Evaluation (FCE) and inquired about when they would be able to obtain a demand, noting their goal of having a demand at least two months before mediation and suggesting a possible December mediation if a demand could be produced by October 7. Betsy also requested Mills’s tax returns for 2016, 2018, and 2020, explaining that the lack of this information significantly limits their ability to evaluate Mills’s future earning impairment claim. Additionally, she asked for the last day Bella Beauty was in business. The email concluded with Betsy’s signature block, listing her title as Attorney and providing the Vine Center address at 333 West Vine Street, Suite 1100, Lexington, KY 40507, along with phone and fax numbers and the website.

Aaron’s reply that same day, August 22, 2022 at 12:30 PM, stated that he would forward any records they might be missing. He noted that Mills had completed the FCE and that he had attached a copy of it. He added that he was in the process of an economic analysis. The message was copied to Sarena Tuttle and included a file-link email address associated with Amy Mills Premise, and Aaron’s own signature block followed, including his Louisville address and contact details, as well as a caution line about external email attachments. A final line in Aaron’s signature appears garbled, reading “-- Aaron Whttps://www.google.com/maps/search/333uisville, KY 40222” along with the standard external-email caution.

A month later, on September 21, 2022, Betsy sent another inquiry to Aaron: she asked when he would be able to provide a demand, noting that she would like it at least two months out from mediation and proposing that if a demand could be ready by October 7, they could set a December mediation, asking for his thoughts.

Aaron replied later that same day, September 21, 2022 at 10:03:40 AM, stating, “I am only waiting on economic report. Fingers crossed.” This indicated that the economic report was the outstanding item delaying a formal demand. The email chain shows the ongoing planning for mediation and the prerequisites (records, FCE, tax documents, and the economic analysis) being coordinated between the two firms, with the implicit goal of moving toward mediation in December pending the completion of the economic report and other documents.

item_538.note: Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2389]. Client: Amy Mills. In the Bella Beuty matter, this notes the communication with Amy Mills.

On August 25, 2022, at 3:14:34 PM Eastern Time, an email was sent from Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm ([EMAIL-2390]) to [EMAIL-2391], with copies to [EMAIL-2392] and to Sarena Tuttle at sarena@whaleylawfirm.com. The message is part of the Amy Mills Premise case file and concerns a request related to the tax records.

The body of the email reads: “Amy, I understand not knowing an exact date and that is OK. Can you give me a range of time? When we answer we will qualify the statement so that they know it isn't exact so it can't be used against you. I don't know what happened to the tax records. OUr previous file software company lost some files on other cases and it is possible these were also lost. That is why they are a previous company. We can get IRS tax printouts directly from the IRS. We may need you to sign so documentation for that but we will let you know.”

The message is signed with the following contact information:
-- 
Aaron Whaley
Whaley Law Firm
712 Lyndon Ln
Louisville, KY 40222
[PHONE-2393]

item_539.note: Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2395]
Client: Amy Mills

In a sequence of communications during August 2022, Amy Mills and The Whaley Law Firm, led by Sarena M. Tuttle, exchanged updates about her case. The thread begins with several messages from Amy Mills in mid-August and continues through August 25, with discussions centered on tax documents, the status of her business, medical issues, and the recent Functional Capacity Evaluation (FCE) and PPP loan matter.

On August 19, 2022, Amy Mills sent a message noting concerns about bankruptcy related to her inability to keep her business open, and she referenced information from a bank indicating that the electric bill paid would count toward PPP forgiveness. She also indicated she had obtained additional PPP funding but was unsure whether she would medically be able to continue. She expressed that she did not believe this information should be entered into her case, and she stated that she had not received an updated FCE or findings. She mentioned an MRI report indicating a current acute tear in her arm and difficulties in getting doctors to agree on treatment. She asserted ongoing issues for nearly four years, including days when she cannot get out of bed and migraines, with only medications and no remedy. She claimed to have a lingering impact from a head injury, memory difficulties, and the inability to obtain appropriate medical treatment; she criticized the lack of alignment among doctors and described the situation as ongoing pain and suffering.

Earlier on August 19, 2022, at 10:15 AM, Sarena Tuttle had replied that the firm had received the FCE Report and that Aaron had forwarded it to the defense for review. She also stated that documents from Hometown Bank regarding Mills’s PPP loans were under review and that she would obtain more detail from Aaron. She added that Mills’s case team would update her as more information became available and provided contact details for herself and the firm.

Also on August 19, 2022, at 11:08 AM, Mills responded with frustration about the PPP information and the status of her business. She reiterated that she was no longer in business and that the PPP loans were based on the 2019 tax year; she had been able to obtain additional PPP funds but was uncertain whether she would recover sufficiently to continue operating. Mills contended that the PPP information should not be entered into her case, and she criticized the lack of an updated FCE or findings. She emphasized that an MRI report showed a current acute tear in her arm and urged that medical malpractice be considered due to alleged misdiagnosis in the emergency department, which she believed prolonged pain and suffering. She argued that the ongoing situation had persisted for nearly four years, with days when she could not get out of bed and migraines, and stated that she was receiving only medications without effective treatment.

On August 19, 2022, at 10:15 AM, Sarena Tuttle responded that the firm had received the FCE Report and that Aaron had forwarded it to the defense for their review. She noted that they had also received documents from Hometown Bank concerning Mills’s PPP loans, which were under review, and that she would seek further details from Aaron. She indicated that she would provide a more detailed update once available.

By August 24, 2022, at 10:11 AM, Amy Mills asked directly, “What exactly does that report mean?” about the FCE. She was seeking clarification on the implications of the report’s findings.

Later that same day, at 12:20 PM, Sarena Tuttle explained to Mills that the FCE report indicated she was able to perform sedentary work—defined as low-level physical work involving mostly sitting, with occasional walking or standing and no lifting over 10 pounds. The message clarified that, while Mills had work limitations, she could perform non-physical duties, such as a desk job or managing her business with employees performing services. The note also stated that Aaron had arranged an evaluation with an economist to determine the financial impact of these restrictions, and that once the economist’s input was received, they would provide a detailed update. It was also noted that the team was still awaiting Mills’s tax documents from 2016 and 2017, as well as the official date Bella Beauty closed its doors. The firm understood that Mills still possessed the equipment and products at her location and that the date of the last services performed was needed to complete the file. Tuttle emphasized that the financial information was required to determine the appropriate amount of compensation Mills should receive and urged Mills to obtain copies of any missing tax documents from the IRS or her tax preparer if they were not in her possession. The firm provided contact information for Sarena M. Tuttle, including the new email address, and the firm’s Louisville, Kentucky address and phone numbers.

Also on August 24, 2022, at 12:20 PM, Mills replied seeking further clarification about the report’s meaning, indicating a need for understanding of its implications for her case.

On August 25, 2022, at 2:29 PM, Mills sent a further update in which she reiterated that she had given all tax documents to the law office and speculated that they might have been lost in the basement, given prior case manager remarks about the previous manager not being thorough and her own willingness to locate the documents. She reaffirmed that the last date of relevant events was 2019, but she refused to provide a precise date and time because she could not be certain, stating she would not misrepresent or lie. She explained that she had been hurt and pursued the process hoping to recover, but she could not physically work, noting that she had built a business through hard work and would have continued if she could. She asserted that she had given her best effort on the associated test and that she had told the truth to the best of her memory. She claimed to depend on others for everything and argued that those others should be scrutinized for failing to act on their responsibilities, linking this to the way she was treated by her bank after the injury.

Also on August 25, 2022, the thread references a prior message from Mills in which she described the physical consequences and inaccuracies she perceived in medical reporting. She indicated that she had been bedridden for two days after a certain appointment, that the doctor had misidentified her dominant hand (she is left-hand dominant, not right-handed), and that her pain at the time of leaving the appointment was an 8 out of 10, a detail she did not see reflected in the report. She stated she could hardly stand or sit before leaving and that chronic migraines had not been accounted for. She described being barely able to leave her home, unable to run her business (which she characterized as a teaching institute with no one else qualified to operate it), and claimed the business was bankrupt. She questioned how she could work when she could barely write her name and mentioned memory problems, distortions from a head injury, and the need for someone to drive her to appointments. She emphasized her educational achievements, including a master's degree, and her longstanding effort to build a life that had been taken from her, noting that others asked her to perform a desk job without considering her migraines and general impact on life.

The communications also include the firm’s standard contact details for Sarena M. Tuttle at The Whaley Law Firm, including a Louisville, KY address, direct line, office line, and a new email address, to facilitate ongoing updates and inquiries. The thread also references Bella Beauty, Mills’s business, and the need to establish the date when its doors officially closed, as well as the status of Mills’s tax documents from 2016 and 2017.

In summary, the August 2022 correspondence focused on the implications of the FCE report (which indicated the ability to perform sedentary work), the status and availability of tax documents (2016 and 2017) and the date Bella Beauty closed, the handling and review of PPP loan information, and Mills’s ongoing medical concerns, including migraines, arm injury, head injury, and related treatment challenges. Throughout, Mills conveyed concern about accuracy in medical documentation, the impact of her injuries on her ability to work, and the financial and legal ramifications of the case.

item_540.note: This case note, titled Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2397] and pertaining to client Amy Mills, records a sequence of email exchanges in August 2022 between Amy Mills and Sarena M. Tuttle of The Whaley Law Firm regarding updates to a functional capacity evaluation (FCE), related financial issues, and the status of Amy’s business and potential claims. On August 25, 2022 at 14:29:36 Eastern Time, Amy wrote to Sarena to contest elements of a prior report. She stated that she had been bedridden for two days after that appointment and had messaged Aaron; she noted that the doctor had identified the dominant hand incorrectly, insisting she is left-hand dominant, not right-handed. She reported that the pain level at the time she left the appointment was an eight, and she did not see that reflected in the report. She said she could hardly stand or sit before leaving, and that nothing in the report accounted for her chronic migraines. She described being barely able to leave her home, unable to run her business, which she described as a teaching institute for which there was no one qualified to do the work other than herself, and she claimed the business is bankrupt. She expressed concern about how she could work when she could barely write her name and noted memory and head-injury-related issues, including getting things mixed up. She recalled having someone drive her to the appointment and characterized her life as one she had built with significant effort, including a master’s degree, as having been effectively taken from her. She criticized the suggestion of a desk job as disregarding her acute, debilitating migraines and suggested that others did not care because it was not their life to live.

Earlier, on August 24, 2022 at 12:20 PM, Sarena replied to Amy with a summary of the FCE findings. She explained that the report concluded Amy is able to perform sedentary work—defined as low-level physical work requiring mostly sitting, with occasional walking or standing and no lifting over 10 pounds. The message stated that although Amy has work limitations, she can perform non-physical jobs such as a desk job or continuing to run her business (with others handling the services while she handles paperwork). Sarena noted that Aaron had arranged an evaluation with an economist to determine the financial impact of these restrictions, and that they would update Amy once the economist’s input was available. She added that they were awaiting tax documents from 2016 and 2017, as well as the official date Bella Beauty closed its doors. She acknowledged that Amy’s equipment and products remained at the location and that Amy still had possession of the site, but they needed the date of the last services performed. Sarena emphasized that financial information was required to determine the appropriate amount of compensation and urged Amy to contact the IRS or her tax preparer to obtain copies if she did not have the documents in her possession. The message concluded with Sarena’s signature and contact information for The Whaley Law Firm (712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222; D: [PHONE-2398]; O: [PHONE-2399] ext 104; E: Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com with a note about the email address change).

Earlier still, also on August 24, 2022, at 10:11 AM, Amy asked, “What exactly does that report mean?”

On August 19, 2022 at 1:49 PM, Amy stated that she would have to repay a debt because she was unable to stay open, and she referenced a bank representative who indicated that an electric bill paid would count toward PPP loan forgiveness. She suggested that she might file bankruptcy due to the financial situation, noting that the bank’s calculations used the 2019 tax year and that she had secured additional PPP funds, though she doubted whether she would be medically able to heal in time to continue operating.

At 11:36 AM on August 19, 2022, Sarena replied that the defense is entitled to know whether Amy recovered lost wages during her recovery, and if so, from whom. Since Amy had received a PPP loan to cover lost wages, Sarena understood that the defense would not be responsible for those lost wages and that subrogation—reimbursement of the funds provided by Hometown Bank—was likely. She said she would forward Amy’s email to Aaron for further address, and she attached a copy of the FCE Report received on August 16, 2022, apologizing for its tardy delivery.

At 11:08 AM on August 19, 2022, Amy expressed frustration, agreeing that the PPP loan information had been obtained but noting that she was no longer in business and would need to file bankruptcy, with the bank’s use of the 2019 tax year complicating matters. She mentioned obtaining additional PPP funds but doubted whether she could be medically healed in time to continue. She argued that the information should not have been entered into her case and that she still possessed an MRI report showing a current acute tear in her arm, though doctors were not aligned to provide treatment. She asserted that misdiagnosis at the initial ER treatment had prolonged pain and suffering and claimed medical malpractice. She described nearly four years of ongoing symptoms, days in bed, migraines so severe that she felt as though she were dying, and a lack of effective remedies beyond medication.

At 10:15 AM on August 19, 2022, Sarena advised that the FCE Report had been received and had been forwarded to the defense for review. She noted that the bank’s PPP loans were under review and that she would reach out to Aaron for a more detailed update.

Finally, at 10:10 AM on August 19, 2022, Amy sent a request for an update on the progress.

Throughout the thread, Amy’s signature blocks include repeated quotations of a Benjamin Franklin line—“The heart of a fool is in his mouth, but the mouth of a wise man is in his heart”—appearing multiple times in her messages.

In sum, the communications document ongoing concerns about the FCE’s interpretation of her ability to work, the implications for potential compensation, the status and financing of her past business (Bella Beauty), and the interplay between medical conditions (including chronic migraines and an acute arm tear) and potential legal or malpractice considerations, alongside PPP loan matters and tax documentation requirements. The firm’s communications also reference an economist evaluation, the possibility of subrogation for loan funds, and the need for updated medical and financial records to determine appropriate compensation.

item_541.note: Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2401]. Client: Amy Mills.

On April 26, 2019, I received email correspondence from Amy Mills requesting more explanation regarding the FCE report. I advised that sedentary work is low-level physical work—mostly sitting, with walking or standing only occasionally, and nothing that requires lifting over 10 pounds. Examples of such work include a desk job or running her business (handling paperwork) while employees perform the actual services.

The client also requested again the 2016 and 2018 tax documents, as well as the date Bella Beauty performed their last service to a client. This message was cc’d.

item_542.note: Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2403]. Client: Amy Mills. This update concerns communications between Amy Mills and Sarena Tuttle of The Whaley Law Firm regarding the status of the case and associated documents.

On August 24, 2022, Amy Mills emailed Sarena Tuttle with the subject “Re: Update,” asking, “What exactly does that report mean?” This appeared to be a follow-up to the ongoing discussion about recent documents and their implications for the case.

Earlier that week, the day before, on Friday, August 19, 2022 at 1:49 PM, Amy Mills wrote that she would have to file bankruptcy because she was unable to keep the business open. She also indicated that the bank determined the electric bill that had been paid would count toward the balance to be forgiven under the PPP. In her words, she noted: “Okay bc I will have to pay bk bc I was unable to stay open but the guy at the bank also said that the electric bill that was paid would also count toward that balance to be forgiven on the ppp.”

Also on August 19, 2022, at 11:36 AM, Sarena Tuttle responded. She explained that the defense is entitled to know whether Amy recovered lost wages during her recovery and, if so, from whom. Since Amy had received a PPP loan to cover lost wages, the defense would likely not be responsible for those “lost wages,” as they were not truly lost in light of the PPP funds. Sarena suggested that the defense may have to subrogate and reimburse Hometown Bank for the funds provided. She also stated that she would forward Amy’s email to Aaron for further address and appended a copy of the Functional Capacity Evaluation (FCE) Report received on August 16, 2022, noting an apology for the delayed sending.

That same day, at 11:08 AM, Amy Mills sent another message expressing concern: she was glad the PPP loan information had been obtained, but since she was no longer in business she would have to file bankruptcy based on the 2019 tax year. She indicated she had been able to obtain more PPP funds but was unsure whether she would medically be able to heal in time to continue. She asserted that none of this should impact the defense or be entered into her case. She also complained that she had not been given an updated FCE report or findings. She further described ongoing medical issues, including an MRI report showing a current acute tear in her arm, difficulties getting doctors aligned on treatment, and a belief that medical malpractice occurred due to misdiagnosis at the initial ER visit. She claimed the situation had persisted for almost four years, with ongoing days in bed, migraines, and only medication without a remedy, despite continued symptoms.

Earlier on August 19, 2022, at 10:15 AM, Sarena replied that the firm had received the FCE Report and that Aaron had forwarded it to the defense for their review. She also noted receipt of documents from Hometown Bank regarding Amy’s PPP loans, which were under review. She said Aaron had been in correspondence with the defense, but she did not have the details of those conversations and would contact him for a more detailed update.

At 10:10 AM on the same day, Amy had sent a brief message to Sarena asking for an update on progress: “Hey Sarena I was just wanting to get an update on where we r with the progress. Thank You, Amy Mills.” Her signature included a set of Benjamin Franklin quotations, culminating in the line: “The heart of a fool is in his mouth, but the mouth of a wise man is in his heart.” This quotation appears multiple times, reflecting her habitual closing, including variants and repetitions of the Franklin quotes.

In summary, the communications document ongoing efforts to interpret the FCE report, locate and review PPP loan documentation with Hometown Bank, and address broader concerns about bankruptcy, medical issues, alleged misdiagnosis and medical malpractice, and the potential impact of these items on the defense and the case. The team discussed potential subrogation to Hometown Bank, and Amy sought clarification on the meaning and implications of recent reports and updates, while also reaffirming her positions regarding the relevance of certain financial and medical documentation to the case.

item_543.note: Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2405]. Client: Amy Mills. Update communications were sent and exchanged in August 2022 between Amy Mills and attorney Sara(ena) M. Tuttle of The Whaley Law Firm regarding a work-capacity assessment and financial documentation, with the most recent update dated August 24, 2022.

On August 24, 2022 at 12:20 p.m. Eastern Time, Sara(ena) M. Tuttle wrote to Amy Mills with an update. In the body of the message, she explained that the FCE (functional capacity evaluation) report indicates Amy is able to perform sedentary work. She defined sedentary work as low-level physical work performed mostly while seated, with occasional walking or standing, and with no lifting over 10 pounds. The report also notes that, despite restrictions on the types of work Amy can perform, she is able to undertake jobs that are not physically demanding—examples given were desk-based employment or running her business (handling paperwork) while employees actually provide the required services. An economist has been arranged by Aaron to evaluate the financial impact of Amy’s restrictions, and a further update will be provided once the economist’s assessment is available.

The update also states that the firm is awaiting certain documents: Amy’s tax returns for 2016 and 2017, as well as the official date on which Bella Beauty ceased operations. The note acknowledges that Amy’s equipment and products remain at her location and she retains possession of the premises, but it requires the date of the last services performed. The message reiterates that while Amy may have concerns about providing financial information, the requested information is necessary to determine the proper amount of compensation she should receive. The firm advised that if Amy does not currently possess the tax documents, she should contact the IRS and/or her tax preparer to obtain copies as soon as possible. The message closes by inviting Amy to contact them with any questions. The email signature provides Sara(ena) M. Tuttle’s contact details and notes a new email address: Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com, along with the firm’s address (712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222), direct line [PHONE-2406], office line [PHONE-2407] ext. 104.

The message thread shows an email sequence surrounding the same topic. Earlier on August 24, 2022 at 10:11 a.m., Amy Mills replied with a query: “What exactly does that report mean?” seeking clarification about the contents and implications of the FCE report.

In the same thread, on August 19, 2022 at 1:49 p.m., Amy Mills wrote that she understood she might have to file bankruptcy because she could not remain in business, and she mentioned that a bank representative indicated the paid electric bill could count toward a balance forgiven under the PPP. She remarked that she was unable to stay open and questioned the relevance of these items to her case.

Earlier on August 19, 2022 at 11:36 a.m., Sarena Tuttle responded to Amy. She explained that the defense is entitled to know whether Amy recovered lost wages during her recovery and, since a PPP loan was received to cover lost wages, the defense would not be responsible for those “lost wages” if they were not actually lost (i.e., they were offset by the PPP funds). She indicated the defense would likely subrogate, meaning reimburse Hometown Bank for those funds. She said she would forward Amy’s email to Aaron for further addresses and attached a copy of the FCE Report received on August 16, 2022, apologizing for not sending it sooner.

Later on August 19, 2022 at 11:08 a.m., Amy Mills replied with several concerns. She stated she was glad the PPP loan information had been obtained but that, since she was no longer in business, bankruptcy would be necessary because the bank’s calculations were based on the 2019 tax year; she noted that she had been able to obtain additional PPP funds but was unsure whether she would medically be able to continue. She stressed that the PPP information should not have been entered into her case. She also complained that she had not received an updated FCE report or its findings. Amy expressed frustration that the defense might be attempting to minimize payments and referenced an MRI report indicating a current acute tear in her arm, but that doctors were not aligned on treatment. She described ongoing medical issues, including four years of symptoms with days she cannot get out of bed and migraines, and she criticized the medical care she received as a result of misdiagnosis, arguing that this prolonged pain was caused by earlier errors. She claimed to be receiving only medication and continued symptoms without a satisfactory remedy.

On August 19, 2022 at 10:15 a.m., Sarena Tuttle replied that they had received the FCE Report and that Aaron had forwarded it to the defense for review. She also stated that documents from Hometown Bank regarding Amy’s PPP loans were under review and that she would contact Aaron for a more detailed update.

Finally, on August 19, 2022 at 10:10 a.m., Amy Mills asked for an update on the progress of the matter.

Throughout these communications, the parties discussed the functional capacity assessment, the status and handling of PPP loan information, potential bankruptcy considerations, the need for tax documents from 2016 and 2017, the date Bella Beauty closed, and the ongoing medical issues, with requests for last services data and copies of tax documents to determine appropriate compensation. The correspondence included the Whaley Law Firm’s contact information, and the new email address for Saraena Tuttle.

item_544.note: In the case titled Amy Mills Premise [DOB-2409], with the client identified as Amy Mills, an email chain dated August 22, 2022 concerns Re: Amy Mills. On that date, at 12:44:12 PM Eastern Daylight Time, Sarena M. Tuttle of The Whaley Law Firm sent an email from sarena@whaleylawfirm.com to [EMAIL-2410], with a cc to [EMAIL-2411]. The message states that Amy did not file taxes in 2020 and that all tax returns Amy claims to be able to find have been provided. Amy will not give a straight answer about when Bella Beauty actually closed; she only says that she has not worked since the DOI and that her belongings are still at her location awaiting her return. Sarena notes that she does not believe the DOI can be used as the closure date since Amy applied for and received PPP loans during 2020. She describes Amy as not very cooperative when spoken to, often digressing to discuss why the information is needed and how they are trying to get out of paying her, but never answering the questions or providing any additional documents. 

Sarena M. Tuttle
The Whaley Law Firm
712 Lyndon Lane
Louisville, KY 40222
D: [PHONE-2412]
O: [PHONE-2413] ext 104
E: Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com
(PLEASE NOTE NEW EMAIL ADDRESS)

On the same day, at 12:31 PM, Aaron Whaley replied with, “I assume we do not have these tax returns because she did not file? Do we know the last date Bella Beauty operated?”

Aaron Whaley
Whaley Law Firm
712 Lyndon Ln
Louisville, KY 40222
[PHONE-2414]

item_546.note: Case: Amy Mills v. Forcht Bank; Client: Amy Mills. This matter is filed under Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2422]. On August 22, 2022, a pair of email messages regarding the case were exchanged between Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm and Betsy Catron of Vine Center, Lexington, KY, with the original messages copied to Sarena Tuttle and to Amy Mills Premise’s file email address. The subject of the correspondence was the Amy Mills v. Forcht Bank matter.

In Betsy Catron’s 10:10 a.m. email on August 22, 2022, she noted that she had requested records and bills from Ms. Mills’ Norton providers and understood that Ms. Mills underwent an MRI at the end of May; however, none of those Norton providers had any records of recent treatment. She stated they were still waiting on records and bills from Baptist Health Corbin and would provide copies upon receipt. She asked Aaron to advise if there was any other recent medical treatment of Ms. Mills. Betsy further inquired about the delayed late-July Functional Capacity Evaluation (FCE) and when they would be able to obtain a demand. She also mentioned that they had requested Ms. Mills’ tax returns for 2016, 2018 and 2020 several times, and that the lack of this information significantly limited their ability to evaluate her future earning impairment claim. Additionally, she asked for the last day Bella Beauty was in business and closed by inviting any questions or discussion, providing her contact details including her title as Attorney, the Vine Center address (333 West Vine Street, Suite 1100, Lexington, KY 40507), telephone number [PHONE-2423]), fax number [PHONE-2424]), and the website (www.whtlaw.com).

In the later 12:29 p.m. email that same day, Aaron Whaley responded to Betsy, saying he would forward any records they might be missing. He confirmed that Amy had completed the FCE and that he had attached a copy. He added that he was in the process of conducting an economic analysis and thanked her. The message was addressed from Aaron Whaley at Whaley Law Firm (712 Lyndon Ln, Louisville, KY 40222; [PHONE-2425]) to Betsy Catron at WHT Law, with cc to Sarena Tuttle and the Amy Mills Premise file address. The exchange confirms ongoing efforts to gather records, finalize the FCE, and progress an economic analysis in connection with the case.

item_547.note: On August 22, 2022, Betsy R. Catron, an attorney with WHT Law, emailed Aaron Whaley and Sarena Tuttle of the Whaley Law Firm regarding Amy Mills v. Forcht Bank. She stated that they had requested records and bills from Ms. Mills’ Norton providers because they understood she underwent an MRI at the end of May. To date, none of those providers have any records of recent treatment for her. They are still waiting on Ms. Mills’ records and bills from Baptist Health Corbin and will provide copies of those upon receipt. She asked to advise if there is any other recent medical treatment of Ms. Mills.

She also noted that you had previously advised of a Functional Capacity Evaluation (FCE) occurring in late July, asking whether it occurred as planned and what your thoughts are on when you will be able to provide a demand. Additionally, they have requested your client’s tax returns for 2016, 2018, and 2020 several times, stating that the lack of this information significantly limits their ability to evaluate her future earning impairment claim. They also need to know the last day that Bella Beauty was in business. If there are questions or if you would like to discuss the above in further detail, please do not hesitate to contact me.

Sincerely,
Betsy R. Catron
Attorney, WHT Law
Vine Center, 333 West Vine Street, Suite 1100, Lexington, KY 40507

item_548.note: Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2430]. Client: Amy Mills.

On August 19, 2022, Amy Mills emailed her attorney, Sarena M. Tuttle, regarding an update in the case. In her message (sent at 1:49:25 PM), Amy explained that she will have to pay bankruptcy because she was unable to keep the business open, and she noted that a bank representative also said the electric bill that had been paid would count toward the balance to be forgiven on the PPP.

Earlier that same day, at 11:36 AM, Sarena replied to Amy. She explained that the defense is entitled to know whether Amy recovered lost wages during her recovery and, if so, from whom. Since Amy had received a PPP loan to cover lost wages, the defense would not be responsible for those lost wages because they were not truly lost. The defense would most likely have to subrogate, meaning reimburse Hometown Bank for the funds provided. Sarena also said she would forward Amy’s email to Aaron for further address, and she attached a copy of the FCE Report received on [DOB-2431], apologizing for the delay in sending it.

Earlier still, at 11:08 AM, Amy had written expressing some frustration. She noted that it was good the PPP loan information had been obtained, but since she is no longer in business she would have to file bankruptcy because the bank’s determination used the 2019 tax year. She also indicated she had been able to obtain more PPP funds but was not sure if she would medically be able to be healed in time to continue. She stated she did not believe the PPP information should be entered into her case or presented to the defense. She complained that she had not received an updated report for the FCE or its findings, and she asserted that the defense appeared to be grasping for any possible means to avoid paying as much as possible. She referenced an MRI report indicating a current acute tear in her arm, but said doctors were not aligned to agree on treatment. She claimed medical malpractice due to an initial misdiagnosis in the ER, which she believed prolonged treatment, pain, and suffering. She wrote that this situation had been ongoing for almost four years, with days when she could not get out of bed and migraines so severe she felt as if she were dying, and that she mostly received medication with continued symptoms and no real remedy.

At 10:15 AM, Sarena sent another update to Amy. She stated that the FCE Report had been received and forwarded to the defense for their review. She noted that documents from Hometown Bank regarding Amy’s PPP loans were also under review. She acknowledged that Aaron had been in correspondence with the defense and that she would obtain a more detailed update from him.

Earlier, at 10:10 AM, Amy had messaged asking for an update on progress, signing off with her name and a Franklin quotation: “The heart of a fool is in his mouth, but the mouth of a wise man is in his heart.” The exchange also included further repeated signature lines from Amy Mills with the same Benjamin Franklin quotation.

This sequence of communications reflects the ongoing handling of reported lost wages, PPP loan implications, potential subrogation to a bank, and concerns about medical issues, misdiagnosis, and malpractice in the context of the case.

item_550.note: Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2434]. Client: Amy Mills.

On August 19, 2022, in a communication from Sarena M. Tuttle of The Whaley Law Firm to Amy Mills, the firm acknowledged Amy’s concerns and explained the defense’s rights in this matter. Sarena stated that the defense is entitled to know whether Amy recovered lost wages during her recovery and, if so, from whom. Because Amy had obtained a Paycheck Protection Program (PPP) loan to cover lost wages, Sarena understood that the defense would not be responsible for those “lost wages,” since they were funded by the loan. The defense would likely have to subrogate, reimbursing Hometown Bank for the funds provided. Sarena also indicated she would forward Amy’s email to Aaron for a more detailed address. She attached a copy of the FCE Report received on August 16, 2022 and apologized for not sending it sooner. The message included the Whaley Law Firm’s contact information and note of Sarena’s new email address: Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com.

Earlier that day, at 10:10 AM, Amy Mills had sent a message containing a link: https://www.google.com/maps/search/712get an update on where we r with the progress. She wrote that she was glad the PPP loan information had been obtained, noting that because she was no longer in business, she would have to file bankruptcy since they used 2019 tax year data, and she had also been able to obtain more PPP funds but was not sure whether she would medically be healed in time to continue. Amy asserted that this information should not be brought to the defense or entered into her case. She complained that she had not received an updated FCE report or its findings, and she expressed concern that the defense was “reaching” to find anything at all to reduce payment. She referenced an MRI report indicating a current acute tear in her arm and described difficulty getting doctors aligned to provide treatment. She also claimed potential medical malpractice due to misdiagnosis at her initial ER treatment, which she believed prolonged her pain and suffering, arguing that this would not have happened if the accident had not occurred. She described her situation as ongoing for almost four years, with days when she could not get out of bed and migraines so severe she felt like she was dying, and she lamented that she was receiving only medications and persistent symptoms without a remedy.

At 10:15 AM, in reply to Amy’s earlier message, Sarena acknowledged receipt of the FCE Report and advised that Aaron had forwarded it to the defense for their review. She confirmed that documents from Hometown Bank regarding Amy’s PPP loans had also been received and were under review. Sarena noted that Aaron had been in correspondence with the defense, but she did not have the details of those conversations at that time; she stated she would reach out to him for a more detailed update. The message included Sarena’s signature block with the firm’s address (712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222) and direct lines, and again reminded that the new email address should be used.

Later, at 11:08 AM, Amy replied again, reiterating her position that it was beneficial to have the PPP loan information but highlighting personal circumstances. She explained that since she was no longer in business, she would need to consider bankruptcy because of the 2019 tax year, and she noted she had obtained additional PPP funds but remained uncertain about her medical prognosis. She insisted that this information should not affect her case, expressed dissatisfaction with not receiving an updated FCE report or findings, and criticized the defense for trying to locate anything to minimize their liability. She reiterated the MRI findings of a current acute tear in her arm and the lack of consensus among doctors regarding treatment. She pressed for accountability regarding alleged medical malpractice from an initial ER misdiagnosis, asserting that the misdiagnosis had prolonged her pain and suffering. She emphasized the ongoing nature of the ordeal—nearly four years—with days when she cannot get out of bed and debilitating migraines, and she complained that she continues to receive only medication and symptoms without a proper remedy.

The thread also included the exchange’s closing quotation from Amy Mills, quoting Benjamin Franklin: “The heart of a fool is in his mouth, but the mouth of a wise man is in his heart.” The communication chain reflects the ongoing efforts to update Amy on FCE-related findings, PPP loan documentation, and potential liability considerations, while also documenting Amy’s concerns about medical treatment, alleged medical malpractice, and the impact of these issues on her case. The case file preserves all names, dates, documents, and contact details, including the instructive note about Sarena’s new email address and the various ways to reach the involved parties.

item_551.note: Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2436]. Client: Amy Mills.

On August 19, 2022, at 10:10 a.m., Amy Mills wrote to Sarena M. Tuttle of The Whaley Law Firm, asking for an update on progress. In her message, she stated she was glad the PPP loan information had been obtained, noting that since she is no longer in business she would have to file bankruptcy because the loans were based on the 2019 tax year. She also mentioned that she had been able to obtain more PPP funds but was unsure whether she would be medically able to be healed in time to continue. She expressed the view that this information should not be entered into her case, and she complained that she had not been given an updated report for the FCE or its findings. She alleged that the defense was grasping for anything they could to minimize the amount they would have to pay. She referenced an MRI report stating a current acute tear in her arm, but said doctors were not on the same page to obtain treatment. She further asserted that the defendants should be held responsible for medical malpractice due to a misdiagnosis at the initial ER treatment, which she claimed prolonged her treatment, pain, and suffering, and she argued that such consequences would not have happened if the misdiagnosis had not occurred in the first place. She stated that this has been ongoing for almost four years, during which she has had days when she cannot get out of bed and experiences migraines so severe that she feels she is dying. She concluded that she primarily receives medication and continued symptoms but no remedy to the problem. Her signature included the line: “The heart of a fool is in his mouth, but the mouth of a wise man is in his heart.” — Benjamin Franklin.

At 10:15 a.m. the same day, Sarena responded to Amy, explaining that they had received the FCE Report and that Aaron had forwarded it to the defense for their review. She also noted that documents from Hometown Bank regarding Amy’s PPP loans had been received and were under review. She acknowledged that Aaron had been in correspondence with the defense, but she did not have the details of those conversations and would reach out to him for a more detailed update. Her message included the firm’s contact information and a note to their client, including the firm’s Louisville address and phone numbers, and the reminder to note a new email address: sarena@whaleylawfirm.com.

Earlier that same day, at 10:10 a.m., Amy had sent another brief message to Sarena asking for an update on progress, signing as Amy Mills and reiterating the quoted Franklin line in her signature.

Later, at 11:08 a.m., Amy continued to correspond, reiterating her concerns and elaborating on the lack of an updated FCE report and the ongoing dispute over the handling of the PPP loan information, the possibility of bankruptcy based on the 2019 tax year, and the medical issues including the MRI-documented arm tear, the need for coordinated medical treatment, and the alleged medical malpractice stemming from an ER misdiagnosis that she claimed has continued to cause pain and suffering for nearly four years. She reiterated that her condition includes days when she cannot get out of bed and severe migraines, and that she has primarily received medication without a lasting remedy.

On Friday, August 19, 2022, at 10:10 a.m., Amy Mills sent an email to Sarena Tuttle at The Whaley Law Firm ([EMAIL-2439]) asking for an update on progress. In her message, she wrote, “Hey Sarena I was just wanting to get an update on where we r with the progress. Thank You, Amy Mills,” and signed with her name, followed by the quote: “The heart of a fool is in his mouth, but the mouth of a wise man is in his heart.” — Benjamin Franklin.

Later that morning, at 10:15:44 a.m., Sarena M. Tuttle replied from The Whaley Law Firm (sarena@whaleylawfirm.com) to Amy Mills. She informed her that the FCE Report had been received and forwarded to the defense for their review, and that documents from Hometown Bank concerning Amy’s PPP loans had also been received and are under review at this time. She noted that Aaron had been in correspondence with the defense, but she did not have the details of those conversations and would reach out to him for a more detailed update. The message included Sarena’s contact details: The Whaley Law Firm, 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222; Direct: [PHONE-2440]; Office: [PHONE-2441] ext 104; Email: Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com, with a parenthetical note reading “PLEASE NOTE NEW EMAIL ADDRESS.”

item_563.note: Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2500]. Client: Amy Mills. This note documents the email correspondence regarding an FCE appointment reminder for July 25, 2022 at 11:00 a.m., and related HIPAA authorization matters.

On July 15, 2022, Amy Mills sent an email from [EMAIL-2501] to Sarena Tuttle at Whaley Law Firm (sarena@whaleylawfirm.com) with the subject “Re: FCE Appointment Reminder - [DOB-2502] 11am.” In her message, Amy wrote: “I think so but I’m not sure I will check And let u for for sure and details (I think I did tho).”

Earlier in the thread, on July 11, 2022 at 1:00 p.m., Sarena Tuttle replied: “Good afternoon Amy, I did receive the HIPAA back. Thank you. I just need to confirm whether or not you applied for a PPP loan and if so, what was the outcome?” At the time, Sarena’s contact details appeared as: The Whaley Law Firm, 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222, D: [PHONE-2503], O: [PHONE-2504], E: Sarena@Whttps://www.google.com/maps/search/712MAIL ADDRESS) (note the garbled email address line).

Earlier still, on July 11, 2022 at 12:50 p.m., Amy Mills wrote: “I think that it did. I think I have it signed now Just check it and let me know you have all you need and then we will go from there.”

On July 7, 2022, at 2:57 p.m., Sarena Tuttle replied: “Your welcome. It would have come from Docusign but I will resend it in case it went to spam or something.” In that message her signature stated: Sarena M. Tuttle, The Whaley Law Firm, 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222, D: [PHONE-2505], O: [PHONE-2506], E: Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com (PLEASE NOTE NEW EMAIL ADDRESS).

Earlier still, on July 7, 2022 at 2:06 p.m., Amy Mills wrote: “Thank you for your email on tha Appointment details for July 25th. You mentioned signing a new medical release form but I have not seen an email on that.”

And on July 5, 2022 at 10:25 a.m., Sarena Tuttle wrote: “Good morning Amy, Thank you for speaking with me this morning, please find your appointment details attached. Your FCE will be held at BioKinetics located at 9900 Corporate Campus Drive, Suite 2400, Louisville, KY 40223. As discussed, please execute the HIPAA authorization sent to you via DocuSign so we can request your updated medical records and I will update you as soon as I am able to find out more about the health insurance issue. If you have any questions, please do not hesitate to contact us to discuss them in further detail.” The signature block for that message again listed Sarena M. Tuttle, The Whaley Law Firm, 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222, D: [PHONE-2507], O: [PHONE-2508], E: Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com (PLEASE NOTE NEW EMAIL ADDRESS).

In summary, the case involves Amy Mills coordinating an FCE appointment for July 25, 2022 at 11:00 a.m., with follow-up regarding HIPAA authorization (DocuSign), whether Amy had applied for a PPP loan and the outcome of that application, and related questions about a medical release form. The FCE was scheduled to be held at BioKinetics, 9900 Corporate Campus Drive, Suite 2400, Louisville, KY 40223. The ongoing communications include confirmations of HIPAA back, discussions of DocuSign actions, potential spam concerns, and references to updated medical records and health insurance issues.

item_564.note: Case: Amy Mills – Premise file dated [DOB-2510]. Client: Amy Mills. Re: FCE Appointment Reminder for July 25, 2022 at 11:00 a.m.

Between July 5 and July 17, 2022, an email thread circulated between Amy Mills and Sarena M. Tuttle of The Whaley Law Firm regarding an upcoming Functional Capacity Evaluation (FCE) and related documents. The subject indicated a reminder for an FCE appointment scheduled for July 25, 2022 at 11:00 a.m., to be held at BioKinetics, located at 9900 Corporate Campus Drive, Suite 2400, Louisville, KY 40223. The thread also references HIPAA authorization and requests for updated medical records, as well as a health insurance issue.

On July 5, 2022, Sarena Tuttle sent Amy Mills appointment details and noted that Amy should execute the HIPAA authorization sent via DocuSign so the firm could obtain updated medical records. Sarena stated she would update Amy as soon as more information became available about the health insurance issue. In her signature, Sarena provided her contact information for The Whaley Law Firm—712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222—with direct and office phone numbers and her email address Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com, and she reminded that this was a new email address.

On July 7, 2022 at 2:06 p.m., Amy Mills replied that she believed the information was correct and that she would review it, adding that she would confirm and provide details as she thought she had done so.

Later that same day, at 2:57 p.m., Sarena responded that she appreciated Amy’s note and that the HIPAA document would have come from DocuSign; she offered to resend it in case it had gone to spam.

On July 11, 2022, at 12:50 p.m., Amy Mills wrote that she believed the DocuSign item had been sent, and that she thought she had signed it, but she would verify and let the firm know if all needed items were in order.

At 1:00 p.m. that day, Sarena replied that she had received the HIPAA item back and thanked Amy. She then requested confirmation of whether Amy had applied for a PPP loan and, if so, what the outcome was.

On July 15, 2022, at 11:16 p.m., Amy Mills responded that she thought she had completed what was required but was not completely sure and would check, asking to be informed for certainty and any needed details.

Finally, on July 17, 2022, at 6:41 p.m., Sarena signed off asking Amy to confirm the outcome once more.

Signature blocks in the exchanges repeatedly identified Sarena M. Tuttle as The Whaley Law Firm representative, with the firm’s Louisville address (712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222) and contact numbers (D: [PHONE-2511]; O: [PHONE-2512]) and Sarena’s email (Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com). The thread also shows Amy Mills’ primary contact as [EMAIL-2513]. The messages reference a new email address for Sarena, noted in her signature as part of the firm’s contact details.

On Monday, July 11, 2022, at 09:35:02 -0700, DocuSign via [EMAIL-2517] sent a message to Aaron Whaley at [EMAIL-2518] regarding this document. The email includes a link to review the document your recipients have signed and any related payments: https://na4.docusign.net/Member/EmailStart.aspx?m=a5e420a2-49cd-4032-8a83-b0578b18dfb6. If the link does not work, you can highlight and copy that entire line and paste it into your browser to get started.

The message was sent by Aaron Whaley, who is using the DocuSign Electronic Signature Service. If you would prefer not to receive email from this sender, you may contact the sender with your request. Do Not Share This Email: this email contains a secure link to DocuSign, and you should not share the email, link, or access code with others. If you have questions about the document or need to modify it, reach out to the sender by emailing them directly. There is also an option labeled Declining to sign and a section for Managing notifications.

If you are having trouble signing the document, you can visit the Help with Signing page in the DocuSign Support Center at https://support.docusign.com/articles/How-do-I-sign-a-DocuSign-document-Basic-Signing.

For questions about the document, the message advised contacting the sender directly by email. item_568.note: Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2525]. Client: Amy Mills. The note is labeled Thursday mediation.

The email record shows that it was sent from [EMAIL-2526] to sarena@whaleylawfirm.com (listed as "'sarena@whaleylawfirm.com'"). The CC field is empty, and the email was dated Friday, July 8, 2022, at 16:26:49 +0000.

In the body of the message, Thomas Knopf invites the recipient to a scheduled Zoom meeting. The subject line appears as Thomas Knopf's Zoom Meeting. The meeting is scheduled for July 14, 2022, at 01:30 PM Eastern Time (US and Canada). To join, the Zoom meeting link is https://us02web.zoom.us/j/[PHONE-2527]. item_571.note: Initial client contact on April 26, 2019, with Amy Mills. This morning she called to report that she is having a bone scan with dye today. Her PCP had requested three additional images—neck, shoulder, and arm—but PHI would only approve one, so an MRI of the arm only was completed. Something was noted on her arm, and she is now having a bone scan to determine its origin. She will call us with any additional information once it is determined.

She agreed to complete the HIPAA form emailed to her and requested an email reminder of the FCE location. A copy was sent to #cc.

item_572.note: In the Amy Mills case file (Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2571]), the client, Amy Mills, was scheduled for a Functional Capacity Evaluation (FCE) to be conducted at BioKinetics in Louisville, Kentucky. The appointment was set for Monday, July 25, 2022 at 11:00 a.m., at 9900 Corporate Campus Drive, Suite 2400, Louisville, KY 40223. This was confirmed in an FCE Appointment Reminder for July 25, 2022 at 11:00 a.m.

The reminder originated in an email sent on Tuesday, July 5, 2022 at 10:26:10 a.m. Eastern Time from Sarena Tuttle of The Whaley Law Firm to Amy Mills ([EMAIL-2572]), with Aaron Whaley CC’d ([EMAIL-2573]). The message thanked Amy for speaking with the sender that morning and stated that appointment details were attached. It noted that the FCE would be held at BioKinetics at the address above, and it requested that Amy execute a HIPAA authorization sent via DocuSign so they could request updated medical records. The sender indicated she would update Amy as soon as she learned more about an outstanding health insurance issue, and she invited Amy to contact them with any questions. The note included the sender’s contact information: Sarena M. Tuttle, The Whaley Law Firm, 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222; D: [PHONE-2574]; O: [PHONE-2575]; E: Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com, with a parenthetical note to “PLEASE NOTE NEW EMAIL ADDRESS.”

Separately, the record shows that the FCE appointment was rescheduled. The entry notes: “Appointment Rescheduled for The Whaley Law Firm Amy Mills [PHONE-2576],” with the specifics that the appointment was for a Functional Capacity Evaluation (Louisville, KY) Biokinetics. The old time was Monday, May 9, 2022 at 11:00 a.m., and the new time became Monday, July 25, 2022 at 11:00 a.m., with the same location: 9900 Corporate Campus Drive, Suite 2400, Louisville, KY 40223. The note confirms that the appointment has been successfully rescheduled. Additional options listed include Change/Cancel Appointment and the ability to add the event to iCal/Outlook, Google Calendar, or to unsubscribe.

item_574.note: In the case of Mills, Amy v. Forcht Bank, Amy Mills is the client. The Knox Circuit Court processed and accepted a filing on June 30, 2022, in Case No. 20-CI-00112, envelope number 4772194. The case caption reads “MILLS, AMY VS. FORCHT BANK.” The filing is identified in the court system as NCP KNOX 20-CI-00112.

The eFiling was submitted by Betsy R. Catron, attorney for the defendant. The notice indicates that the filing was processed on June 30, 2022, at 12:54 PM Eastern. The electronic notification was sent from [EMAIL-2583] and was addressed to the following recipients: Aaron Gregory Whaley ([EMAIL-2584]), Betsy R. Catron ([EMAIL-2585]), and Joshua Ellis ([EMAIL-2586]). The case caption is Mills, Amy vs. Forcht Bank, Case No. 20-CI-00112, Envelope Number 4772194. The notice confirms that the following documents were included in the eFiling: MOTION - OTHER and TENDERED DOCUMENT. The documents can be viewed at the Kentucky Court of Justice eFiling retrieval link: https://kcoj.kycourts.net/eFilingRetrieval/Home/Package?id=BCF4311D-D742-4D7B-8A12-095598964DD2. The notice also states that this message was automatically generated by the Kentucky Court of Justice eFiling system and that the email should not be replied to.

A Motion Hour is scheduled for July 11, 2022 at 9:00 AM ET in the Circuit Courtroom, before Hon. Michael Caperton (Hon. MICHAEL CAPERTON).

The message concludes with the Kentucky Court of Justice Confidentiality Notice, which states that the message and any attachments are intended only for the addressee and may contain information that is privileged, confidential, and/or proprietary work product. If you are not the intended recipient, you should not read, copy, retain, or disseminate the message or attachments, and you should not forward them without express written consent of the sender. If you have received the message in error, you should contact the sender immediately and delete all copies. Transmission or misdelivery does not waive any applicable legal privilege.

Financials: On June 22, 2022, Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm sent an email regarding Amy’s finances. The message was from [EMAIL-2612] to [EMAIL-2613], with a CC to sarena@whaleylawfirm.com (Sarena Tuttle) and to [EMAIL-2614]. The date on the message is Wednesday, June 22, 2022, at 12:57:40 PM, -0400. In the body of the email, Whaley wrote: “Amy, I tried calling you and your voicemail is full. I know you have given us all you have on your financials but I just wanted to see if you applied for a PPP loan for bella beauty? If so did you get one? Defense counsel is seeking that information. Thanks.” The email is signed by: Aaron Whaley, Whaley Law Firm, 712 Lyndon Ln, Louisville, KY 40222, [PHONE-2615].

item_588.note: Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2632]. The client is Amy Mills. In reviewing this file, mediation is set for July 14, 2022; however, Amy rescheduled her FCE and will not be seen until July 25, 2022. Would you like to reschedule mediation for late August so the report will have plenty of time to be received and reviewed? This note was addressed to @aaronw.
