item_590.note: This is a case note for Amy Mills in the Mills v. Forcht Bank matter, dated in the file as Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2636]. The correspondence involves The Whaley Law Firm and WHT Law (Vine Center) and centers on obtaining discovery materials and scheduling issues related to Amy Mills.

On June 20, 2022 at 13:55:23 -0400, Sarena M. Tuttle of The Whaley Law Firm wrote to Betsy R. Catron, Attorney at Vine Center (WHT Law), with Aaron Whaley copied on the email. The message stated: “Good afternoon Betsy, we just wanted to touch base and let you know we are working on obtaining updated medical records and will forward them as they come in.” The email was signed by Sarena M. Tuttle and included her contact information: The Whaley Law Firm, 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222; direct line [PHONE-2637]; office line [PHONE-2638]; email Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com, with a note to please note the new email address.

The chain also includes an earlier message from Betsy Catron dated June 6, 2022 at 1:23 PM. In that message, Betsy addressed Aaron Whaley with the question, “Hi Aaron, have you had any luck getting your client’s July 29 FCE moved to an earlier date?” Betsy then stated that they needed Amy Mills’s tax returns for 2016, 2018, and 2020, and that they would also need any medical treatment records and bills from December 16, 2021 to the present, noting that December 16, 2021 was the last date of treatment they had. She added that mediation was currently set with Judge Knopf and that she could contact the judge’s office for additional mediation dates. The message closed with “Thank you,” and was signed by Betsy R. Catron, Attorney, with the Vine Center address listed as 333 West Vine Street, Suite 1100, Lexington, KY 40507. The firm’s contact details included phone number [PHONE-2639], fax number [PHONE-2640], and the website www.whtlaw.com.

item_591.note: Case: Amy Mills. This case file, titled Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2642], concerns the client Amy Mills. The latest correspondence is a June 20, 2022 email from Sarena M. Tuttle of The Whaley Law Firm to Amy Mills ([EMAIL-2643]), with Aaron Whaley copied. The subject was “Re: Case Update - Additional Documents & Information Request.” The message states that the firm had attempted to reach Amy again that day, but the recording indicated her voice mailbox was full and no message could be left. They had also emailed with no response, and Sarena emphasizes that, at this stage of litigation, communication is imperative and there is urgent information to discuss. She asks Amy to contact the office as soon as possible regarding her claim. The signature block includes The Whaley Law Firm’s address at 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222, the direct line [PHONE-2644], the office line [PHONE-2645], and the email Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com, with a note indicating this is a new email address.

Earlier in the same case, on June 10, 2022, at 12:29 PM, Sarena Tuttle sent an email to Amy. In that message, Sarena wrote that they had attempted to contact Amy that morning and the call went to voicemail but the voicemail was full, so they could not leave a message. She asked Amy to contact them as soon as possible to discuss the current status of her claim and to review her medical treatment to date. Sarena also reminded Amy that they had discussed tax information several times and that the Defense still needed Amy’s 2016 and 2018 tax documents, as well as the date that Bella Beauty closed and stopped accepting clients. She requested that Amy locate and/or request copies of these documents so the Defense could provide them, noting that these documents are required to calculate any lost income and loss of future earnings to which Amy may be entitled. The email closed with Sarena’s standard signature and contact information, including the new email address.

Approximately an hour later, on June 10, 2022 at 1:23 PM, Sarena Tuttle sent another message to Amy. This time, she acknowledged that Amy had been having issues locating past tax information and explained that they had located an IRS website that would allow Amy to either request or download copies of past tax information. Sarena provided the link to the IRS site (https://www.irs.gov/individuals/get-transcript) and invited Amy to set up an account and request the missing documents for the Defense Counsel. The message concluded with Sarena’s signature block, again listing The Whaley Law Firm’s address, the direct and office phone numbers, and the email Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com, along with the note to “PLEASE NOTE NEW EMAIL ADDRESS.”

Forcht Bank. In that email, Aaron states, “Thus far unsuccessful. We will update all records.”

Earlier that same day, at 1:23 PM, Betsy Catron wrote to Aaron. Her message began with a question about the client’s July 29 FCE: “Have you had any luck getting your client’s July 29 FCE moved to an earlier date?” She proceeded to list documents and records needed: tax returns for 2016, 2018, and 2020; medical treatment records and bills from December 16, 2021 to the present, which is the last date of treatment that they have. She noted that mediation is presently set with Judge Knopf and added that she can contact his office for additional dates for mediation. The message is signed by Betsy R. item_597.note: This note concerns the Amy Mills case (Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2664]). On May 31, 2022, at 2:39 PM, Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm asked if there was any way Amy Mills’s Functional Capacity Evaluation (FCE) could be moved up.

In response, Sarena M. Tuttle of The Whaley Law Firm replied on June 1, 2022 at 9:57 AM: “No, I do not believe so.” She explained that the FCE had been scheduled and that Brian or Jeremy had reached out about moving it up because the doctor would be out of town. They accepted the earlier appointment, but Amy called the providers and rescheduled it on her own. She added that she was unsure when the doctor was leaving, but when she tried to schedule an IME for Mrs. Miller, the website showed the IME slots were booked out through September.

Sarena’s signature follows:
Sarena M. Tuttle
The Whaley Law Firm
712 Lyndon Lane
Louisville, KY 40222
D: [PHONE-2665]
O: [PHONE-2666]
E: Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com
(PLEASE NOTE NEW EMAIL ADDRESS)

item_604.note: Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2685]. Client: Amy Mills.

In this case, the client Amy Mills had an appointment for a Functional Capacity Evaluation with Biokinetics in Louisville, Kentucky. The original appointment was scheduled for Monday, May 9, 2022 at 11:00 a.m. at 9900 Corporate Campus Drive, Suite 2400, Louisville, KY 40223.

On May 9, 2022, Amy Mills requested to reschedule because she planned to undergo additional tests on May 29 and preferred to wait. The rescheduled appointment was set to Monday, July 25, 2022 at 11:00 a.m., still for the Functional Capacity Evaluation with Biokinetics in Louisville, KY. The rescheduling was processed by Commonwealth IME via mobile app and was coordinated with The Whaley Law Firm Amy Mills [PHONE-2686]. The new time is 11:00 a.m. EDT on July 25, 2022, replacing the original 11:00 a.m. time on May 9, 2022.

The rescheduled appointment details were communicated in an email that confirmed the new time and location: Monday, July 25, 2022 at 11:00 a.m. at 9900 Corporate Campus Drive, Suite 2400, Louisville, KY 40223, for a Functional Capacity Evaluation with Biokinetics. The notice indicated the appointment had been successfully rescheduled and included standard calendar options to add the appointment to iCal/Outlook or Google Calendar, and noted that the system is powered by Acuity Scheduling, with an option to unsubscribe.

item_605.note: This note covers communications between Amy Mills and Sarena M. Tuttle of The Whaley Law Firm regarding Amy’s neuroSurgery appointment and related imaging. The thread begins with an email from Sarena on April 26, 2022, in which she advised Amy to contact her neurosurgery provider and request updated imaging as recommended by Dr. Gregory Nazar, attaching a copy of Dr. Nazar’s addendum for the physician’s review. In reply, later that same day, Amy indicated that May 3 would be “a week from today” and that May 3 at 5:30 p.m. was her first available slot; she planned to send a follow-up email confirming when her MRI appointment would be set. Sarena acknowledged with “Sounds good, thank you!”

Earlier that same day, Amy had written that the May 3 appointment was the first available, and she intended to send an email attaching the MRI appointment details once they were set. In response, Sarena again confirmed and Amy reiterated her plan to notify once the MRI appointment was scheduled.

Subsequently, on May 6, 2022, Amy reported that she had attended an appointment with Teresa, who ordered the scans but, as of that time, they had not been able to schedule her in yet; Amy asked whether they should reschedule the neurosurgery appointment that week or the following week. Later on May 6, Sarena requested the location information for the scheduled imaging, providing her firm’s Louisville contact details (The Whaley Law Firm, 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222; D: [PHONE-2688]; O: [PHONE-2689]; E: Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com; note the new email address).

Amy answered, informing that the imaging was scheduled for May 26, 2022 at 1:30 p.m. She explained that the imaging center was very backed up and that she would require three scans to be completed together, which would take longer, but that May 26 was the soonest available slot at the diagnostic center to obtain the MRI.

On May 7, 2022, Amy clarified to Sarena that the imaging center is Corbin Imaging and Diagnostic Center, which is part of Corbin Hospital.

Finally, on May 9, 2022 at 10:20:28 a.m. Eastern Time, Amy reported that the Louisville neuroSurgery appointment had been rescheduled for July 25 at 11:00 a.m. She noted that the clinic had called her back after she had left a message the previous week regarding her MRI appointments and their scheduling. The thread as a whole shows ongoing coordination about updated imaging per Dr. Nazar’s addendum, scheduling updates for MRI imaging (including three scans to be done together at Corbin Imaging and Diagnostic Center on May 26 at 1:30 p.m.), and the rescheduling of the Louisville neuroSurgery appointment to July 25 at 11:00 a.m.

item_608.note: Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2697]. Client: Amy Mills.

In the matter of a NeuroSurgery Appointment, correspondence between Amy Mills and Sarena M. Tuttle of The Whaley Law Firm occurred in April and May of 2022 regarding updated neurosurgical imaging as recommended by Dr. Gregory Nazar. On April 26, 2022, Sarena sent Amy an email instructing her to contact her neurosurgery provider to request updated imaging per Dr. Nazar’s addendum (which Sarena attached for the provider’s review). The message also included Sarena’s contact information and a note to reference her new email address, with the firm’s Louisville, Kentucky location and phone numbers.

Later on April 26, 2022, Amy replied that May 3rd at 5:30 p.m. would be her first available appointment, and she would send a follow-up email attached to let Sarena know when the MRI appointment set up by her neuro team would occur. She expressed thanks. Sarena responded to that update later that day with, “Sounds good, thank you,” and signed off with her firm’s details, including address, direct and office phone numbers, and her new email address.

On May 6, 2022, at 8:46 a.m., Amy wrote again, stating that she had attended her appointment with Teresa on Tuesday, and Teresa had ordered the necessary scans, but they had not yet been able to schedule her in; she noted that they were cutting the appointment with the neurosurgery doctor close and asked whether they should reschedule that appointment for not this coming week but the next, or something else.

Later that same day, at 9:14 a.m., Amy provided an update that she had just scheduled the MRI-related appointment for May 26, 2022, at 1:30 p.m. She explained that the imaging center is very backed up and that she would need three procedures conducted together, which would take longer; that timing reflects the earliest availability she could secure at the diagnostic center to obtain the MRI.

item_609.note: This note concerns the case for Amy Mills, identified as Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2699], with the client listed as Amy Mills.

On May 4, 2022, Biokinetics via FreshBooks sent an email to Terri Mitchell regarding Whaley Law Firm’s comment on invoice 0000088 for $550.00. The message conveyed that Whaley Law Firm had commented on the invoice and asked, “Is this not payable online?” It included options to “View Invoice and Reply” and a FreshBooks login, along with branding mentioning the FreshBooks service to create invoices, track expenses, and collaborate on projects.

The following day, May 5, 2022, at 19:19:43 +0000, Terri Mitchell ([EMAIL-2700]) replied to [EMAIL-2701] with the subject “Re: Whaley Law Firm commented on invoice 0000088 ($550.00).” She explained that their invoicing system had changed plans and removed that feature, and that they are working on finding a new system. She apologized for the inconvenience and asked that a check be mailed to 9900 Corporate Campus Dr, Suite 2400, Louisville, KY 40223. The message was signed, “Thanks, Terri Mitchell,” followed by “Commonwealth IME” and the website WWW.COMMONWEALTHIME.COM, and it included a cid image reference.

item_612.note: Case: Amy Mills – Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2708]. Client: Amy Mills.

Re: NeuroSurgery Appointment.

On April 26, 2022, at 3:07 PM, Sarena M. Tuttle of The Whaley Law Firm sent an email to Amy Mills regarding a neuro-surgery appointment. In her message, she thanked Amy for taking a couple of moments to speak with her and advised Amy to contact her neurosurgery provider and request updated imaging as recommended by Dr. Gregory Nazar. She attached a copy of Dr. Nazar’s addendum for Amy to provide to her neurosurgery physician for their review. The signature included the firm’s details: The Whaley Law Firm, 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222; Direct: [PHONE-2709]; Office: [PHONE-2710]; Email: Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com; with a note reading “PLEASE NOTE NEW EMAIL ADDRESS.”

Later, on the same day, at 3:16 PM, Amy Mills replied via email. Her message stated: “May 3rd at 5:30 a week from today - (this was her first available so I got this one) I will send you an email attached to this on as a reply to let you know when my MRI appointment she sets up will be. Thank You so much, Amy Mills.”
item_612.note_summary: Mills | Mills – Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2711]. | 2022-04-26

item_622.note: In the case of Amy Mills v. Forcht Bank, premised under Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2743], the client is Amy Mills. A mediation in this matter was scheduled to take place on July 14, 2022 at 1:30 p.m. via Zoom, with the Hon. Thomas J. Knopf presiding. The Notice of Mediation had been filed that day with the Knox County Circuit Court, and his office would provide the Zoom link and login information closer to the date.

On March 11, 2022, Sarena M. Tuttle of The Whaley Law Firm emailed Amy Mills to advise that the Notice of Mediation had been filed with the Knox County Circuit Court and that the mediation was set for July 14, 2022 at 1:30 p.m. via Zoom. She noted that the Hon. Thomas J. Knopf would preside and that his office would supply the Zoom link and login information closer to the mediation date. The message included her signature block for The Whaley Law Firm: 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222; direct line [PHONE-2744]; office line [PHONE-2745]; email Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com (PLEASE NOTE NEW EMAIL ADDRESS).

Earlier, the thread shows a message from Aaron Whaley dated March 25, 2022, addressed to Sarena. He asked whether all of Amy Mills’s updated medical records are on hand and stated that he wanted those records sent to Dr. Nazar and that Amy should be scheduled for a functional capacity exam as soon as possible. He added that they would need the results of that exam to use the economist.

For completeness, the signatures reflect the parties involved: Sarena M. Tuttle of The Whaley Law Firm with the Louisville address and contact details listed above, and Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm with the firm’s Louisville address and phone number (712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222; [PHONE-2746]).

item_627.note: The case file is labeled Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2774] for client Amy Mills. In March 2022, the Knox County Circuit Court clerk processed and accepted a filing in Mills v. Forcht Bank, Case No. 20-CI-00112, envelope number 4399402. The case caption is listed as MILLS, AMY VS. FORCHT BANK, and the record indicates this filing was processed on March 11, 2022, at 1:34 PM Eastern.

The electronic filing (eFiling) was submitted by Aaron Gregory Whaley, attorney for the plaintiff. The notice confirms that the case is before the Knox Circuit Court, Knox (Circuit). The envelope number associated with this filing is 4399402, and the notice was electronically mailed to the following recipients: Aaron Gregory Whaley ([EMAIL-2775]), Betsy R. Catron ([EMAIL-2776]), and Joshua Ellis ([EMAIL-2777]). There were no scheduled events listed for this filing.

The eFiling includes one document: NOTICE - OTHER. Additional details and access to the filing are provided via links: Dashboard receipt at https://kcoj.kycourts.net/efiling/Dashboard/Receipt?caseid=4399402 and a document retrieval page at https://kcoj.kycourts.net/eFilingRetrieval/Home/Package?id=8DA0E98D-DA05-4710-9521-8FB085556743. The notice states that it was automatically generated by the Kentucky Court of Justice eFiling system and instructs recipients not to reply to the email.

Kentucky Court of Justice Confidentiality Notice: This message and/or attachment is intended only for the addressee and may contain information that is privileged, confidential and/or proprietary work product. If you are not the intended recipient, or an authorized employee, agent or representative of the intended recipient, do not read, copy, retain or disseminate this message or any attachment. Do not forward this message and attachment without the express written consent of the sender. If you have received this message in error, please contact the sender immediately and delete all copies of the message and any attachment. Transmission or misdelivery shall not constitute waiver of any applicable legal privilege.

item_633.note: The case at hand is Amy Mills v. Forcht Bank, Knox Cir II, with mediation scheduling discussions spanning February and March 2022 between The Whaley Law Firm and the Vine Center, on behalf of Amy Mills. The participants in the emails were Sarena M. Tuttle of The Whaley Law Firm and Betsy R. Catron, Associate Attorney at Vine Center, with the matter noted as Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2795] and client Amy Mills. Sarena’s signature repeatedly notes the Whaley Firm’s Louisville office and includes a new email address, Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com, along with direct-dial and main phone numbers.

On February 23, 2022, Sarena opened the mediation scheduling discussion with Betsy Catron, asking whether the parties were agreeable to Hon. Thomas Knopf as the mediator and requesting available dates. She also asked Betsy to provide a selection of alternative mediators if Knopf was not agreeable. Sarena’s email included guidance about external emails and attached her firm’s contact information.

In a March 4, 2022 email, Sarena advised that she had attached the 2018 tax documents requested. She explained that Amy Mills did not file a tax return in 2020 because she did not work due to the accident and that Mills was having trouble locating 2016 tax documents; if Mills located the 2016 documents, they would be forwarded. She reiterated the request for consent to Judge Knopf as mediator and asked what dates were available, inviting Betsy to provide a selection of other mediators if Knopf was not agreeable. The message closed with the standard offer to discuss any questions further and repeated the contact details for Sarena and her new email address.

By March 10, 2022, Betsy replied that they were agreeable to Judge Knopf and asked whether a July mediation could be scheduled. The exchange continued with Sarena acknowledging the need to review the tax documents and confirming that July appeared to be a suitable month for mediation, noting that her office was fairly flexible that month and that they believed Judge Knopf was as well. She also asked whether Betsy had any specific dates in mind.

Later that same day, March 10, Sarena responded again, stating that July sounded great for mediation and that her team was open to scheduling within that month and believed Judge Knopf was as well. She asked Betsy to share any specific date preferences.

On March 11, Betsy replied that they were pretty open after July 11, with the exception of July 19. She asked for confirmation of availability and continued the dialogue about July mediation.

That same day, March 11, at 11:03 a.m., Sarena proposed July 14, 2022, as a target date, asking whether a half-day would be sufficient or if they would prefer a full day. She indicated a preference for the afternoon if a half-day schedule was acceptable.

At 11:52 a.m. on March 11, Betsy confirmed that they were good with July 14 and that a half-day would be fine, with the afternoon also acceptable.

Finally, at 12:04 p.m. on March 11, Sarena expressed appreciation and stated that she would get the mediation scheduled and noticed. She signed off with her contact information once again, reinforcing that the arrangement would be set and communicated to the parties involved.

In sum, the parties converged on Hon. Thomas Knopf as the mediator, settled on July 14, 2022 for a mediation session, and negotiated a preferred half-day timeframe in the afternoon, with both sides indicating flexibility around exact timing and confirming the plan to schedule and notice the mediation accordingly.

item_634.note: In the case of Amy Mills v Forcht Bank, Knox Circuit II, mediation was being scheduled in 2022. The client is Amy Mills. The Whaley Law Firm, represented by Sarena M. Tuttle, coordinated the mediation scheduling with Betsy R. Catron, Associate Attorney at Vine Center. The communication trail began with requests to confirm a mediator and to gather available dates, with Hon. Thomas J. Knopf proposed as the mediator and other mediators to be offered if Knopf was not agreeable. The parties discussed July as a potential mediation month, with a preference for afternoons and half-day sessions if possible. The email chain shows input from multiple participants and includes a note that the Notice of Mediation would be attached when finalized, and that the mediation would be conducted via Zoom, with Zoom login details to be provided by the National Arbitration and Dispute Association (NADA) closer to the date. The correspondence also included copies to NADA so that they could be cc’d on any mediation-related communications.

Chronologically, on February 23, 2022, Sarena Tuttle sent a message to Betsy Catron asking whether the office was agreeable to Hon. Thomas Knopf as the mediator and requesting available dates, with a suggestion to provide alternatives if Knopf was not agreeable. The message, which cautioned recipients about external emails, also indicated the correspondence would be forwarded to a wider audience, including Aaron Whaley and the Amy Mills filevine address, and noted that NADA would be cc’d on mediation communications. On March 4, 2022, Sarena followed up with attached 2018 Tax Documents; she explained that Amy Mills did not file a 2020 tax return because she did not work due to the accident and that she was having trouble locating 2016 tax documents; if the 2016 documents were located, they would be forwarded. She asked Betsy to indicate whether Hon. Thomas Knopf was agreeable as mediator and which dates were available, and if Knopf was not agreeable, to propose other mediators. This message also included the Whaley Firm’s contact details and stated that they looked forward to an amicable resolution.

On March 10, 2022, Betsy replied that she was agreeable to Judge Knopf and asked whether July mediation would work, noting they were pretty open and suggesting a half-day in the afternoon if possible. Later that same day, Sarena responded that July sounded great and asked Betsy to confirm whether a full day or half day would be needed, reiterating a preference for an afternoon half-day. By March 11, 2022, Betsy stated that they were good with July 14, 2022 and that a half-day would be fine, with the afternoon also acceptable. Sarena then confirmed that she would get the mediation scheduled and noticed.

Subsequently, on March 11, 2022 at 12:43 PM, Sarena sent a final, formal notice confirming the mediation details: Hon. Thomas J. Knopf would serve as mediator for Amy Mills v Forcht Bank, with the mediation set for July 14, 2022 at 1:30 PM via Zoom. The Notice of Mediation would be provided by the NADA, which would supply Zoom information closer to the mediation date. The Whaley Law Firm reported that the NADA had been provided Amy Mills’ contact information to cc them on any mediation correspondence. The notice was attached, and the email included Sarena’s updated contact details, including her new email address, along with the Whaley Law Firm’s address (712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222) and direct and office phone numbers (D: [PHONE-2797]; O: [PHONE-2798]). It also included her email address Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com.

For reference, Betsy Catron’s contact details were also present in the correspondence: Associate Attorney at Vine Center, 333 West Vine Street, Suite 1100, Lexington, KY 40507; P: [PHONE-2799]; F: [PHONE-2800]; website: www.whtlaw.com. The emails demonstrate a coordinated effort to schedule a July mediation session, confirm the mediator, address tax documentation issues, and arrange Zoom access through NADA, with all relevant parties kept informed and CC’d as appropriate.

item_635.note: This file, labeled Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2802], concerns client Amy Mills and relates to the civil action Amy Mills v Forcht Bank in Knox Circuit Court, Division II (Civil Action 20-CI-00112).

On March 11, 2022, at 12:20:30 PM Eastern Time, Sarena M. Tuttle of The Whaley Law Firm in Louisville sent an appointment request to Retired Judges Mediation and Arbitration Services regarding scheduling Hon. Thomas J. Knopf (Ret.) for the afternoon of Thursday, July 14, 2022. The location was to be by video conference. The request was sent on behalf of Sarena Tuttle, representing the plaintiff, Amy Mills, in the matter of Amy Mills v Forcht Bank (Knox County). The full case caption requested included the plaintiff and defendant, and the addresses and email addresses of counsel.

Counsel for the case were identified as follows: For the plaintiff, Aaron G. Whaley, Esq., The Whaley Law Firm, 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222; email [EMAIL-2803]; counsel for Plaintiff Amy Mills. A request was also made to cc the paralegal at Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com (noting this as a new email address). For the defendant, Betsy R. Catron, Esq., Ward, Hocker & Thornton, PLLC, 333 W. Vine Street, #1100, Lexington, KY 40507; email [EMAIL-2804]; counsel for Defendant Forcht Bank. Sarena M. Tuttle’s contact details were provided as The Whaley Law Firm, 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222, with direct and office phones D: [PHONE-2805]; O: [PHONE-2806], and email Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com (noting the new email address).

Earlier that same day, Debbie Shahayda, Administrator of Retired Judges Mediation and Arbitration Services, replied to the message. She stated that she had Hon. Knopf on the calendar for July 14 at 1:30 PM and requested that the sender provide the full case caption, including the names, addresses, and email addresses of counsel.

Separately, an automated message from the National Academy of Distinguished Neutrals (NADN) confirmed a new appointment request. The NADN email, sent to Debbie Shahayda, notified that there was an online request for Hon. Thomas J. Knopf (Ret.) for the afternoon of Thursday, July 14, 2022, with location listed as By Video Conference. The appointment request was submitted by Sarena Tuttle, on behalf of the attorney Sarena Tuttle, representing the plaintiff, The Whaley Law Firm, at 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222, telephone [PHONE-2807] and fax [PHONE-2808]. The case name listed was Amy Mills v Forcht Bank (Knox County). The NADN note also indicated that defense counsel/attendees were in agreement for the appointment. NADN Kentucky Chapter and NADN’s national site were provided, along with contact details for NADN support.

In summary, the file documents a mediation/arbitration appointment request for July 14, 2022, at 1:30 PM (afternoon) by video conference with Hon. Thomas J. Knopf (Ret.) for the case Amy Mills v Forcht Bank, Knox County, Civil Action 20-CI-00112, Knox Circuit Court – Division II. The request incorporates the full case caption details, counsel names, addresses, emails, and contact information for both plaintiff and defense counsel, and notes that defense counsel were in agreement for the appointment. It also records the communications from Debbie Shahayda requesting the full caption and the NADN’s automated confirmation of the appointment request.

item_641.note: Case: Amy Mills v. Forcht Bank, Knox Circuit II, Mediation Scheduling. Client: Amy Mills. The matter centers on arranging mediation for the above-referenced case, with the discussions spanning February and March 2022 between Sarena M. Tuttle of The Whaley Law Firm and Betsy R. Catron of WHT Law, regarding a mediator, dates, and logistical details.

The scheduling dialogue began with an email from Sarena Tuttle dated February 23, 2022. In that message, she thanked Mrs. Catron for the opportunity to schedule mediation and asked whether the office would be agreeable to Hon. Thomas Knopf as the mediator, and what dates were available. If Knopf were not agreeable, she requested a selection of alternative mediators. She noted that the client’s 2018 tax documents were attached and indicated that Amy Mills did not file a tax return in 2020 because she was not working due to the accident, and she was having trouble locating 2016 tax documents; if 2016 documents could be located, they would be forwarded upon receipt. She concluded by expressing a desire to work toward an amicable resolution and invited further questions. The email included contact details for Sarena M. Tuttle and carried a note about the new email address.

A follow-up email from Sarena Tuttle on March 4, 2022 reiterated the request to schedule mediation and confirmed the attachment of the 2018 Tax Documents. She again explained that Amy Mills did not file in 2020 due to not working after the accident and was having trouble locating 2016 documents, with a commitment to forward 2016 documents if located. Sarena asked whether the office was agreeable to Hon. Thomas Knopf as mediator and requested available dates; if Knopf was not agreeable, she asked for a selection of other mediators. She closed with a positive note about working toward an amicable resolution and encouraged direct contact for questions. This March 4 email was CC’d to Aaron Whaley and included the case file email address [EMAIL-2834].

On March 10, 2022, Betsy Catron replied that WHT Law was agreeable to Judge Knopf and asked whether July mediation could be attempted. Separately that same day, Sarena Tuttle acknowledged receipt of the tax documents, apologized for any confusion, and stated that she would review the file again. She indicated that July sounded great for mediation, that they were pretty open that month, and that she believed Judge Knopf was as well. She asked if there were any specific dates preferred.

Later that day, Betsy responded that they were pretty open, with availability anytime after July 11, and that July 19 should be avoided. On March 11, 2022, Sarena proposed July 14, 2022 as a great day and asked whether a full-day or half-day would be necessary, noting a preference for the afternoon if a half-day was acceptable.

Finally, on March 11, 2022 at 16:52:45, Betsy confirmed that July 14, 2022 could work and indicated that a half-day would be sufficient, with the afternoon also being acceptable. The correspondence closed with Betsy’s standard contact information and the firm’s branding.

In summary, the parties pursued mediation scheduling for Amy Mills v. Forcht Bank before Hon. Thomas Knopf, with July 14, 2022 identified as the preferred date and a preference for a half-day session in the afternoon, while remaining flexible about the exact duration. The exchange also documented the client’s tax document status (2018 provided; 2020 not filed due to lack of work; 2016 to be located if possible) and included the attachment of the 2018 tax documents in the March 4 communications.

item_643.note: Case: Amy Mills v. Forcht Bank – Knox Circuit II – Mediation Scheduling. Client: Amy Mills. This matter is referenced by the file as Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2838].

Over a chain of email communications between Betsy R. Catron, Associate Attorney at Whitten, Hocking & Thompson? Wait no: WHT Law (Vine Center, Lexington, KY) and Sarena M. Tuttle of The Whaley Law Firm (Louisville, KY), the lawyers discussed scheduling mediation for the case. The dates and key points are as follows.

On February 23, 2022, Sarena M. Tuttle sent an email to Betsy Catron requesting mediation in the above-referenced cases and asking if the parties were agreeable to Hon. Thomas Knopf as the mediator, along with available dates. If Hon. Knopf was not agreeable, she requested a selection of other mediators. The message noted that the 2018 tax documents were attached, and that their client stated she did not file a tax return in 2020 because she did not work due to the accident, with trouble locating 2016 tax documents. The email stated that if the 2016 documents were located, they would be forwarded upon receipt, and asked Betsy Catron to advise whether Hon. Thomas Knopf was acceptable and what dates were available. The message concluded with a note of willingness to work toward an amicable resolution for all parties involved and provided contact details for Sarena: The Whaley Law Firm, 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222; D: [PHONE-2839]; O: [PHONE-2840]; E: Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com; with a parenthetical note: PLEASE NOTE NEW EMAIL ADDRESS. The email chain also included external email caution banners: “External Email: Please use caution when opening attachments and links from external senders.”

On March 4, 2022, Sarena Tuttle again wrote Betsy Catron. She stated that 2018 tax documents were attached and explained that their client did not file a 2020 tax return due to not working because of the accident, and that locating 2016 tax documents was proving difficult; if the 2016 documents could be located, they would be forwarded upon receipt. In the meantime, Sarena asked Betsy to advise whether they were agreeable to Hon. Thomas Knopf as the mediator and to share available dates; if Hon. Knopf was not agreeable, to provide a selection of other mediators. The email closed with a forward-looking note about reaching an amicable resolution and an invitation to contact for further discussion. Sarena’s signature again included The Whaley Law Firm details and a reminder of the new email address (PLEASE NOTE NEW EMAIL ADDRESS).

Earlier still, on March 10, 2022 at 1:39 PM, Betsy Catron replied. She apologized for the delayed response and stated that WHT Law was agreeable to Judge Knopf as the mediator. She asked Sarena whether they could attempt a July mediation and thanked her.

Later that same day, March 10, 2022 at 2:40 PM, Sarena Tuttle responded. She acknowledged the tax document issue and said she would review the file again. In the meantime, she agreed that July sounded great for mediation, noting that their firm was pretty open that month and believed Judge Knopf was as well. She asked if Betsy had any preferred dates.

Finally, on March 11, 2022 at 3:33 PM UTC, Betsy Catron replied: “We are pretty open right now. Anytime after July 11 should be fine, except for July 19.” Her message closed with her standard signature block, including the Vine Center address at 333 West Vine Street, Suite 1100, Lexington, KY 40507, phone number [PHONE-2841], fax [PHONE-2842], and the website www.whtlaw.com.

Notes of the participants’ signatures show the following details. Betsy R. Catron was identified as an Associate Attorney with WHT Law, and her signature block lists the Vine Center address, her Lexington office contact information, and the firm’s website. Sarena M. Tuttle was identified with The Whaley Law Firm, and her signature block lists the address 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222, direct line [PHONE-2843], office line [PHONE-2844], and her email Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com, with a parenthetical note indicating the address is a new email address (PLEASE NOTE NEW EMAIL ADDRESS).

In sum, the email dialogue established mutual openness to mediation in July, with Hon. Thomas Knopf (Hon. Thomas Knopf/Judge Knopf) identified as the preferred mediator, and a scheduling window that becomes available after July 11, with the exception of July 19. The discussions included references to tax documents (2018 attached, 2020 non-filing due to not working from the accident, difficulty locating 2016) and a plan to forward missing documents if located.

item_645.note: Case: Amy Mills v. Forcht Bank, Knox Circuit II, Mediation Scheduling. The client is Amy Mills, in the matter identified as Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2850].

In the matter of mediation scheduling between The Whaley Law Firm and WHT Law, the initial exchanges occurred on February 23, 2022. Sarena M. Tuttle of The Whaley Law Firm emailed Betsy R. Catron of WHT Law to acknowledge the invitation to schedule mediation in the above-referenced case and to inquire whether both sides would be agreeable to Hon. Thomas Knopf as the mediator, and what dates would be available. If Hon. Knopf was not agreeable, she requested a selection of other mediators of WHT Law’s choosing. She concluded by expressing a desire to work toward an amicable resolution for all parties and invited any questions, providing contact details for further discussion. The Whaley Law Firm information listed included the firm’s Louisville, Kentucky office, and the standard contact details for Sarena Tuttle (712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222; D: [PHONE-2851]; O: [PHONE-2852]; E: Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com; and noting a new email address). There is also an external caution note about emails from external senders.

On March 4, 2022, Sarena Tuttle replied to Betsy Catron and attached the 2018 tax documents that had been requested. She explained that their client did not file a tax return in 2020 because she did not work due to the accident, and that the client was having trouble locating the 2016 tax documents; if the 2016 documents could be located, they would be forwarded upon receipt. In the same message, Sarena asked Betsy to confirm whether her office was agreeable to Hon. Thomas Knopf as the mediator and to provide dates when they were available. If Hon. Knopf was not agreeable, she requested that Betsy supply a selection of other mediators of WHT Law’s choosing. She reaffirmed their intention to work toward an amicable resolution for all parties and invited questions or discussion as needed. The email was signed by Sarena M. Tuttle of The Whaley Law Firm, with the firm’s Louisville address listed again, and included her direct line and general office line, and noted the new email address. The message was cc’d to Aaron Whaley and to [EMAIL-2853].

Earlier still, on February 23, 2022, Sarena Tuttle had written to Mrs. Catron with the same mediation scheduling request. She thanked Mrs. Catron for the opportunity to schedule mediation in the above-referenced cases and reiterated the request to know whether the parties were agreeable to Hon. Thomas Knopf as the mediator and to provide available dates; she also asked for alternative mediators if Knopf was not agreeable. She closed by expressing a commitment to an amicable resolution for all parties and inviting any questions, providing the same contact details and noting the newly provided email address. The message carried the Whaley Law Firm contact information: 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222; D: [PHONE-2854]; O: [PHONE-2855]; E: Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com (PLEASE NOTE NEW EMAIL ADDRESS). The note also carried an external email caution banner.

On March 10, 2022, Betsy Catron responded to Sarena, apologizing for the delayed reply and stating that WHT Law was agreeable to Judge Knopf as the mediator. Betsy asked Sarena whether a July mediation could be scheduled and closed with her thanks. Betsy’s signature identified her as Associate Attorney at WHT Law, with the firm’s Lexington, Kentucky address listed as Vine Center, 333 West Vine Street, Suite 1100, Lexington, KY 40507, and her contact details: P [PHONE-2856], F [PHONE-2857], and the firm’s website www.whtlaw.com. This message was CC’d to Aaron Whaley and [EMAIL-2858].

In summary, between February 23 and March 10, 2022, The Whaley Law Firm and WHT Law exchanged multiple emails to schedule mediation for Amy Mills v. Forcht Bank, Knox Circuit II, with a preference for Hon. Thomas (Thomas) Knopf as mediator, consideration of July as a mediation date, and with the client’s tax documents (2018 attached, and notes about 2016 and 2020 filings) discussed for production as needed. The communications included the parties’ official contact details and noted a new email address for Sarena Tuttle.

item_648.note: On February 23, 2022, at 2:55 PM, Sarena M. Tuttle of The Whaley Law Firm sent an email to Mrs. Catron regarding mediation scheduling in the case Amy Mills v. Forcht Bank – Knox Circle II. She thanked Mrs. Catron for the opportunity to schedule mediation and asked whether she and her office were agreeable to Hon. Thomas Knopf as the mediator, and what dates they were available. If Hon. Knopf was not agreeable, she requested a selection of other mediators from Mrs. Catron’s list. The message stated a desire to work toward an amicable resolution for all parties involved and invited any questions.

On March 4, 2022, at 1:32 PM, Sarena M. Tuttle sent a follow-up email with the same subject line. She attached the requested 2018 tax documents. She explained that their client, Amy Mills, did not file a 2020 tax return because she did not work due to the accident, and she was having trouble locating her 2016 tax documents; if she locates them, they will be forwarded upon receipt. In the meantime, the office again asked whether Mrs. Catron and her office were agreeable to Hon. Thomas Knopf as the mediator and what dates they were available; if Knopf is not agreeable, they requested a selection of other mediators of Mrs. Catron’s choosing. The message closed with the firm looking forward to an amicable resolution for all parties involved and inviting any questions. The signature block identified Sarena M. Tuttle of The Whaley Law Firm, 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222, with direct line [PHONE-2868], office line [PHONE-2869], and email Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com (PLEASE NOTE NEW EMAIL ADDRESS).

item_652.note: Case: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2881]. Client: Amy Mills. This note documents an email verification exchange involving Amy Mills and The Whaley Law Firm, with no one cc’d on the thread. The email thread is titled “Re: Email verification” and begins with a message from Amy Mills to Sarena Tuttle on March 3, 2022 at 16:32:08 -0500. In her message, Amy writes: “This is Kahlilys notes after the lat appointment but I don’t agree with the findings and the radiologist report for the scan doesn’t agree with it either as we have discussed.”

On March 3, 2022 at 9:55 AM, Sarena M. Tuttle of The Whaley Law Firm (712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222) replied: “Currently we are awaiting additional medical records and the tax information you found. The last we spoke you were able to locate additional tax information but I have not received it as of yet.” Her message includes the contact details: D: [PHONE-2882], O: [PHONE-2883], E: Sarena@Whttps://www.google.com/maps/search/712MAIL ADDRESS).

Earlier in the thread, at 12:10 AM on March 3, 2022, Amy Mills had written: “Just wanted to touch base and see where we are and how things are going and progressing on. Thanks, Amy Mills.”

Prior messages in the thread include: on February 22, 2022 at 2:57 PM, Amy Mills wrote: “Got it thank you ??” and on February 22, 2022 at 12:03 PM, Sarena Tuttle wrote: “Received.” Sarena’s signature block appears in that message, listing: Sarena M. Tuttle, The Whaley Law Firm, 712 Lyndon Lane, Louisville, KY 40222; D: [PHONE-2884]; O: [PHONE-2885]; E: Sarena@WhaleyLawFirm.com (PLEASE NOTE NEW EMAIL ADDRESS).

Earlier still, on February 22, 2022 at 12:00 PM, Aaron Whaley wrote: “test,” followed by his signature: Aaron Whaley, Whaley Law Firm, 712 Lyndon Ln, Louisville, KY 40222; [PHONE-2886].

This narrative preserves the full content and sequence of the email verification correspondence as documented in the case file.

item_661.note: Case: Amy Mills – Mills v. Forcht Bank, Knox Circuit Court. File name: Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2927]. The client is Amy Mills.

On February 22, 2022, Betsy R. Catron, Associate Attorney at Whaley Harrison & Thorne (WHT Law), sent an email from [EMAIL-2928] to Aaron Whaley at [EMAIL-2929], with copies to Sarena Tuttle at [EMAIL-2930] and to the [EMAIL-2931]. The email concerns the Mills v. Forcht Bank case in Knox Circuit Court. 

In the body of the message, Betsy stated that the Court had entered their proposed Agreed Order extending all deadlines in the case, using a reference date of January 1, 2023. She noted that attached were records recently received from Norton Orthopedic. Based on prior discussions, she indicated it appears Amy Mills’s last treatment was December 16, 2021, and she asked Aaron to advise whether Amy continues any fall-related treatment.

Betsy also reminded Aaron that his client had previously indicated an intention to pursue a claim for impairment of her ability to earn money in the future, and that tax returns are required as discovery. The parties are missing tax returns for the years 2016, 2018, and 2020, and Betsy asked Aaron to provide those as soon as possible.

Finally, Betsy requested that once Aaron has had an opportunity to review the new deadlines, he should call her to share his thoughts on setting the case for mediation.

The message closed with Betsy’s signature: Betsy R. Catron, Associate Attorney, Whit—Whaley? (WHT) Law, Vine Center, 333 West Vine Street, Suite 1100, Lexington, KY 40507. Phone: [PHONE-2932]; Fax: [PHONE-2933]; Website: www.whtlaw.com.

item_669.note: Case: Amy Mills premises file dated [DOB-2956]. Client: Amy Mills. Re: FW: Mills v. Forcht Bank; Knox Circuit Court; Case No. 20-CI-00112. This record is drawn from an email thread in January 2022 among Amy Mills’ counsel and their colleagues, regarding the Mills v. Forcht Bank case in Knox Circuit Court, and specifically about drafting and filing a proposed order to extend deadlines, as well as updates on court scheduling.

The chain begins with an email exchange on January 11–13, 2022 among Aaron Whaley (Whaley Law Firm), Betsy Catron (Whitt, Catron & Henderson / WHTLaw), Renee Day (legal assistant to several colleagues), and others cc’d in the thread, including Sarena Tuttle and Amy Mills’ Filevine recipient. In one early exchange, Aaron Whaley wrote on January 11, 2022 at 4:23 PM to Betsy Catron and Renee Day (and cc’d others) that he was not lazy but would be willing to draft, and that she could bill that time. Betsy Catron replied on January 11 at 6:12 PM that “That is fine with me.” Later that same evening, at 10:03 PM, Whaley reiterated the sentiment about drafting.

Earlier that day, Renee Day sent a message (January 11 at 4:07 PM) to counsel attaching the Notice of Withdrawal of Motion to Compel Only that had been filed with the court in the above-referenced case, asking to be informed if there were questions or concerns. The thread shows Betsy Catron’s replying correspondence over the next days. On January 12, 2022 at 7:21 PM Betsy Catron wrote, “No problem. I’ll get it done tomorrow. Thanks!” and included her standard firm signature and contact information.

On January 11, 2022 at 4:23 PM, Aaron had also exchanged messages with Renee Day and others about the approach to filing, specifically suggesting the possibility of a “Proposed Agreed Order” accompanied by a Motion to Adopt, and outlining grounds such as the Plaintiff having recently been released from active treatment and the need for more time to retrieve records, consult experts, and mediate. He noted that while the Court would not extend deadlines by agreed order, they could pursue such a filing with the appropriate grounds.

On January 13, 2022, Betsy Catron indicated progress by writing at 2:48 PM: “Hi Aaron – please see attached. Let me know if you approve to file.” Aaron Whaley responded at 2:54 PM: “Looks good, Note my new email address. Thanks.” The thread shows that Aaron’s new email address was [EMAIL-2957], and Betsy acknowledged approval to file the attached materials. The email traffic on January 11–13 also includes Betsy’s prior communications in which she indicated she would handle the filing the following day, with Aaron later confirming his new contact address.

Over the ensuing days, the thread includes Renee Day’s communications and standard firm email footers, including the confidentiality notice and multiple cautionary notes regarding external emails. The thread also shows the firm’s ongoing exchange about drafting and filing the necessary documents to extend deadlines and adopt an agreed order.

On January 19, 2022, Betsy Catron sent an email noting a development from the Knox Circuit Court Clerk’s office: “Judge Caperton’s Knox Circuit Court has been cancelled for tomorrow 1/20/22. All cases will be rescheduled for 2/14/22 at the regular time. If you have any questions, please call the clerk’s office at 546 3075.” Aaron Whaley replied with a brief, handwritten sentiment: “thank god.” Betsy then circulated the update to the chain, and the matter remained pending in light of the rescheduling.

In sum, the January 2022 correspondence reflects a coordinated effort among Amy Mills’ counsel and associated staff to draft and file a proposed agreed order to extend deadlines in Mills v. Forcht Bank (Knox Circuit Court, Case No. 20-CI-00112), with discussions about extending deadlines by six months to complete record retrieval, consult experts, and mediate, alongside the procedural steps already taken (including the withdrawal of a prior motion to compel), and an interim court scheduling change due to cancellation of a hearing scheduled for January 20, 2022, with the next date set for February 14, 2022 at the regular time. The thread includes references to the parties’ drafting and filing process, the new contact information for Aaron Whaley, and the various cc’d participants and assistants, as well as the standard firm email disclosures and external email cautions.

item_670.note: This note documents an email thread about the Mills v. Forcht Bank matter in Knox Circuit Court, Case No. 20-CI-00112, on behalf of client Amy Mills. The involved parties were Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm and Betsy R. Catron of Vine Center/WHT Law, with Sarena Tuttle and the Amy Mills Filevine alias copied on various messages.

On January 19, 2022, Aaron Whaley sent the latest message in the thread to Betsy Catron, with Sarena Tuttle and Amy Mills’ Filevine address CC’d, and the subject line referencing Mills v. Forcht Bank and the Knox Circuit Court case. In his email, Whaley wrote a single line: “thank god.” This followed Betsy Catron’s earlier note about the Knox Circuit Court scheduling issue (described below).

Earlier in the week, Betsy Catron had written on January 19, 2022 at 3:01 PM that she had just seen a posting on the Knox Circuit Court Clerk’s Facebook page informing that Judge Caperton’s Knox Circuit Court had been cancelled for January 20, 2022. All cases would be rescheduled for February 14, 2022 at the regular time, and she provided the clerk’s office phone number (546 3075) for questions. She signed as Betsy R. Catron, Associate Attorney at Vine Center, with the firm’s Lexington, Kentucky contact details.

The thread shows a longer back-and-forth spanning January 11–13, 2022, concerning filing documents and drafting. On January 13, 2022 at 2:54 PM, Aaron noted his new email address and said, “Looks good, Note my new email address. Thanks.” Earlier that same day, at 2:48 PM, Betsy had written, “Hi Aaron – please see attached. Let me know if you approve to file.” The previous day, January 12, 2022 at 7:21 PM, Betsy had responded, “No problem. I’ll get it done tomorrow. Thanks!” On January 11, 2022 at 10:03 PM, Aaron had quipped, “I am not lazy but care to draft? You can bill that. ????,” to which Betsy had replied earlier that day, “That is fine with me.” Earlier still, at 4:23 PM on January 11, 2022, Renee Day had forwarded to counsel the attached Notice of Withdrawal of Motion to Compel Only that had been filed with the court that afternoon, inviting any questions or concerns. Renee Day signed as Legal Assistant to Jillian House, Betsy Catron, Catie Coldiron Joseph, and Kaylee Collins, at the Vine Center, with the firm’s standard disclaimer at the bottom of the message.

In the sequence of these communications, Betsy later confirmed, “No problem. I’ll get it done tomorrow. Thanks!” and the correspondence continued toward filing an item described as a “Proposed Agreed Order” and a “Motion to adopt.” Aaron had suggested drafting such an Agreed Order, with grounds stating that the plaintiff had only recently been released from active treatment and that extending deadlines by another six months would allow counsel to complete record retrieval, consult experts, and mediate the case. The exchange noted that the court would not permit extending the deadline via an agreed order, and they discussed filing the Proposed Agreed Order along with the Motion to adopt.

The sequence also includes a formal attachment in Renee Day’s January 11, 2022 email, which is the Notice of Withdrawal of Motion to Compel Only, filed with the court in the above-referenced case. The thread closes with the standard firm email signatures and confidentiality notices. The final details show Aaron Whaley at Whaley Law Firm in Louisville, Kentucky, with his contact information provided for further correspondence.

item_671.note: On January 13, 2022 at 3:10 PM Eastern, the Kentucky Court of Justice eFiling system generated a Notice of Electronic Filing for the Knox Circuit Court case MILLS, AMY VS. FORCHT BANK, Case Number 20-CI-00112. The associated envelope number is 4214056. The eFiler for this filing was Betsy R. Catron, Attorney for Defendant. The court involved is Knox (Circuit).

The notice was electronically mailed to three recipients: Aaron Gregory Whaley at [EMAIL-2961]; Betsy R. Catron at [EMAIL-2962]; and Joshua Ellis at [EMAIL-2963]. The documents included in this eFiling are: Motion for Extension of Time; Motion to Adopt Proposed Agreed Order; and the Tendered Document: Agreed Order. The documents may be viewed at https://kcoj.kycourts.net/eFilingRetrieval/Home/Package?id=56F96B74-10F5-421F-AD9B-EEEE415B78FE.

This notice was automatically generated by the Kentucky Court of Justice eFiling system. Do not reply to this email.

Kentucky Court of Justice Confidentiality Notice

This message and/or attachment is intended only for the addressee and may contain information that is privileged, confidential and/or proprietary work product. If you are not the intended recipient, or an authorized employee, agent or representative of the intended recipient, do not read, copy, retain or disseminate this message or any attachment. Do not forward this message and attachment without the express written consent of the sender. If you have received this message in error, please contact the sender immediately and delete all copies of the message and any attachment. Transmission or misdelivery shall not constitute waiver of any applicable legal privilege.

item_672.note: Case: Amy Mills – Premise file dated [DOB-2965]. Client: Amy Mills. Re: Mills v. Forcht Bank; Knox Circuit Court; Case No.: 20-CI-00112.

This note records a sequence of email exchanges in January 2022 among counsel and staff regarding the Knox Circuit Court case Mills v. Forcht Bank. The participants include Aaron Whaley of Whaley Law Firm ([EMAIL-2966]), Betsy R. Catron, Associate Attorney at WHT Law (Vine Center, 333 West Vine Street, Lexington, KY 40507), Renee Day (Legal Assistant to Betsy Catron and colleagues), and others CC’d on the thread, including Sarena Tuttle and the Amy Mills Premise email alias. Aaron also notes his new email address in the thread. The subject line repeatedly refers to Mills v. Forcht Bank, Knox Circuit Court, Case No. 20-CI-00112.

On January 11, 2022, at 4:07 PM, Renee Day sent an email to counsel attaching the Notice of Withdrawal of Motion to Compel Only that had been filed with the court that afternoon in the above-referenced case, asking to (please) let them know if there were questions or concerns. Later that same day, at 4:23 PM, Aaron Whaley replied to Betsy Catron (and cc’d Renee Day and [EMAIL-2967]), joking, “I am not lazy but care to draft? You can bill that.” On January 11 at 6:12 PM, Betsy Catron responded, “That is fine with me.”

At 10:03 PM on January 11, 2022, Aaron Whaley sent another message to Betsy Catron (cc’ing Renee Day and [EMAIL-2968]) noting: “Received, thank you. I know the Court won't let us extend the deadline by agreed order but you want to file something like a ‘Proposed Agreed Order’? It is just an Agreed Order along with a Motion to adopt. As grounds we can say that Plaintiff has only recently been released from active treatment and extending the deadlines another 6 months will allow counsel to complete record retrieval, consult experts and mediate the case. Something like that.” 

Earlier that same day, January 11, at 4:23 PM, Renee Day had been corresponding about the matter, and at 4:07 PM she had advised that the Notice of Withdrawal of Motion to Compel Only had been filed with the court earlier that afternoon. Betsy Catron replied later that day (at 6:12 PM) that this approach was acceptable.

The following day, January 12, 2022, at 7:21 PM Betsy Catron wrote: “No problem. I’ll get it done tomorrow. Thanks!” and signed her message as Associate Attorney from Vine Center.

On January 13, 2022, at 2:48 PM Betsy Catron sent, “Hi Aaron – please see attached. Let me know if you approve to file.” In that message she again referenced the attachment and sought approval to file the proposed course of action. Aaron Whaley responded about five minutes later (at 2:53:30 PM) with, “Looks good, Note my new email address. Thanks.”

Throughout the thread, standard firm contact details and disclosures appeared in signatures, including the Vine Center address and phone numbers, and the typical confidentiality notice was included in the messages from Renee Day. The thread confirms the progression toward filing an agreed order and a motion to adopt, with the goal of extending deadlines to permit record retrieval, expert consultation, and mediation, in connection with Mills v. Forcht Bank, Knox Circuit Court, Case No. 20-CI-00112.

item_675.note: This case note concerns Amy Mills in the matter titled Amy Mills v. Forcht Bank, file reference Amy-Mills-Premise-[DOB-2974], with the client listed as Amy Mills. On Thursday, January 13, 2022, at 20:14:12 UTC, Deborah Jackson, the legal assistant to George B. Hocker, Ashley K. Brown, Maxwell D. Smith, William J. Barker II, and Jared M. Hudson, sent an email regarding the above-referenced matter. The message was sent from [EMAIL-2975] to [EMAIL-2976] and [EMAIL-2977], with Betsy Catron at [EMAIL-2978] CC’d. The subject line stated that a Joint Motion had been filed in the matter.

In the body of the email, Deborah Jackson wrote: “Good afternoon, Please see attached Joint Motion filed in the above-referenced matter. Thanks and have a great day!” The email then closed with “Thanks, Deborah Jackson” and identified her as the Legal Assistant to George B. Hocker, Ashley K. Brown, Maxwell D. Smith, William J. Barker, II and Jared M. Hudson.

The email included [WHT_EmailLogo] and the firm’s information, identifying Vine Center at 333 West Vine Street, Suite 1100, Lexington, KY 40507, with a phone number of [PHONE-2979], a fax number of [PHONE-2980], and the website www.whtlaw.com.

Additionally, the message contained a confidentiality notice: “Information contained in this email transmission is privileged and confidential. If you are not the intended recipient, do not read, distribute or reproduce this transmission (including any attachments). If you have received this email in error, please delete it from your system without copying it, and notify the sender by reply email, so that our records can be corrected. Thank you.”

item_676.note: Case progression update for Mills v. Forcht Bank, Knox Circuit Court Case No. 20-CI-00112. This note documents significant case activity from November 2025 through February 2026.

On October 29, 2025 at 12:30 PM, a site inspection of the Forcht Bank premises was conducted with consulting expert David Johnson. The inspection was attended by Aaron Whaley and representatives from the defense.

In November 2025, medical records for Amy Mills were subpoenaed and obtained. The records were subsequently provided to defense counsel via Dropbox link in January 2026.

On February 4, 2026, supplemental discovery responses were prepared and transmitted to defense counsel. These responses included updated information regarding Amy Mills' medical treatment and expert disclosures. Aaron Whaley coordinated with Adam Redden on the preparation and filing of these responses.

Throughout late January and early February 2026, correspondence between Aaron Whaley, Adam Redden (associate attorney handling day-to-day case management), and Betsy Catron (defense counsel) focused on scheduling mediation. Dates discussed included March 30-31, 2026. Defense counsel requested Amy Mills' availability for mediation scheduling.

On February 13, 2026, a phone conference was scheduled between Adam Redden and Betsy Catron at 11:30 AM (rescheduled from 9:30 AM) to discuss case status, mediation scheduling, and next steps.

Expert consultant David Johnson has been formally retained to provide consulting services regarding premises liability issues. His inspection findings from the October 29, 2025 site visit are being incorporated into case strategy.

The case remains in active litigation with discovery substantially complete. The next major milestone is scheduling and conducting mediation, anticipated for late March 2026.
item_676.note_summary: Mills | Case progression Nov 2025 - Feb 2026... | 2026-02-13
